problem_statement.Title: Inconsistent permissions for uploaded files when FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS is left as None (TemporaryUploadedFile vs MemoryUploadedFile)
problem_statement.Description: Files uploaded to FileSystemStorage can end up with different filesystem permission bits depending on the upload handler used (temporary-file + rename vs streaming write). With the global default FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS = None, FileSystemStorage skips a final chmod, so files moved from temporary files inherit the tempfile's restrictive mode (commonly 0o600) while streaming-write files use mode 0o666 masked by the process umask (commonly 0o644).
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) settings default: django/conf/global_settings.py sets FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS = None, encoding the assumption "do not enforce a default permission" for uploaded files. (location: django/conf/global_settings.py)

2) conditional chmod in storage: FileSystemStorage._save only calls os.chmod(full_path, self.file_permissions_mode) when self.file_permissions_mode is not None. file_permissions_mode returns settings.FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS when instance value is unset, i.e. file_permissions_mode == settings.FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS. Because the global default is None, the chmod step is skipped. (location: django/core/files/storage.py, method: FileSystemStorage._save and property file_permissions_mode)

3) restrictive tempfile mode preserved by move: TemporaryUploadedFile uses NamedTemporaryFile (django/core/files/temp.py → tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile) which on Unix often creates temp files with mode 0o600; django/core/files/move.py::file_move_safe prefers os.rename (which preserves source permission bits) and its fallback copystat also preserves mode. The combination causes destination files to inherit the tempfile's restrictive 0o600 unless a chmod is applied afterwards. (locations: django/core/files/uploadedfile.py, django/core/files/temp.py, django/core/files/move.py)

4) test expectation encodes old default: tests/test_utils/tests.py::test_override_file_upload_permissions asserts default_storage.file_permissions_mode is None, reflecting the current global default. Changing the global default will make that test fail unless updated. (location: tests/test_utils/tests.py)

5) investigation gap: the analysis did not include platform/runtime validation (Windows behavior, specific filesystems, umask interactions at runtime) nor execution of the test suite; therefore platform-specific nuances and third-party storage backend interactions remain unvalidated. (checkpoint: investigation gap)
StepsToReproduce: Use a Django project with default settings (no FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS set).
Configure default file storage to FileSystemStorage and ensure MEDIA_ROOT points to a filesystem you can inspect.
Start the dev server or a test view that accepts file uploads.
Upload a small file (so MemoryUploadedFile path / streaming-write is used). After upload, inspect the saved file permissions (e.g., ls -l). Record the mode (typically 0o644 when umask masks 0o666).

Upload a larger file (so TemporaryUploadedFile + NamedTemporaryFile is used, leading to file_move_safe os.rename). After upload, inspect the saved file permissions. Observe that the mode is often 0o600.
Compare the two results: note the inconsistent permissions between small and large uploads.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Uploaded files should have consistent, predictable permissions independent of which upload handler (in-memory vs temporary-file + rename) was used. When no explicit FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS is provided, there should be a stable default final permission for saved files (user-visible example: 0o644).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: With the default FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS = None, files uploaded via TemporaryUploadedFile + os.rename can retain the tempfile's restrictive mode (commonly 0o600), while files saved via the streaming-write path end up with mode 0o666 masked by umask (commonly 0o644). This leads to inconsistent file modes depending on upload size/handler.

hints_text:
I think you're talking about ef70af77ec53160d5ffa060c1bdf5ed93322d84f (#28540). I guess the question is whether or not that documentation should be duplicated elsewhere.
Thank you Tim, this is precisely what I was looking for! I can only see one issue with the current docs (if you excuse me for bothering you with such minor details). ​The documentation for the FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS setting reads: If this isn’t given or is None, you’ll get operating-system dependent behavior. On most platforms, temporary files will have a mode of 0o600, and files saved from memory will be saved using the system’s standard umask. As I would understand this text, only temporary files get a mode of 0o600. I would then ask myself: "Why should I care about temporary files, they should be gone anyway after the file is uploaded?" and skip setting FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS. What is important but is not properly conveyed to the user is that not only temporary files themselves, but also the actual files which end up in the media folder get permissions of 0o600. Currently a developer can only discover this either by careful reading of the Deployment checklist page (manage.py check --deploy does not seem to check FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS) or by hitting the inconsistent permissions accidentally (like I did). I propose to unify the docs for FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS on the Settings page and the Deployment checklist page like this: ​https://gist.github.com/earshinov/0340f741189a14d4fd10e3e902203ad6/revisions#diff-14151589d5408f8b64b7e0e580770f0e Pros: It makes more clear that one gets different permissions for the *uploaded* files. It makes the docs more unified and thus easier to synchronously change in the future if/when required. I recognize that my edits might seem too minor and insignificant to be worth the hassle of editing the docs, committing, re-publishing them etc., but still I hope you will find them useful enough to be integrated into the official docs.
Now that I think about, maybe Django could provide # <Commentary about inconsistent permissions when this setting is omitted> FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSINS=0o600 in the ​default project settings so that developers don't miss it? 600 seems a reasonable default, particularly because people would get 600 anyway (at least on some operating systems) when the TemporaryFileUploadHandler is engaged.
Since this has come up again, I've suggested on django-developers (​https://groups.google.com/d/topic/django-developers/h9XbQAPv5-I/discussion) that we adjust the FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSION default to 0o644 (This was the conclusion I eventually came to from the discussion on #28540.) Lets see what people say there.
Thus far, no great objections on the mailing list to adjusting the FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSION default. Thus I'm going to rename this and Accept on that basis. A PR would need to: Adjust the default. Add a Breaking Change note to releases/2.2.txt (on the assumption we can get it in for then.) — This should include a set to None to restore previous behaviour' type comment. Adjust the references in the settings docs and deployment checklist. Make sure any other references are adjusted.
Replying to Carlton Gibson: Thus far, no great objections on the mailing list to adjusting the FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSION default. Thus I'm going to rename this and Accept on that basis. Thank you! Hopefully, this change will prevent confusion and unpleasant surprises for Django users in the future.
Hello everyone, I would like to work on this. But before that there are few important questions: There is a related setting called FILE_UPLOAD_DIRECTORY_PERMISSIONS. Its document says that This value mirrors the functionality and caveats of the FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS setting. Shall we also change its default from None to 0o644(Please suggest if something different should be provided for directories) and update its document as well? Since 2.2 pre-release branch is now in feature freeze state, Shall we move the change to 3.0 version? On a side note, some tests must be refactored for new values for both of these settings. I think that's alright.
That note is referring to that non-leaf directories are created using the process umask. (See ​`makedirs()` docs.) This is similar to FILE_UPLOAD_PERMISSIONS, when not using the temporary file upload handler. The underlying issue here is the inconsistency in file permissions, depending on the file size, when using the default settings that Django provides. There is no such inconsistency with directory permissions. As such changes should not be needed to FILE_UPLOAD_DIRECTORY_PERMISSIONS. (Any issues there would need to be addressed under a separate ticket.)
Replying to Carlton Gibson: I see and understand the issue better now. Thanks for the clarification. I'll make the changes as you have suggested in your previous comment. Only question remaining is about introducing this change in 3.0 version. Shall we move it to 3.0 release?
Shall we move it to 3.0 release? Yes please.

diff --git a/tests/model_fields/test_filepathfield.py b/tests/model_fields/test_filepathfield.py
new file mode 100644

@@ -0,0 +1,22 @@
+import os
+
+from django.db.models import FilePathField
+from django.test import SimpleTestCase
+
+
+class FilePathFieldTests(SimpleTestCase):
+    def test_path(self):
+        path = os.path.dirname(__file__)
+        field = FilePathField(path=path)
+        self.assertEqual(field.path, path)
+        self.assertEqual(field.formfield().path, path)
+
+    def test_callable_path(self):
+        path = os.path.dirname(__file__)
+
+        def generate_path():
+            return path
+
+        field = FilePathField(path=generate_path)
+        self.assertEqual(field.path(), path)
+        self.assertEqual(field.formfield().path, path)

problem_statement.Title: FilePathField.path is evaluated immediately; makemigrations embeds machine-specific resolved paths (callables not honored)
problem_statement.Description: When a FilePathField is defined using an expression like os.path.join(settings.LOCAL_FILE_DIR, 'example_dir') at module import time, manage.py makemigrations resolves that path on the current machine and the resolved absolute path is written into the migration. FilePathField does not accept a callable for path in practice because django/forms/fields.FilePathField.__init__ treats path as a concrete string and performs filesystem inspection immediately.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Incorrect assumption: django/forms/fields.py:FilePathField.__init__ assumes path is always a concrete string available at init/import time. Location: django/forms/fields.py, FilePathField.__init__. Evidence: uses self.path directly in filesystem operations (os.walk(self.path), os.scandir(self.path)) and in display computation (f.replace(path, "", 1)). This forces immediate evaluation and machine-specific values to be observed/written.

2. Incorrect/fragile serialization interaction: django/db/models/fields/__init__.py:FilePathField.deconstruct records kwargs['path'] = self.path. Location: django/db/models/fields/__init__.py, FilePathField.deconstruct. Evidence: deconstruct preserves the original object passed (string or callable) into migration kwargs, so migrations will capture whatever was passed; combined with (1) this produces migrations that contain resolved strings when callers pass expressions evaluated at import time.

3. Missing callable-handling pattern on form side: The established pattern used by FileField (django/db/models/fields/files.py) shows the intended relationship: store the callable in the field and call it at use-time. Evidence: FileField uses "if callable(self.upload_to): filename = self.upload_to(instance, filename)" and deconstructs upload_to into kwargs. FilePathField.__init__ lacks the analogous runtime callable resolution.

4. Required relationship/formula for correct behavior: when initializing the form field, compute resolved_path as: resolved_path = self.path() if callable(self.path) else self.path. Use resolved_path for filesystem inspection calls (os.walk(resolved_path), os.scandir(resolved_path)) and for display string adjustments (f.replace(resolved_path, "", 1)). This preserves models.FilePathField.deconstruct behavior while avoiding embedding machine-specific resolved paths during migration generation.

5. Test/serialization gap: tests/forms_tests/field_tests/test_filepathfield.py only covers string path usage and does not exercise path being a callable or verify that using a top-level callable produces an importable migration reference. Migration serialization also requires that any callable be a top-level importable object; this constraint is not covered by current tests.
StepsToReproduce:
Define a model that uses FilePathField with a module-level expression that resolves to a filesystem path, e.g.:
  file = models.FilePathField(path=os.path.join(settings.LOCAL_FILE_DIR, 'example_dir'))
Run python manage.py makemigrations on machine A.
Open the generated migration file and inspect the FilePathField kwargs; observe an absolute filesystem path resolved for the path argument (e.g. /home/<username>/server_files/example_dir).
Attempt to run the migration on machine B (where the original resolved path does not exist) or inspect that the migration no longer contains the original os.path.join expression.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: If a callable (or an expression intended to be serialized as a callable) is provided as FilePathField.path, migrations should preserve a serializable callable reference where possible and not bake a machine-specific resolved absolute path into the migration. Form/administration code should generate choices using the path resolved at runtime on the environment where the form is initialized, not at migration generation/import time. The behavior should match the established callable pattern used by FileField: store the callable for serialization and call it when the filesystem must be inspected.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: manage.py makemigrations resolves module-level path expressions and writes the resolved absolute path into the migration file. The migration contains a machine-specific path (e.g. /home/<username>/server_files/example_dir) instead of a callable or portable expression, causing portability/breakage across different machines/environments. The developer had to manually edit the migration to restore an os.path.join expression.

hints_text:
So, to clarify, what exactly is the bug/feature proposal/issue here? The way I see it, you're supposed to use os.path.join() and LOCAL_FILE_DIR to define a relative path. It's sort of like how we use BASE_DIR to define relative paths in a lot of other places. Could you please clarify a bit more as to what the issue is so as to make it easier to test and patch?
Replying to Hemanth V. Alluri: So, to clarify, what exactly is the bug/feature proposal/issue here? The way I see it, you're supposed to use os.path.join() and LOCAL_FILE_DIR to define a relative path. It's sort of like how we use BASE_DIR to define relative paths in a lot of other places. Could you please clarify a bit more as to what the issue is so as to make it easier to test and patch? LOCAL_FILE_DIR doesn't have to be the same on another machine, and in this case it isn't the same on the production server. So the os.path.join() will generate a different path on my local machine compared to the server. When i ran ./manage.py makemigrations the Migration had the path resolved "hardcoded" to my local path, which will not work when applying that path on the production server. This will also happen when using the BASE_DIR setting as the path of your FilePathField, seeing as that's based on the location of your project folder, which will almost always be on a different location. My suggestion would be to let makemigrations not resolve the path and instead keep the os.path.join(), which I have now done manually. More importantly would be to retain the LOCAL_FILE_DIR setting in the migration.
Replying to Sebastiaan Arendsen: Replying to Hemanth V. Alluri: So, to clarify, what exactly is the bug/feature proposal/issue here? The way I see it, you're supposed to use os.path.join() and LOCAL_FILE_DIR to define a relative path. It's sort of like how we use BASE_DIR to define relative paths in a lot of other places. Could you please clarify a bit more as to what the issue is so as to make it easier to test and patch? LOCAL_FILE_DIR doesn't have to be the same on another machine, and in this case it isn't the same on the production server. So the os.path.join() will generate a different path on my local machine compared to the server. When i ran ./manage.py makemigrations the Migration had the path resolved "hardcoded" to my local path, which will not work when applying that path on the production server. This will also happen when using the BASE_DIR setting as the path of your FilePathField, seeing as that's based on the location of your project folder, which will almost always be on a different location. My suggestion would be to let makemigrations not resolve the path and instead keep the os.path.join(), which I have now done manually. More importantly would be to retain the LOCAL_FILE_DIR setting in the migration. Please look at this ticket: https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/6896 I think that something like what sandychapman suggested about an extra flag would be cool if the design decision was approved and if there were no restrictions in the implementation for such a change to be made. But that's up to the developers who have had more experience with the project to decide, not me.
This seems a reasonable use-case: allow FilePathField to vary path by environment. The trouble with os.path.join(...) is that it will always be interpreted at import time, when the class definition is loaded. (The (...) say, ...and call this....) The way to defer that would be to all path to accept a callable, similarly to how FileField's upload_to takes a callable. It should be enough to evaluate the callable in FilePathField.__init__(). Experimenting with generating a migration looks good. (The operation gives path the fully qualified import path of the specified callable, just as with upload_to.) I'm going to tentatively mark this as Easy Pickings: it should be simple enough.
Replying to Nicolas Noé: Hi Nicolas, Are you still working on this ticket?
Sorry, I forgot about it. I'll try to solve this real soon (or release the ticket if I can't find time for it).
​PR
Can I work on this ticket ?
Sure, sorry for blocking the ticket while I was too busy...
I think that Nicolas Noe's solution, ​PR, was correct. The model field can accept a callable as it is currently implemented. If you pass it a callable for the path argument it will correctly use that fully qualified function import path in the migration. The problem is when you go to actually instantiate a FilePathField instance, the FilePathField form does some type checking and gives you one of these TypeError: scandir: path should be string, bytes, os.PathLike or None, not function This can be avoided by evaluating the path function first thing in the field form __init__ function, as in the pull request. Then everything seems to work fine.
Hi, If I only change self.path in forms/fields.py, right after __init__ I get this error: File "/home/hpfn/Documentos/Programacao/python/testes/.venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/forms/fields.py", line 1106, in __init__ self.choices.append((f, f.replace(path, "", 1))) TypeError: replace() argument 1 must be str, not function The 'path' param is used a few lines after. There is one more time. Line 1106 can be wrong. If I put in models/fields/__init__.py - after super(): if callable(self.path): self.path = self.path() I can run 'python manage.py runserver'
It can be: if callable(path): path = path() at the beginning of forms/fields.py
​PR
All comments in the original PR (​https://github.com/django/django/pull/10299/commits/7ddb83ca7ed5b2a586e9d4c9e0a79d60b27c26b6) seems to be resolved in the latter one (​https://github.com/django/django/pull/10924/commits/9c3b2c85e46efcf1c916e4b76045d834f16050e3).

problem_statement.Title: SQLCompiler incorrectly removes ORDER BY clauses when expressions are multiline RawSQL
problem_statement.Description: When ORDER BY expressions are provided as multiline RawSQL, SQLCompiler's deduplication logic incorrectly identifies distinct expressions as duplicates and removes one. The mis-identification occurs during extraction of the expression portion before 'ASC'/'DESC' and manifests when the compiled SQL contains newline characters.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Regex assumes expression is single-line — django/db/models/sql/compiler.py::SQLCompiler.__init__ sets self.ordering_parts = re.compile(r'(.*)\s(ASC|DESC)(.*)') which assumes '.' will match the whole expression up to 'ASC'/'DESC'. Dot does not match newlines, so multiline expressions are truncated. 

2) Extraction uses truncated group — django/db/models/sql/compiler.py::SQLCompiler.get_order_by calls without_ordering = self.ordering_parts.search(sql).group(1) and uses that value as the deduplication key. Because group(1) is truncated for multiline SQL, different expressions that share the same trailing line compare equal. 

3) Extra-select path repeats same incorrect assumption — django/db/models/sql/compiler.py::SQLCompiler.get_extra_select also calls ordering_parts.search(sql).group(1) and compares (without_ordering, params) against select entries without normalizing the select-side SQL; both call sites must be consistent. 

4) RawSQL preserves newlines — django/db/models/expressions.py::RawSQL.as_sql returns '(%s)' % self.sql and leaves user-supplied newlines intact, so the compiler receives multiline SQL strings. This is the input that causes the ordering_parts regex to fail. 

5) Deduplication key depends on extracted expression and params — get_order_by builds seen entries using the tuple (without_ordering, params_hash) (i.e., the deduplication formula is: dedup_key = (self.ordering_parts.search(sql).group(1), params)). Because without_ordering is truncated, the dedup_key collides for distinct expressions. 

6) Ordering token assumption — django/db/models/expressions.py:OrderBy.template emits '%(expression)s %(ordering)s' and ordering uses literal 'ASC'/'DESC'; the ordering_parts regex depends on these exact tokens. (This is a hardcoded token dependency the extraction relies on.)
StepsToReproduce: Define RawSQL instances containing multiline SQL expressions (including newlines) that differ before but share identical final lines. Example: three RawSQL CASE expressions where the last line is the same 'else null end' or similar.
Call queryset.order_by(...) with those RawSQL expressions, e.g. MyModel.objects.all().order_by(RawSQL(multiline_case_1).desc(), RawSQL(multiline_case_2).asc(), RawSQL(multiline_case_3).desc()).
Evaluate/iterate the queryset so Django compiles the SQL (e.g., list(queryset) or print(queryset.query)).
Observe the generated SQL ORDER BY: one of the provided ordering expressions is missing (the middle one in the example).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: All ORDER BY expressions provided by the user are preserved in the compiled SQL in the same order and with the requested ASC/DESC, regardless of whether expressions are multiline or contain identical trailing lines.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: One of the ORDER BY clauses created from multiline RawSQL is removed from the compiled SQL. The compiler's ordering_parts regex returns only the final line (e.g., 'else null end') as the extracted expression, causing distinct multiline expressions that share that trailing line to be treated as duplicates and one to be dropped.

hints_text:
Is there a reason you can't use ​conditional expressions, e.g. something like: MyModel.objects.annotate( custom_order=Case( When(...), ) ).order_by('custom_order') I'm thinking that would avoid fiddly ordering_parts regular expression. If there's some shortcoming to that approach, it might be easier to address that. Allowing the ordering optimization stuff to handle arbitrary RawSQL may be difficult.
Is there a reason you can't use ​conditional expressions No, but I didn't knew about the issue, and writing raw sqls is sometimes faster (not in this case ;) I'm really happy having possibility to mix raw sqls with object queries. Next time I'll use expressions, for sure. Allowing the ordering optimization stuff to handle arbitrary RawSQL may be difficult. Personally I'd like to skip RawSQL clauses in the block which is responsible for finding duplicates. If someone is using raw sqls, he knows the best what he is doing, IMO. And it is quite strange if Django removes silently part of your SQL. This is very confusing. And please note that printing a Query instance was generating incomplete sql, but while checking Query.order_by manually, the return value was containing all clauses. I thought that just printing was affected, but our QA dept told me the truth ;) I know there is no effective way to compare similarity of two raw clauses. This may be hard for expression objects, too, but you have a possibility to implement some __eq__ magic (instead of comparation of generated sqls). Unfortunately I don't know why duplicates detection was implemented, so it's hard to tell how to improve this part.
Patches welcome, I suppose.
​PR
Is there a reason why you didn't add tests?
I was waiting for confirmation, I've added a test. Is it enough?

problem_statement.Title: Merging 3+ Media objects can emit spurious MediaOrderConflictWarning and produce unintuitive ordering
problem_statement.Description: When three or more Media objects are merged (e.g. via widget/form media composition), Django can raise a MediaOrderConflictWarning for files whose relative order was not actually specified by both inputs, and the final JS ordering can be unintuitive. The warning and reported pair of files can be incorrect for three-way merges.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect warning predicate in Media.merge (django/forms/widgets.py: class Media, static method merge): merge builds combined_list = list(list_1) and sets last_insert_index = len(list_1); during reversed traversal of list_2 it treats the condition "index > last_insert_index" as evidence of an ordering conflict between list_1 and list_2 and unconditionally uses combined_list[last_insert_index] and combined_list[index] in the warning. This encodes the wrong assumption that an observed reversal in the intermediate combined_list implies an explicit disagreement between the two input lists.

2) n-way merging is implemented as sequential pairwise merges (django/forms/widgets.py: Media.__add__ and Media._js): Media.__add__ accumulates _js_lists and Media._js computes the final list by repeated binary merges (js = merge(js, obj)). Therefore a prior insertion from an earlier list (A or B) can create an observed reversal when merging the intermediate result with a later list C, even though C and the earlier list did not disagree. The implementation encodes merge(merge(A, B), C) semantics, which interacts with (1) to produce spurious warnings.

3) Warning uses elements from the intermediate combined_list that may not be present in both inputs (django/forms/widgets.py: Media.merge): the code reports combined_list[last_insert_index] and combined_list[index] as the two files in conflict without verifying those two elements are members of both list_1 and list_2. The correct predicate for an explicit conflict (as used as a diagnostic relationship) is that both implicated elements belong to the intersection: a, b ∈ set(list_1) ∩ set(list_2).

4) Test coverage gap (tests/forms_tests/tests/test_media.py): there is no existing test asserting absence of MediaOrderConflictWarning for the three-way scenario from the bug report (['color-picker.js'], ['text-editor.js'], ['text-editor.js','text-editor-extras.js','color-picker.js']), so regressions that only affect warning emission are not caught. This missing test location encodes an assumption that current warnings are correct for multi-way merges.
StepsToReproduce: Create the following widgets and form in a Django project:
from django import forms

class MyForm(forms.Form):
	background_color = forms.CharField(widget=ColorPicker())
	intro = forms.CharField(widget=SimpleTextWidget())
	body = forms.CharField(widget=FancyTextWidget())
Instantiate MyForm() and access MyForm().media (or print MyForm().media._js).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: No MediaOrderConflictWarning should be emitted for this three-way composition, and the final resolved JS order should be user-intuitively ordered as: ['text-editor.js', 'text-editor-extras.js', 'color-picker.js']. The warning, if emitted, should refer only to a pair of files whose relative order was explicitly specified by both inputs.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior:
Accessing MyForm().media emits a MediaOrderConflictWarning reporting the pair:
text-editor-extras.js
text-editor.js
and the final Media output (observed) is: Media(css={}, js=['text-editor-extras.js', 'color-picker.js', 'text-editor.js']).

hints_text:
As a tentative fix, I propose that media objects should explicitly distinguish between cases where we do / don't care about ordering, notionally something like: class FancyTextWidget(forms.Widget): class Media: js = { ('text-editor.js', 'text-editor-extras.js'), # tuple = order is important 'color-picker.js' # set = order is unimportant } (although using a set for this is problematic due to the need for contents to be hashable), and the result of adding two media objects should be a "don't care" so that we aren't introducing dependencies where the original objects didn't have them. We would then defer assembling them into a flat list until the final render call. I haven't worked out the rest of the algorithm yet, but I'm willing to dig further if this sounds like a sensible plan of attack...
Are you testing with the fix from #30153?
Yes, testing against current master (b39bd0aa6d5667d6bbcf7d349a1035c676e3f972).
So ​https://github.com/django/django/commit/959d0c078a1c903cd1e4850932be77c4f0d2294d (the fix for #30153) didn't make this case worse, it just didn't improve on it. The problem is actually the same I encountered, with the same unintuitive error message too. There is still a way to produce a conflicting order but it's harder to trigger in the administration interface now but unfortunately still easy. Also, going back to the state of things pre 2.0 was already discussed previously and rejected. Here's a failing test and and an idea to make this particular test pass: Merge the JS sublists starting from the longest list and continuing with shorter lists. The CSS case is missing yet. The right thing to do would be (against ​worse is better) to add some sort of dependency resolution solver with backtracking but that's surely a bad idea for many other reasons. The change makes some old tests fail (I only took a closer look at test_merge_js_three_way and in this case the failure is fine -- custom_widget.js is allowed to appear before jquery.js.) diff --git a/django/forms/widgets.py b/django/forms/widgets.py index 02aa32b207..d85c409152 100644 --- a/django/forms/widgets.py +++ b/django/forms/widgets.py @@ -70,9 +70,15 @@ class Media: @property def _js(self): - js = self._js_lists[0] + sorted_by_length = list(sorted( + filter(None, self._js_lists), + key=lambda lst: -len(lst), + )) + if not sorted_by_length: + return [] + js = sorted_by_length[0] # filter(None, ...) avoids calling merge() with empty lists. - for obj in filter(None, self._js_lists[1:]): + for obj in filter(None, sorted_by_length[1:]): js = self.merge(js, obj) return js diff --git a/tests/forms_tests/tests/test_media.py b/tests/forms_tests/tests/test_media.py index 8cb484a15e..9d17ad403b 100644 --- a/tests/forms_tests/tests/test_media.py +++ b/tests/forms_tests/tests/test_media.py @@ -571,3 +571,12 @@ class FormsMediaTestCase(SimpleTestCase): # was never specified. merged = widget3 + form1 + form2 self.assertEqual(merged._css, {'screen': ['a.css', 'b.css'], 'all': ['c.css']}) + + def test_merge_js_some_more(self): + widget1 = Media(js=['color-picker.js']) + widget2 = Media(js=['text-editor.js']) + widget3 = Media(js=['text-editor.js', 'text-editor-extras.js', 'color-picker.js']) + + merged = widget1 + widget2 + widget3 + + self.assertEqual(merged._js, ['text-editor.js', 'text-editor-extras.js', 'color-picker.js'])
Thinking some more: sorted() is more likely to break existing code because people probably haven't listed all dependencies in their js attributes now. Yes, that's not what they should have done, but breaking peoples' projects sucks and I don't really want to do that (even if introducing sorted() might be the least disruptive and at the same time most correct change) wanting to handle the jquery, widget1, noConflict and jquery, widget2, noConflict case has introduced an unexpected amount of complexity introducing a complex solving framework will have a really bad impact on runtime and will introduce even more complexity and is out of the question to me I'm happy to help fixing this but right now I only see bad and worse choices.
I don't think sorting by length is the way to go - it would be trivial to make the test fail again by extending the first list with unrelated items. It might be a good real-world heuristic for finding a solution more often, but that's just trading a reproducible bug for an unpredictable one. (I'm not sure I'd trust it as a heuristic either: we've encountered this issue on Wagtail CMS, where we're making extensive use of form media on hierarchical form structures, and so those media definitions will tend to bubble up several layers to reach the top level. At that point, there's no way of knowing whether the longer list is the one with more complex dependencies, or just one that collected more unrelated files on the way up the tree...) I'll do some more thinking on this. My hunch is that even if it does end up being a travelling-salesman-type problem, it's unlikely to be run on a large enough data set for performance to be an issue.
I don't think sorting by length is the way to go - it would be trivial to make the test fail again by extending the first list with unrelated items. It might be a good real-world heuristic for finding a solution more often, but that's just trading a reproducible bug for an unpredictable one. Well yes, if the ColorPicker itself would have a longer list of JS files it depends on then it would fail too. If, on the other hand, it wasn't a ColorPicker widget but a ColorPicker formset or form the initially declared lists would still be preserved and sorting the lists by length would give the correct result. Since #30153 the initially declared lists (or tuples) are preserved so maybe you have many JS and CSS declarations but as long as they are unrelated there will not be many long sublists. I'm obviously happy though if you're willing to spend the time finding a robust solution to this problem. (For the record: Personally I was happy with the state of things pre-2.0 too... and For the record 2: I'm also using custom widgets and inlines in feincms3/django-content-editor. It's really surprising to me that we didn't stumble on this earlier since we're always working on the latest Django version or even on pre-release versions if at all possible)
Hi there, I'm the dude who implemented the warning. I am not so sure this is a bug. Let's try tackle this step by step. The new merging algorithm that was introduced in version 2 is an improvement. It is the most accurate way to merge two sorted lists. It's not the simplest way, but has been reviewed plenty times. The warning is another story. It is independent from the algorithm. It merely tells you that the a certain order could not be maintained. We figured back than, that this would be a good idea. It warns a developer about a potential issue, but does not raise an exception. With that in mind, the correct way to deal with the issue described right now, is to ignore the warning. BUT, that doesn't mean that you don't have a valid point. There are implicit and explicit orders. Not all assets require ordering and (random) orders that only exist because of Media merging don't matter at all. This brings me back to a point that I have [previously made](https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/30153#comment:6). It would make sense to store the original lists, which is now the case on master, and only raise if the order violates the original list. The current implementation on master could also be improved by removing duplicates. Anyways, I would considers those changes improvements, but not bug fixes. I didn't have time yet to look into this. But I do have some time this weekend. If you want I can take another look into this and propose a solution that solves this issue. Best -Joe
"Ignore the warning" doesn't work here - the order-fixing has broken the dependency between text-editor.js and text-editor-extras.js. I can (reluctantly) accept an implementation that produces false warnings, and I can accept that a genuine dependency loop might produce undefined behaviour, but the combination of the two - breaking the ordering as a result of seeing a loop that isn't there - is definitely a bug. (To be clear, I'm not suggesting that the 2.x implementation is a step backwards from not doing order checking at all - but it does introduce a new failure case, and that's what I'm keen to fix.)
To summarise: Even with the new strategy in #30153 of holding on to the un-merged lists as long as possible, the final merging is still done by adding one list at a time. The intermediate results are lists, which are assumed to be order-critical; this means the intermediate results have additional constraints that are not present in the original lists, causing it to see conflicts where there aren't any. Additionally, we should try to preserve the original sequence of files as much as possible, to avoid unnecessarily breaking user code that hasn't fully specified its dependencies and is relying on the 1.x behaviour. I think we need to approach this as a graph problem (which I realise might sound like overkill, but I'd rather start with something formally correct and optimise later as necessary): a conflict occurs whenever the dependency graph is cyclic. #30153 is a useful step towards this, as it ensures we have the accurate dependency graph up until the point where we need to assemble the final list. I suggest we replace Media.merge with a new method that accepts any number of lists (using *args if we want to preserve the existing method signature for backwards compatibility). This would work as follows: Iterate over all items in all sub-lists, building a dependency graph (where a dependency is any item that immediately precedes it within a sub-list) and a de-duplicated list containing all items indexed in the order they are first encountered Starting from the first item in the de-duplicated list, backtrack through the dependency graph, following the lowest-indexed dependency each time until we reach an item with no dependencies. While backtracking, maintain a stack of visited items. If we encounter an item already on the stack, this is a dependency loop; throw a MediaOrderConflictWarning and break out of the backtracking loop Output the resulting item, then remove it from the dependency graph and the de-duplicated list If the 'visited items' stack is non-empty, pop the last item off it and repeat the backtracking step from there. Otherwise, repeat the backtracking step starting from the next item in the de-duplicated list Repeat until no items remain
This sounds correct. I'm not sure it's right though. It does sound awfully complex for what there is to gain. Maintaining this down the road will not get easier. Finding, explaining and understanding the fix for #30153 did already cost a lot of time which could also have been invested elsewhere. If I manually assign widget3's JS lists (see https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/30179#comment:5) then everything just works and the final result is correct: # widget3 = Media(js=['text-editor.js', 'text-editor-extras.js', 'color-picker.js']) widget3 = Media() widget3._js_lists = [['text-editor.js', 'text-editor-extras.js'], ['color-picker.js']] So what you proposed first (https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/30179#comment:1) might just work fine and would be good enough (tm). Something like ​https://github.com/django/django/blob/543fc97407a932613d283c1e0bb47616cf8782e3/django/forms/widgets.py#L52 # Instead of self._js_lists = [js]: self._js_lists = list(js) if isinstance(js, set) else [js]
@Matthias: I think that solution will work, but only if: 1) we're going to insist that users always use this notation wherever a "non-dependency" exists - i.e. it is considered user error for the user to forget to put color-picker.js in its own sub-list 2) we have a very tight definition of what a dependency is - e.g. color-picker.js can't legally be a dependency of text-editor.js / text-editor-extras.js, because it exists on its own in ColorPicker's media - which also invalidates the [jquery, widget1, noconflict] + [jquery, widget2, noconflict] case (does noconflict depend on widget1 or not?) I suspect you only have to go slightly before the complexity of [jquery, widget1, noconflict] + [jquery, widget2, noconflict] before you start running into counter-examples again.
PR: ​https://github.com/django/django/pull/11010 I encountered another subtle bug along the way (which I suspect has existed since 1.x): #12879 calls for us to strip duplicates from the input lists, but in the current implementation the only de-duplication happens during Media.merge, so this never happens in the case of a single list. I've now extended the tests to cover this: ​https://github.com/django/django/pull/11010/files#diff-7fc04ae9019782c1884a0e97e96eda1eR154 . As a minor side effect of this extra de-duplication step, tuples get converted to lists more often, so I've had to fix up some existing tests accordingly - hopefully that's acceptable fall-out :-)
Matt, great work. I believe it is best to merge all lists at once and not sequentially as I did. Based on your work, I would suggest to simply use the algorithms implemented in Python. Therefore the whole merge function can be replaced with a simple one liner: import heapq from collections import OrderedDict def merge(*sublists): return list(OrderedDict.fromkeys(heapq.merge(*sublists))) # >>> merge([3],[1],[1,2],[2,3]) # [1, 2, 3]
It actually behaves different. I will continue to review your pull-request. As stated there, it would be helpful if there is some kind of resource to understand what strategy you implemented. For now I will try to review it without it.

problem_statement.Title: sqlmigrate prints BEGIN/COMMIT even when backend cannot roll back DDL
problem_statement.Description: The sqlmigrate management command prints transaction wrappers (BEGIN/COMMIT) around generated SQL whenever a migration is marked atomic, even if the current database backend does not support rolling back DDL. This makes the displayed SQL misleading compared to how the migration would actually run.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption: sqlmigrate assumes migration.atomic alone determines whether to show transaction wrappers. Location: django/core/management/commands/sqlmigrate.py: Command.handle — it sets self.output_transaction = migration.atomic. (Currently: output_transaction = migration.atomic; correct relationship used elsewhere is a conjunction.)

2) Incorrect assumption: BaseCommand trusts subclass-controlled self.output_transaction as the single source of truth for whether to wrap SQL in transaction START/END text. Location: django/core/management/base.py: BaseCommand.execute — it adds connection.ops.start_transaction_sql()/end_transaction_sql() when self.output_transaction is True.

3) Inconsistency: the schema editor computes actual atomic execution using both the migration atomic flag and the backend feature flag. Location & formula: django/db/backends/base/schema.py: BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor — atomic_migration = connection.features.can_rollback_ddl and atomic. This shows sqlmigrate's single-variable decision is inconsistent with runtime behavior.

4) Hardcoded/default feature value: the backend feature flag defaults to False, so many backends will not support DDL rollback. Location & value: django/db/backends/base/features.py: BaseDatabaseFeatures.can_rollback_ddl = False.

5) Test coverage gap: existing tests only verify no-wrappers for migration.atomic == False by switching migration modules; there is no test for migration.atomic == True combined with connection.features.can_rollback_ddl == False. Location: tests/migrations/test_commands.py — missing test that mocks connection.features.can_rollback_ddl = False while using an atomic migration.

6) Related commands encode a different assumption: other management commands (sqlflush, sqlsequencereset) set output_transaction = True at class level, so they will always print wrappers regardless of can_rollback_ddl. Location: command classes for sqlflush and sqlsequencereset (class-level attribute output_transaction = True).
StepsToReproduce: Have a migration that is marked atomic (migration.atomic == True).

Ensure the current DB backend does not support transactional DDL (connection.features.can_rollback_ddl == False). For testing, patch/mock: mock.patch.object(connection.features, 'can_rollback_ddl', False).
Run the management command: ./manage.py sqlmigrate <app_label> <migration_name> (or call_command('sqlmigrate', ...)).
Inspect the command output and observe that BEGIN and COMMIT lines are present around the generated SQL.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When displaying SQL for a migration, BEGIN/COMMIT transaction wrappers are shown only if the migration is atomic and the current database backend actually supports rolling back DDL (i.e., only when both migration.atomic is True and connection.features.can_rollback_ddl is True).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: sqlmigrate displays BEGIN/COMMIT wrappers whenever migration.atomic is True, even on backends where connection.features.can_rollback_ddl is False, causing the printed SQL to imply transactional DDL where it would not occur at runtime.

problem_statement.Title: Correct expected format in invalid DurationField error message
problem_statement.Description: The user-facing format string for DurationField is incorrect and misleading. The built-in error message and form help do not match the actual parser behavior: the message says "[DD] [HH:[MM:]]ss[.uuuuuu]" while the parser accepts "[DD] [[HH:]MM:]ss[.uuuuuu]" (seconds required; minutes optional; hours optional only when minutes are present). Model and form DurationField messages also lack a clear default help_text.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect hardcoded model error message: django/db/models/fields/__init__.py::DurationField.default_error_messages['invalid'] encodes the format as "[DD] [HH:[MM:]]ss[.uuuuuu]", which misstates which components are optional. 

2) Generic form error message: django/forms/fields.py::DurationField.default_error_messages['invalid'] is a generic "Enter a valid duration." and provides no user-visible format guidance. 

3) Missing model->form help text: django/db/models/fields/__init__.py::DurationField.formfield does not set a default help_text when the model field has none, so ModelForm/ADMINS forms show no format hint. 

4) Authoritative parser enforces a different relationship: django/utils/dateparse.py::standard_duration_re requires seconds and makes minutes optional and hours optional only when minutes are present (regex fragments: r'((?:(?P<hours>\d+):)(?=\d+:\d+))?' , r'(?:(?P<minutes>\d+):)?' , r'(?P<seconds>\d+)'). The user-visible format must reflect this rule; the correct format string is "[DD] [[HH:]MM:]ss[.uuuuuu]". 

5) Tests/docs dependency risk: tests and documentation (e.g. tests/forms_tests/field_tests/test_durationfield.py) may assert the old exact message text; these locations encode an implicit assumption about the old string and must be considered when changing messages. 

6) No parser change required: the regex in django/utils/dateparse.py is the authoritative implementation of accepted inputs and should not be altered to match the old message.
StepsToReproduce: In a Django project define a model with a DurationField (or use an existing one).
Open a ModelForm or the admin for an instance of that model.
Enter the text "14:00" into the duration input and submit the form.
Observe the parsed value and any ValidationError messages shown for the field.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When a user enters a duration, invalid-value feedback should clearly state the accepted format that the parser actually accepts: "[DD] [[HH:]MM:]ss[.uuuuuu]" (seconds mandatory; minutes optional; hours optional only if minutes present). Model-driven forms should provide a helpful default help_text when none is supplied so users see the expected format.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The invalid-duration message shown to users says "[DD] [HH:[MM:]]ss[.uuuuuu]" which misstates optionality. Form fields show a generic "Enter a valid duration." and ModelForms do not provide a default help_text. Entering "14:00" is parsed as "00:14:00" (14 minutes), which can be surprising given the misleading guidance.

problem_statement.Title: UsernameValidator allows trailing newline in usernames
problem_statement.Description: ASCIIUsernameValidator and UnicodeUsernameValidator accept usernames that end with a trailing newline because their class-level regex patterns use ^...$ anchors which, in Python, allow a match before a final newline.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Incorrect assumption: ^...$ enforces absolute end-of-string. Location: django/contrib/auth/validators.py — ASCIIUsernameValidator.regex = r'^[\w.@+-]+$'. Evidence: $ can match before a final '\n', so the pattern accepts 'joe\n'. Correct relationship: the pattern must enforce absolute anchors (e.g. r'\A[\w.@+-]+\Z').

2. Incorrect assumption repeated: ^...$ used for UnicodeUsernameValidator. Location: django/contrib/auth/validators.py — UnicodeUsernameValidator.regex = r'^[\w.@+-]+$' (flags = 0). Evidence: same $ newline quirk causes trailing-newline usernames to be accepted.

3. Operational context making the anchors critical: RegexValidator applies the pattern with self.regex.search(str(value)). Location: django/core/validators.py::RegexValidator.__call__. Relationship: because the implementation uses regex.search rather than a full-match API, the pattern itself must enforce full-string constraints (use \A and \Z) to prevent matches that rely on $ matching before a final newline.

4. Missing test coverage for the edge case: tests/auth_tests/test_validators.py does not contain a test asserting that usernames ending with '\n' are invalid. Location: tests/auth_tests/test_validators.py: UsernameValidatorsTests. Evidence: absence of a trailing-newline test allows this regression to persist unnoticed.

5. Deconstruction/migration note: both validator classes are @deconstructible and used by the User model (django/contrib/auth/models.py sets username_validator = UnicodeUsernameValidator()). Location: django/contrib/auth/validators.py and django/contrib/auth/models.py. Relationship: changing the class-level regex affects the validator's default state used by migrations/serializations.
StepsToReproduce: Create an instance of UnicodeUsernameValidator() and call it with the value 'joe\n'. Observe whether a ValidationError is raised.
Create an instance of ASCIIUsernameValidator() and call it with the value 'glenn\n'. Observe whether a ValidationError is raised.
Attempt to create or save a User (or other model field using the username validator) with username set to 'alice\n' and observe whether model validation allows it.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Usernames that end with a newline character are rejected by the username validators and produce a validation error when validating or saving user data.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Username validators accept strings that end with a newline (for example, 'joe\n' is considered valid), allowing creation or validation of usernames with trailing newlines.

problem_statement.Title: HttpResponse mishandles memoryview/bytearray and returns repr bytes instead of underlying data
problem_statement.Description: When a memoryview (or bytearray) is passed into HttpResponse (e.g. HttpResponse(memoryview(b"My Content"))), the response body becomes the object's repr encoded as bytes (b'<memory at 0x...>') instead of the underlying binary data (b'My Content'). This appears when DB drivers (PostgreSQL) return BinaryField contents as memoryview.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/http/response.py::HttpResponseBase.make_bytes — incorrect assumption: any non-bytes, non-str value should be stringified and encoded. The code falls back to return str(value).encode(self.charset), which converts memoryview/bytearray to their repr (b'<memory at 0x...>') instead of their buffer contents. Correct relationship: bytes-like buffers should be converted with bytes(value). Conceptual current logic: if isinstance(value, bytes): return bytes(value) ; if isinstance(value, str): return value.encode(self.charset) ; return str(value).encode(self.charset). Correct relationship (preserve): if isinstance(value, (bytes, bytearray, memoryview)): return bytes(value) ; if isinstance(value, str): return value.encode(self.charset) ; return str(value).encode(self.charset).

2) django/http/response.py::HttpResponse.content (setter) — incorrect assumption: any object with __iter__ (except bytes and str) is an iterable-of-chunks to be joined. The setter uses: if hasattr(value, '__iter__') and not isinstance(value, (bytes, str)): ... which treats memoryview and bytearray as iterables and iterates them, producing wrong assembly even if make_bytes is fixed. Correct relationship: the iterable-of-chunks branch must exclude bytearray and memoryview so they are handled as single bytes-like values. Conceptual conditional change: replace "not isinstance(value, (bytes, str))" with "not isinstance(value, (bytes, str, bytearray, memoryview))".

3) Both locations are coupled — django/http/response.py::HttpResponseBase.make_bytes and ::HttpResponse.content (setter) must both be reconciled. If only make_bytes is changed, content.setter may still iterate a memoryview and join chunk results incorrectly; if only content.setter is changed, make_bytes will still stringify unknown objects. Both incorrect assumptions together cause the observed behavior.

4) django/http/response.py::StreamingHttpResponse._set_streaming_content — remaining semantic gap: StreamingHttpResponse expects streaming_content to be an iterable-of-chunks. If a user passes a bare memoryview as streaming_content, the implementation will iterate it (yielding ints/bytes), which may be surprising; the agent did not decide whether to special-case bare memoryview for streaming_content. This is a related API/semantics concern and a potential remaining edge case.

5) Missing test coverage — there are no (reported) unit tests covering HttpResponse and StreamingHttpResponse constructed with memoryview/bytearray inputs (constructor, write/writelines, streaming_content), so the incorrect behavior is not caught by tests and may regress. Adding tests for these inputs is required to validate the intended relationships above.
StepsToReproduce: from django.http import HttpResponse
response = HttpResponse("My Content")
inspect response.content  # shows b'My Content'
response = HttpResponse(b"My Content")
inspect response.content  # shows b'My Content'
response = HttpResponse(memoryview(b"My Content"))
inspect response.content  # shows b'<memory at 0x...>' (incorrect)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When given bytes-like inputs (bytes, bytearray, memoryview), HttpResponse.content should contain the underlying binary data. Example: HttpResponse(memoryview(b"My Content")).content == b"My Content". Similarly, HttpResponse(bytearray(b"...")).content == underlying bytes. For streaming responses, an iterable that yields memoryview objects should produce their underlying bytes when assembled.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: HttpResponse(memoryview(b"My Content")).content returns the memoryview object's repr encoded as bytes (e.g. b"<memory at 0x7fcc47ab2648>") instead of b"My Content". Memoryview/bytearray are treated either as stringifiable objects (make_bytes stringifies) or as iterables-of-chunks (content.setter iterates them), producing incorrect output.

hints_text:
I guess HttpResponseBase.make_bytes ​could be adapted to deal with memoryview objects by casting them to bytes. In all cases simply wrapping the memoryview in bytes works as a workaround HttpResponse(bytes(model.binary_field)).
The fact make_bytes would still use force_bytes if da56e1bac6449daef9aeab8d076d2594d9fd5b44 didn't refactor it and that d680a3f4477056c69629b0421db4bb254b8c69d0 added memoryview support to force_bytes strengthen my assumption that make_bytes should be adjusted as well.
I'll try to work on this.

problem_statement.Title: Collector.delete fast-path does not clear in-memory instance PK after delete
problem_statement.Description: When deleting a single model instance that has no related dependencies, the fast-path in Collector.delete issues the DB DELETE but returns before clearing the instance's primary key attribute in memory. The in-memory instance retains its old pk after .delete().
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Wrong assumption: The single-object "fast delete" branch can return immediately without performing per-instance in-memory cleanup. Location: django/db/models/deletion.py :: Collector.delete. Evidence/formula: fast-path runs with transaction.mark_for_rollback_on_error(): count = sql.DeleteQuery(model).delete_batch([instance.pk], self.using) and then returns count, {model._meta.label: count} without calling setattr(instance, model._meta.pk.attname, None).

2) Missing mirror of intended cleanup: The non-fast-path relies on per-instance cleanup expressed as setattr(instance, model._meta.pk.attname, None). Location: django/db/models/deletion.py :: Collector.delete (cleanup loop). This is the concrete operation the fast-path omitted.

3) Callers depend on Collector.delete to produce the post-delete in-memory state (including clearing pk). Location(s): django/db/models/base.py :: Model.delete and django/db/models/query.py :: QuerySet.delete. These callers call collector.collect(...) then return collector.delete() and do not clear instance.pk themselves.

4) Return-shape constraint: The fast-path encodes an early return of (count, {model._meta.label: count}); any fix must preserve this return tuple shape so callers remain compatible. Location: django/db/models/deletion.py :: Collector.delete (fast-path return).

5) Investigation omissions (affecting confidence in scope, not the primary root cause): The agent did not fully audit Collector.fast_deletes to confirm no other in-memory cleanup is required, did not verify interactions with pre_delete/post_delete signal handlers (which may expect instance.pk during signals), and did not run/attach a unit test demonstrating instance.pk becomes None after the fast-path .delete(). Locations to review: django/db/models/deletion.py :: Collector.fast_deletes and relevant signal handling call sites.
StepsToReproduce: Define or use a model with no related/dependent objects.
Create and save an instance of that model (e.g., obj = MyModel.objects.create(...)).
Call obj.delete().
Inspect the in-memory instance's primary key (e.g., print(obj.pk) or assert obj.pk is None).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: After calling .delete() on a model instance with no dependencies, the instance's in-memory primary key (pk) should be cleared (visible as None) to reflect that the row no longer exists in the database.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: After calling .delete() on a model instance with no dependencies, the instance's in-memory primary key still holds the previous value (not None) even though the database row was deleted.

hints_text:
Reproduced at 1ffddfc233e2d5139cc6ec31a4ec6ef70b10f87f. Regression in bc7dd8490b882b2cefdc7faf431dc64c532b79c9. Thanks for the report.
Regression test.
I have attached a simple fix which mimics what ​https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/db/models/deletion.py#L324-L326 does for multiple objects. I am not sure if we need ​https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/db/models/deletion.py#L320-L323 (the block above) because I think field_updates is only ever filled if the objects are not fast-deletable -- ie ​https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/db/models/deletion.py#L224 is not called due to the can_fast_delete check at the beginning of the collect function. That said, if we want to be extra "safe" we can just move lines 320 - 326 into an extra function and call that from the old and new location (though I do not think it is needed).

problem_statement.Title: auth.0011_update_proxy_permissions raises IntegrityError when permissions already exist for proxy target
problem_statement.Description: When running migrations (auth.0011_update_proxy_permissions) on a project with models recreated/converted to proxy models, the migration can raise django.db.utils.IntegrityError due to duplicate (content_type, codename) keys in auth_permission. The migration attempts to move Permission.content_type values in bulk and collides with existing Permission rows for the target content type.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption: a bulk update of Permission.content_type will not create duplicate (content_type, codename) pairs. Location: django/contrib/auth/migrations/0011_update_proxy_permissions.py:update_proxy_model_permissions. Evidence: the code performs Permission.objects.filter(..., content_type=old_content_type).update(content_type=new_content_type) which can produce duplicates when a Permission with the same (content_type=new_content_type, codename) already exists (IntegrityError shown in stacktrace).

2) Incorrect assumption: it is safe to change Permission.content_type values without respecting the model/DB uniqueness invariant. Location: django/contrib/auth/models.py: Permission.Meta.unique_together = (('content_type', 'codename'),). Relationship to preserve: Permission uniqueness = unique_together(('content_type','codename')). The migration violates this invariant by producing two rows with the same (content_type, codename).

3) Incorrect assumption: ContentType lookup semantics do not affect which Permission rows are moved. Location: django/contrib/contenttypes/models.py: ContentTypeManager.get_for_model. Relationship used by the migration: get_for_model(model, for_concrete_model=True) uses model._meta.concrete_model to obtain the concrete_content_type and get_for_model(model, for_concrete_model=False) obtains the proxy_content_type; the migration relies on these distinct content types as old/new.

4) Incorrect assumption: moving Permission rows or changing permission_id values in many-to-many through tables will not create duplicate through-table rows. Location: Group/User many-to-many through models (resolved via Group._meta.get_field('permissions').remote_field.through and User._meta.get_field('user_permissions').remote_field.through). Relationship to respect: through-table uniqueness for pairs (group_id, permission_id) and (user_id, permission_id). The migration does not check for existing target through-table rows before reassigning, which can violate these uniqueness constraints.

5) Incorrect assumption: reverting the migration is safe by delegating to the same bulk-update logic in reverse. Location: django/contrib/auth/migrations/0011_update_proxy_permissions.py:revert_proxy_model_permissions (calls update_proxy_model_permissions(..., reverse=True)). The same collision risks apply in reverse and require symmetric handling of duplicates and through-table mappings.
StepsToReproduce: Have a Django project with existing auth.Permission rows and a model that has been recreated/converted as a proxy model (so permissions exist for both the concrete and proxy ContentType scenarios).
Upgrade or set the project to use Django 2.2 containing auth.0011_update_proxy_permissions migration.
Run python manage.py migrate.
Observe the migration failing while applying auth.0011_update_proxy_permissions with an IntegrityError about duplicate key (content_type_id, codename).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Migrations complete successfully. Permissions that need to be associated with a proxy content type are moved or merged without raising unique-constraint errors; resulting auth_permission rows maintain the invariant that (content_type, codename) is unique, and user/group permission relations are preserved without creating duplicate through-table rows.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Migration auth.0011_update_proxy_permissions raises django.db.utils.IntegrityError: duplicate key value violates unique constraint on (content_type_id, codename) because the migration performs a blind bulk update of Permission.content_type which collides with existing Permission rows for the target content type. Migration aborts with the IntegrityError.

hints_text:
Please provide a sample project or enough details to reproduce the issue.
Same problem for me. If a Permission exists already with the new content_type and permission name, IntegrityError is raised since it violates the unique_key constraint on permission model i.e. content_type_id_code_name
To get into the situation where you already have permissions with the content type you should be able to do the following: Start on Django <2.2 Create a model called 'TestModel' Migrate Delete the model called 'TestModel' Add a new proxy model called 'TestModel' Migrate Update to Django >=2.2 Migrate We think this is what happened in our case where we found this issue (​https://sentry.thalia.nu/share/issue/68be0f8c32764dec97855b3cbb3d8b55/). We have a proxy model with the same name that a previous non-proxy model once had. This changed during a refactor and the permissions + content type for the original model still exist. Our solution will probably be removing the existing permissions from the table, but that's really only a workaround.
Reproduced with steps from comment. It's probably regression in 181fb60159e54d442d3610f4afba6f066a6dac05.
What happens when creating a regular model, deleting it and creating a new proxy model: Create model 'RegularThenProxyModel' +----------------------------------+---------------------------+-----------------------+ | name | codename | model | +----------------------------------+---------------------------+-----------------------+ | Can add regular then proxy model | add_regularthenproxymodel | regularthenproxymodel | +----------------------------------+---------------------------+-----------------------+ Migrate Delete the model called 'RegularThenProxyModel' Add a new proxy model called 'RegularThenProxyModel' +----------------------------------+---------------------------+-----------------------+ | name | codename | model | +----------------------------------+---------------------------+-----------------------+ | Can add concrete model | add_concretemodel | concretemodel | | Can add regular then proxy model | add_regularthenproxymodel | concretemodel | | Can add regular then proxy model | add_regularthenproxymodel | regularthenproxymodel | +----------------------------------+---------------------------+-----------------------+ What happens when creating a proxy model right away: Create a proxy model 'RegularThenProxyModel' +----------------------------------+---------------------------+---------------+ | name | codename | model | +----------------------------------+---------------------------+---------------+ | Can add concrete model | add_concretemodel | concretemodel | | Can add regular then proxy model | add_regularthenproxymodel | concretemodel | +----------------------------------+---------------------------+---------------+ As you can see, the problem here is that permissions are not cleaned up, so we are left with an existing | Can add regular then proxy model | add_regularthenproxymodel | regularthenproxymodel | row. When the 2.2 migration is applied, it tries to create that exact same row, hence the IntegrityError. Unfortunately, there is no remove_stale_permission management command like the one for ContentType. So I think we can do one of the following: Show a nice error message to let the user delete the conflicting migration OR Re-use the existing permission I think 1. is much safer as it will force users to use a new permission and assign it accordingly to users/groups. Edit: I revised my initial comment after reproducing the error in my environment.
It's also possible to get this kind of integrity error on the auth.0011 migration if another app is migrated first causing the auth post_migrations hook to run. The auth post migrations hook runs django.contrib.auth.management.create_permissions, which writes the new form of the auth_permission records to the table. Then when the auth.0011 migration runs it tries to update things to the values that were just written. To reproduce this behavior: pip install Django==2.1.7 Create an app, let's call it app, with two models, TestModel(models.Model) and ProxyModel(TestModel) the second one with proxy=True python manage.py makemigrations python manage.py migrate pip install Django==2.2 Add another model to app python manage.py makemigrations migrate the app only, python manage.py migrate app. This does not run the auth migrations, but does run the auth post_migrations hook Note that new records have been added to auth_permission python manage.py migrate, this causes an integrity error when the auth.0011 migration tries to update records that are the same as the ones already added in step 8. This has the same exception as this bug report, I don't know if it's considered a different bug, or the same one.
Yes it is the same issue. My recommendation to let the users figure it out with a helpful message still stands even if it may sound a bit painful, because: It prevents data loss (we don't do an automatic delete/create of permissions) It prevents security oversights (we don't re-use an existing permission) It shouldn't happen for most use cases Again, I would love to hear some feedback or other alternatives.
I won’t have time to work on this for the next 2 weeks so I’m de-assigning myself. I’ll pick it up again if nobody does and I’m available to discuss feedback/suggestions.
I'll make a patch for this. I'll see about raising a suitable warning from the migration but we already warn in the release notes for this to audit permissions: my initial thought was that re-using the permission would be OK. (I see Arthur's comment. Other thoughts?)
Being my first contribution I wanted to be super (super) careful with security concerns, but given the existing warning in the release notes for auditing prior to update, I agree that re-using the permission feels pretty safe and would remove overhead for people running into this scenario. Thanks for taking this on Carlton, I'd be happy to review.

problem_statement.Title: Autoreloader (StatReloader/WatchmanReloader) does not detect edits to manage.py (startup script not watched)
problem_statement.Description: When running the development server, editing the project startup script (manage.py) does not trigger the autoreloader to restart the process. The reloader runs but never reports or watches the manage.py file, so changes to that file are ignored.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Missing registration of the startup script in run_with_reloader: django/utils/autoreload.py :: run_with_reloader assumes creating the reloader (reloader = get_reloader()) is sufficient but never calls reloader.watch_file(Path(sys.argv[0])) (i.e., sys.argv[0] is not added to reloader.extra_files). This omission means the startup script is not added to the canonical watched set.

2) watched_files does not include sys.argv[0] by design: django/utils/autoreload.py :: BaseReloader.watched_files computes the watched set as iter_all_python_module_files() + frozenset(self.extra_files) + directory globs. Because sys.argv[0] (manage.py) is generally not an imported module, iter_all_python_module_files() does not include it; without an explicit watch_file() call it is excluded.

3) StatReloader relies on watched_files for change detection: django/utils/autoreload.py :: StatReloader.snapshot_files checks mtimes only for paths in self.watched_files(), using stats[path] = path.stat().st_mtime. If manage.py is not in watched_files(), changes to it never update stats and therefore never trigger a reload.

4) WatchmanReloader relies on watched_files for subscriptions: django/utils/autoreload.py :: WatchmanReloader._update_watches calls watched_files(include_globs=False) and groups results by parent directory to call _subscribe_dir(directory, filenames). If manage.py is missing from watched_files(), no Watchman subscription is created for it and Watchman will not report its changes.

5) start_django/reloader.run timing expectation: django/utils/autoreload.py :: start_django calls reloader.run(...). The code implicitly assumes any files that must be watched (including the startup script) are already registered (in reloader.extra_files) before start_django is invoked; run_with_reloader does not populate extra_files with sys.argv[0], so the child reloader never sees the script.

6) Incorrect implicit assumption (general): The codebase assumes the canonical set of watched files will be discoverable from imported modules, error files, explicit watch_file/watch_dir calls, or configured globs, and does not special-case the process startup script (sys.argv[0]).
StepsToReproduce: Install and run a Django development server (example environment in report: Python 3.6.2, Django 2.2.1).
Start the server: python manage.py runserver
Edit manage.py (for example, add a print statement) and save the file.
Observe that the development server does not autoreload/restart after saving manage.py (no reload occurs).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Editing the project startup script (manage.py) while the development server is running should be detected by the autoreloader and cause the server to restart automatically.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: After modifying manage.py and saving, the development server does not restart; the autoreloader does not report changes to manage.py and no automatic reload occurs.

Thanks for the report. I simplified scenario. Regression in c8720e7696ca41f3262d5369365cc1bd72a216ca. Reproduced at 8d010f39869f107820421631111417298d1c5bb9.
Argh. I guess this is because manage.py isn't showing up in the sys.modules. I'm not sure I remember any specific manage.py handling in the old implementation, so I'm not sure how it used to work, but I should be able to fix this pretty easily.
Done a touch of debugging: iter_modules_and_files is where it gets lost. Specifically, it ends up in there twice: (<module '__future__' from '/../lib/python3.6/__future__.py'>, <module '__main__' from 'manage.py'>, <module '__main__' from 'manage.py'>, ...,) But getattr(module, "__spec__", None) is None is True so it continues onwards. I thought I managed to get one of them to have a __spec__ attr but no has_location, but I can't seem to get that again (stepping around with pdb) Digging into wtf __spec__ is None: ​Here's the py3 docs on it, which helpfully mentions that ​The one exception is __main__, where __spec__ is set to None in some cases
Tom, will you have time to work on this in the next few days?
I'm sorry for assigning it to myself Mariusz, I intended to work on it on Tuesday but work overtook me and now I am travelling for a wedding this weekend. So I doubt it I'm afraid. It seems Keryn's debugging is a great help, it should be somewhat simple to add special case handling for __main__, while __spec__ is None we can still get the filename and watch on that.
np, Tom, thanks for info. Keryn, it looks that you've already made most of the work. Would you like to prepare a patch?

diff --git a/django/conf/__init__.py b/django/conf/__init__.py
--- a/django/conf/__init__.py
+++ b/django/conf/__init__.py
@@ -15,7 +15,8 @@
 
 import django
 from django.conf import global_settings
-from django.core.exceptions import ImproperlyConfigured
+from django.core.exceptions import ImproperlyConfigured, ValidationError
+from django.core.validators import URLValidator
 from django.utils.deprecation import RemovedInDjango40Warning
 from django.utils.functional import LazyObject, empty
 
@@ -109,6 +110,26 @@ def configure(self, default_settings=global_settings, **options):
             setattr(holder, name, value)
         self._wrapped = holder
 
+    @staticmethod
+    def _add_script_prefix(value):
+        """
+        Add SCRIPT_NAME prefix to relative paths.
+
+        Useful when the app is being served at a subpath and manually prefixing
+        subpath to STATIC_URL and MEDIA_URL in settings is inconvenient.
+        """
+        # Don't apply prefix to valid URLs.
+        try:
+            URLValidator()(value)
+            return value
+        except (ValidationError, AttributeError):
+            pass
+        # Don't apply prefix to absolute paths.
+        if value.startswith('/'):
+            return value
+        from django.urls import get_script_prefix
+        return '%s%s' % (get_script_prefix(), value)
+
     @property
     def configured(self):
         """Return True if the settings have already been configured."""
@@ -128,6 +149,14 @@ def PASSWORD_RESET_TIMEOUT_DAYS(self):
             )
         return self.__getattr__('PASSWORD_RESET_TIMEOUT_DAYS')
 
+    @property
+    def STATIC_URL(self):
+        return self._add_script_prefix(self.__getattr__('STATIC_URL'))
+
+    @property
+    def MEDIA_URL(self):
+        return self._add_script_prefix(self.__getattr__('MEDIA_URL'))
+
 
 class Settings:
     def __init__(self, settings_module):
