+class MediaURLStaticURLPrefixTest(SimpleTestCase):
+    def set_script_name(self, val):
+        clear_script_prefix()
+        if val is not None:
+            set_script_prefix(val)
+
+    def test_not_prefixed(self):
+        # Don't add SCRIPT_NAME prefix to valid URLs, absolute paths or None.
+        tests = (
+            '/path/',
+            'http://myhost.com/path/',
+            None,
+        )
+        for setting in ('MEDIA_URL', 'STATIC_URL'):
+            for path in tests:
+                new_settings = {setting: path}
+                with self.settings(**new_settings):
+                    for script_name in ['/somesubpath', '/somesubpath/', '/', '', None]:
+                        with self.subTest(script_name=script_name, **new_settings):
+                            try:
+                                self.set_script_name(script_name)
+                                self.assertEqual(getattr(settings, setting), path)
+                            finally:
+                                clear_script_prefix()
+
+    def test_add_script_name_prefix(self):
+        tests = (
+            # Relative paths.
+            ('/somesubpath', 'path/', '/somesubpath/path/'),
+            ('/somesubpath/', 'path/', '/somesubpath/path/'),
+            ('/', 'path/', '/path/'),
+            # Invalid URLs.
+            ('/somesubpath/', 'htp://myhost.com/path/', '/somesubpath/htp://myhost.com/path/'),
+            # Blank settings.
+            ('/somesubpath/', '', '/somesubpath/'),
+        )
+        for setting in ('MEDIA_URL', 'STATIC_URL'):
+            for script_name, path, expected_path in tests:
+                new_settings = {setting: path}
+                with self.settings(**new_settings):
+                    with self.subTest(script_name=script_name, **new_settings):
+                        try:
+                            self.set_script_name(script_name)
+                            self.assertEqual(getattr(settings, setting), expected_path)
+                        finally:
+                            clear_script_prefix()

problem_statement.Title: Runtime SCRIPT_NAME not applied to STATIC_URL/MEDIA_URL — generated and matched static/media URLs miss SCRIPT_NAME
problem_statement.Description: When a Django app is mounted under a WSGI/ASGI SCRIPT_NAME (a runtime script prefix), URLs built from settings.STATIC_URL or settings.MEDIA_URL are not prepended with that runtime prefix. The problem appears in template tags, storage.url(), staticfiles post-processing (hashed/manifest rewrites), the dev static handler, and the helper that builds static URL patterns.
StepsToReproduce: Mount the Django application under a subpath (set a runtime SCRIPT_NAME / script prefix, e.g. '/prefix') via WSGI/ASGI or reverse proxy so incoming requests are '/prefix/...'.
Set settings.STATIC_URL = '/static/' (and/or settings.MEDIA_URL = '/media/').
In a template, use {% load static %} and render {% static 'css/foo.css' %}; or call staticfiles_storage.url('css/foo.css') / default_storage.url('css/foo.css').
Use ManifestStaticFilesStorage / HashedFilesMixin and run collectstatic (or trigger post-processing that rewrites CSS/JS references).
Run the development server (DEBUG=True) so django.conf.urls.static.static() and StaticFilesHandler are involved, then request the page at the mounted subpath (e.g. GET /prefix/...).
Observe generated URLs and dev-server responses for static/media assets.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: All user-visible static and media URLs include the runtime script prefix when the app is mounted under a subpath: e.g. URLs rendered by {% static %}, storage.url(), staticfiles post-processing, and the development static handler should be '/prefix/static/...', '/prefix/media/...', etc., matching request paths. Dev server static URL patterns should match and serve these prefixed requests. Post-processing should correctly detect and rewrite prefixed references.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Templates and APIs that use settings.STATIC_URL or settings.MEDIA_URL directly produce unprefixed URLs (e.g. '/static/...'), storage.url() returns URLs missing SCRIPT_NAME, HashedFilesMixin fails to match and rewrite CSS/JS references when runtime URLs are prefixed, the development static handler and urlpatterns built by static() do not match requests under the script prefix and therefore do not serve static files when the app is mounted under a subpath.

hints_text:
This change doesn't seem correct to me (for one, it seems like it could break existing sites). Why not include the appropriate prefix in your STATIC_URL and MEDIA_URL settings?
This is not a patch. This is just an idea I got about the patch for {% static %} only. The patch will (probably) involve FileSystemStorage and StaticFileSystemStorage classes. The main idea behind this feature was that Django will auto detect script_name header and use that accordingly for creating static and media urls. This will reduce human efforts for setting up sites in future. This patch will also take time to develop so it can be added in Django2.0 timeline.
What I meant was that I don't think Django should automatically use SCRIPT_NAME in generating those URLs. If you're running your site on a subpath, then you should set your STATIC_URL to '​http://example.com/subpath/static/' or whatever. However, you might not even be hosting static and uploaded files on the same domain as your site (in fact, for user-uploaded files, you shouldn't do that ​for security reasons) in which case SCRIPT_URL is irrelevant in constructing the static/media URLs. How would the change make it easier to setup sites?
I think that the idea basically makes sense. Ideally, a Django instance shouldn't need to know at which subpath it is being deployed, as this can be considered as purely sysadmin stuff. It would be a good separation of concerns. For example, the Web administrator may change the WSGIScriptAlias from /foo to /bar and the application should continue working. Of course, this only applies when *_URL settings are not full URIs. In practice, it's very likely that many running instances are adapting their *_URL settings to include the base script path, hence the behavior change would be backwards incompatible. The question is whether the change is worth the incompatibility.
I see. I guess the idea would be to use get_script_prefix() like reverse() does as I don't think we have access to request everywhere we need it. It seems like some public APIs like get_static_url() and get_media_url() would replace accessing the settings directly whenever building URLs. For backwards compatibility, possibly these functions could try to detect if the setting is already prefixed appropriately. Removing the prefix from the settings, however, means that the URLs are no longer correct when generated outside of a request/response cycle though (#16734). I'm not sure if it might create any practical problems, but we might think about addressing that issue first.
I'm here at DjangoCon US 2016 will try to create a patch for this ticket ;) Why? But before I make the patch, here are some reasons to do it. The first reason is consistency inside Django core: {% url '...' %} template tag does respect SCRIPT_NAME but {% static '...' %} does not reverse(...) function does respect SCRIPT_NAME but static(...) does not And the second reason is that there is no way to make it work in case when SCRIPT_NAME is a dynamic value - see an example below. Of course we shouldn't modify STATIC_URL when it's an absolute URL, with domain & protocol. But if it starts with / - it's relative to our Django project and we need to add SCRIPT_NAME prefix. Real life example You have Django running via WSGI behind reverse proxy (let's call it back-end server), and another HTTP server on the front (let's call it front-end server). Front-end server URL is http://some.domain.com/sub/path/, back-end server URL is http://1.2.3.4:5678/. You want them both to work. You pass SCRIPT_NAME = '/sub/path/' from front-end server to back-end one. But when you access back-end server directly - there is no SCRIPT_NAME passed to WSGI/Django. So we cannot hard-code SCRIPT_NAME in Django settings because it's dynamic.
Pull-request created: ​https://github.com/django/django/pull/7000
At least documentation and additional tests look like they are required.
Absolutely agree with your remarks, Tim. I'll add tests. Could you point to docs that need to be updated?
I would like to take this ticket on and have a new PR for it: ​https://github.com/django/django/pull/10724

problem_statement.Title: Autoreloader intermittently crashes with ValueError: embedded null byte during Path.resolve()
problem_statement.Description: Django's development auto-reloader (StatReloader / WatchmanReloader) intermittently raises ValueError: embedded null byte while resolving filesystem paths, causing the reloader to crash during startup/file scanning. The exception originates from pathlib/os.readlink when resolving module files or sys.path entries.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Assumption: Path.resolve(strict=True).absolute() only raises FileNotFoundError for missing/unresolvable paths. Location: django/utils/autoreload.py — iter_modules_and_files (calls path.resolve(strict=True).absolute()) where only FileNotFoundError is caught.

2) Assumption: Same as (1) for sys.path entries. Location: django/utils/autoreload.py — sys_path_directories (calls Path(path).resolve(strict=True).absolute()) where only FileNotFoundError is caught.

3) Origin of the unexpected exception: stdlib pathlib.resolve can propagate ValueError raised by os.readlink when a symlink target contains an embedded NUL byte. Location: Python stdlib pathlib (pathlib.py -> _resolve -> accessor.readlink -> os.readlink), as shown in the traceback (ValueError: embedded null byte).

4) Wider impact: Both reloaders use the same resolution paths, so the unhandled ValueError in the above locations affects BaseReloader.watched_files, StatReloader.snapshot_files and WatchmanReloader._update_watches (all call iter_all_python_module_files / iter_modules_and_files and sys_path_directories), allowing the exception to terminate the autoreloader.

5) Investigation/robustness gaps: The code assumes FileNotFoundError is the only relevant resolve-time exception and does not account for other filesystem errors (e.g. ValueError from os.readlink, potentially other OSErrors). There is no debug logging or tests reported that would make identifying the offending path easier.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Starting the development server with the autoreloader enabled should not crash because of an unresolvable or malformed filesystem entry. The autoreloader should continue and monitor other files; user-visible behavior is that the server stays running and file-change detection continues to work for valid paths.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Occasionally, during startup/file scanning, the autoreloader raises an uncaught ValueError: embedded null byte originating from pathlib/os.readlink while resolving a path (traceback shows django/utils/autoreload.py -> path.resolve().absolute() -> pathlib -> os.readlink), which crashes the autoreloader and stops the development server.

hints_text:
Thanks for the report, however as you've admitted there is too many unknowns to accept this ticket. I don't believe that it is related with pathlib, maybe samba connection is unstable it's hard to tell.
I don't believe that it is related with pathlib Well ... it definitely is, you can see that from the stacktrace. The difference between 2.2 and 2.1 (and every version prior) for the purposes of this report is that AFAIK 2.2 is using pathlib.resolve() which deals with symlinks where under <2.2 I don't think the equivalent (os.path.realpath rather than os.path.abspath) was used. But yes, there's no path forward to fix the ticket as it stands, short of not using pathlib (or at least .resolve()).
Hey Keryn, Have you tried removing resolve() yourself, and did it fix the issue? I chose to use resolve() to try and work around a corner case with symlinks, and to generally just normalize the paths to prevent duplication. Also, regarding your comment above, you would need to use print(repr(path)), as I think the print machinery stops at the first null byte found (hence just /Users, which should never be monitored by itself). If you can provide me some more information I'm more than willing to look into this, or consider removing the resolve() call.
Replying to Tom Forbes: Hey Keryn, Have you tried removing resolve() yourself, and did it fix the issue? I chose to use resolve() to try and work around a corner case with symlinks, and to generally just normalize the paths to prevent duplication. Also, regarding your comment above, you would need to use print(repr(path)), as I think the print machinery stops at the first null byte found (hence just /Users, which should never be monitored by itself). If you can provide me some more information I'm more than willing to look into this, or consider removing the resolve() call. Hi Tom, I am also getting this error, see here for the stackoverflow question which I have attempted to answer: ​https://stackoverflow.com/questions/56406965/django-valueerror-embedded-null-byte/56685648#56685648 What is really odd is that it doesn't error every time and looks to error on a random file each time. I believe the issue is caused by having a venv within the top level directory but might be wrong. Bug is on all versions of django >= 2.2.0
Felix, I'm going to re-open this ticket if that's OK. While this is clearly something "funky" going on at a lower level than we handle, it used to work (at least, the error was swallowed). I think this is a fairly simple fix.

problem_statement.Title: Debug 404 renderer crashes when a path converter raises Http404 during URL resolution (DEBUG=True)
problem_statement.Description: When a path converter's to_python raises Http404 during URL resolution, Django's debug 404 renderer (technical_404_response) errors while building the technical 404 page. Instead of the detailed debug 404, the user sees a generic server error message.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/views/debug.py:technical_404_response — incorrect assumption that resolve(request.path) can only raise Resolver404; the method only catches Resolver404 so an Http404 raised by resolve() (for example, from a converter) propagates and breaks debug-page generation. Relationship: technical_404_response currently treats resolve() raising Resolver404 specially but does not treat resolve() raising Http404 equivalently when building the debug 404.

2) django/urls/resolvers.py:RoutePattern.match — encodes the convention that converters signal a pattern "no match" by raising ValueError and only catches ValueError; it does not catch Http404. As a result, a converter raising Http404 is intentionally treated as a genuine Http404 and allowed to propagate instead of being converted into a non-match.

3) django/urls/resolvers.py:URLResolver.resolve — resolution and pattern-matching logic let non-Resolver exceptions (including Http404) propagate rather than converting them to Resolver404; this behavior causes resolve(request.path) to raise Http404 when a converter raises Http404.

4) django/core/handlers/exception.py:response_for_exception — when settings.DEBUG is True, Http404 is routed to debug.technical_404_response. Because response_for_exception delegates Http404 handling to technical_404_response, the debug renderer must be robust to resolve() re-raising Http404 during its own attempt to introspect the request path.
StepsToReproduce: Set DEBUG = True in Django settings.
Implement a path converter whose to_python(...) raises django.http.Http404 for certain input values.
Add a URL pattern that uses this converter so that resolving a specific request path invokes the converter.
Request the URL that causes the converter to raise Http404.
Observe the response returned by the server.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When DEBUG is True and Http404 is raised during URL resolution (for example inside a path converter), Django should render the technical 404 debug page with detailed diagnostic information and traceback.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The debug 404 renderer fails while building the technical 404 page and the client receives a generic server error (plain text: "A server error occurred. Please contact the administrator.") instead of the technical 404 debug page.

hints_text:
It seems that other exceptions correctly result in a technical 500 response.
The technical_404_response view performs a new URL resolving (cf ​https://github.com/django/django/blob/a8e492bc81fca829f5d270e2d57703c02e58701e/django/views/debug.py#L482) which will obviously raise a new Http404 which won't be caught as only Resolver404 is checked. That means the WSGI handler fails and the WSGI server returns the previously described default error message (indeed the error message is the default one from wsgiref.handlers.BaseHandler ​https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/wsgiref.html#wsgiref.handlers.BaseHandler.error_body). The solution seems to be to catch Http404 instead of Resolver404 in technical_404_response. This will result in a technical 404 page with the Http404's message displayed and will match the behaviour of when DEBUG is False.
Created ​PR , but I am not sure how to write the tests. I've looking about the response before and after catch Http404 instead of Resolver404, and there is no difference. Should I also change the technical_404.html for response?
I've added test to the patch, but not sure if it is correct.
I have made the requested changes; please review again

problem_statement.Title: models.E028 raised when different apps/models use the same db_table name in multi-DB setups (router ignored)
problem_statement.Description: Django system checks raise models.E028 complaining that db_table 'X' is used by multiple models when two models (in different apps) share the same db_table name even though they are routed to different database aliases.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Global db_table uniqueness assumption in django/core/checks/model_checks.py::check_all_models: the function builds a single global mapping with db_table_models = defaultdict(list), does db_table_models[model._meta.db_table].append(model._meta.label), then emits models.E028 when len(model_labels) != 1. This enforces global uniqueness of db_table names across all models regardless of which database each model is routed to.

2) Index/constraint checks share the same global-uniqueness assumption in django/core/checks/model_checks.py: indexes = defaultdict(list) and constraints = defaultdict(list) are collected and checked globally, causing analogous false positives for index and constraint names across multiple databases.

3) The system check ignores the router-based migration decision source: migrations and creation code use django/db/utils.ConnectionRouter.allow_migrate_model(db_alias, model) (router exposed in django/db/__init__.py) to decide the target DB for a model; check_all_models does not consult router.allow_migrate_model and therefore cannot tell when identical names are scoped to different DB aliases.

4) Options.can_migrate is insufficient to substitute for router decisions: django/db/models/options.py::Options.can_migrate(connection) only checks managed/proxy/vendor/features and does NOT consult database routers, so relying on it would not reflect the actual per-db placement determined by router.allow_migrate_model.

5) Correct grouping relationship (as identified by the investigation) is database-aware grouping: instead of db_table_models = defaultdict(list) use a per-db grouping such as db_table_models = defaultdict(lambda: defaultdict(list)) and include a model in db_table_models[model._meta.db_table][db_alias] only if router.allow_migrate_model(db_alias, model). The same per-db grouping/formula must apply to indexes and constraints.
StepsToReproduce: Create two Django apps (e.g. base and app2) each defining a model with the same db_table name (or set db_table explicitly to the same string).
Configure DATABASES with at least two aliases (e.g. 'default' and 'other') and implement a DB router that routes the models from base to one alias and models from app2 to the other alias.
Upgrade to Django 2.2 (or run with the Django version that contains the reported check behavior).
Run Django system checks (e.g. python manage.py check) or run migrations.
Observe models.E028: "db_table 'table_name' is used by multiple models: base.ModelName, app2.ModelName."
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: System checks should not report models.E028 (or equivalent index/constraint-name duplicate errors) when two models share the same db_table/index/constraint name but are routed to different database aliases. Duplicate-name errors should only be reported when the router and migration logic indicate the models will be migrated to the same database alias.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Django raises models.E028 reporting that the same db_table name is used by multiple models (listing both model labels) even though the project's DB router places those models on different database aliases, producing a false-positive error in multi-database setups.

problem_statement.Title: No system check that Field.max_length >= length of longest Field.choices value
problem_statement.Description: Field.choice values can be longer than a Field's max_length without triggering a system check. The omission is visible during model system checks and results in the mismatch only being discovered later when saving a record that uses a too-long choice value.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Field._check_choices (django/db/models/fields/__init__.py::Field._check_choices) assumes validating the shape of self.choices is sufficient and does not compare stored choice keys against getattr(self, 'max_length', None). It omits any check that longest_choice_length <= self.max_length. The investigation identifies the exact relationship that is missing: longest = max(len(choice) for choice, _ in self.flatchoices if isinstance(choice, str)) and, when isinstance(self.max_length, int), the condition longest > self.max_length should be surfaced as a system check error (proposal used id='fields.E122'). The current method contains no code implementing this relationship.  

2) CharField.__init__ and CharField._check_max_length_attribute (django/db/models/fields/__init__.py::CharField.__init__ and ::CharField._check_max_length_attribute) enforce presence/positivity of max_length and add a MaxLengthValidator for runtime/form validation, but they encode the incorrect assumption that those checks suffice and do not verify compatibility between max_length and declared choices. This omission leaves a gap where choices longer than max_length are not caught until save-time.  

3) Field._get_flatchoices / Field.flatchoices (django/db/models/fields/__init__.py::Field._get_flatchoices) is the canonical source for iterating all stored choice keys (including optgroups). The codebase contains this asset but no consumer currently uses it to compute choice key lengths for a max_length compatibility check; the missing consumer is effectively the absent length comparison in Field._check_choices. The exact recommended iteration expression is: longest = max(len(choice) for choice, _ in self.flatchoices if isinstance(choice, str)).  

4) Lazy translation Promises: the investigation found the proposed length check decision currently skips choice keys that are lazy translation Promises (isinstance(choice, Promise)). This encodes the assumption that Promises should not be forced during system checks. As a result, choices provided as lazy strings will not be measured and may evade the length check, meaning some i18n usages remain unchecked unless Promise handling is changed.  

5) Scope: the missing cross-check is a Field-level omission that affects all field subclasses that expose a max_length attribute (e.g., CharField, EmailField, URLField, FilePathField, GenericIPAddressField, UUIDField). Several subclasses define default max_length values, but those defaults do not replace the need to verify user-supplied choices against the effective max_length.
StepsToReproduce: Define a model with a field that has a max_length smaller than one of its declared choice values (for example, CharField(max_length=2, choices=[('toolong', 'Toolong')] )).
Run the Django system checks (e.g. python manage.py check) and observe that no system check error is reported about choices exceeding max_length.
Create and save a model instance that uses the too-long choice value and observe that the problem is only detected at save-time (the save fails or raises a database/validation error).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The system check step (manage.py check / model checks) should report an explicit error when any stored choice key length exceeds the field's max_length, so developers see and fix the mismatch before runtime/save-time.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: No system check warns when a choice value's stored length is greater than the field's max_length. The incompatibility is only noticed later when attempting to save a record using the too-long choice value.

problem_statement.Title: Filtering on query result overrides GROUP BY of internal query
problem_statement.Description: When a grouped Query (e.g. values('email').annotate(Max('id'))) is used as a RHS subquery in a lookup (for example filter(id=a[:1])), the ORM mutates the original Query's SELECT list to force the pk/id column. That mutation changes the compiled subquery's GROUP BY clause (becoming GROUP BY id) instead of preserving the original GROUP BY (email). This occurs in Django's ORM SQL generation paths.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption: it's acceptable to mutate a user-supplied Query in-place when preparing it for use as a subquery/lookup. Location: django/db/models/sql/where.py: SubqueryConstraint.as_sql. Evidence: as_sql calls query.set_values(self.targets) on the original query_object.

2) Incorrect assumption: callers of Query.set_values expect in-place mutation of select/values/group_by is harmless. Location: django/db/models/sql/query.py: Query.set_values. Relationship/effect: set_values performs clear_select_fields() and add_fields(field_names) and (when group_by=True) adds concrete fields and calls set_group_by(), thereby changing query.select and query.group_by.

3) Incorrect assumption: forcing a SELECT list for a subquery on the original Query will not affect GROUP BY. Location: django/db/models/sql/compiler.py: SQLCompiler.get_group_by. Precise relationship: the compiler computes GROUP BY from query.group_by and from the current select list by iterating over select expressions and calling expr.get_group_by_cols(); therefore changing query.select changes the derived GROUP BY. (Conceptual formula: GROUP_BY_output = query.group_by ∪ ⋃_{expr ∈ query.select} expr.get_group_by_cols()).

4) Incorrect assumption: it's acceptable for lookup processing to clear and add SELECT columns on the original rhs Query. Locations: django/db/models/lookups.py: Exact.process_rhs and In.process_rhs. Evidence: these call rhs.clear_select_clause() and rhs.add_fields(['pk']) when rhs.has_limit_one() or similar, mutating the original Query and causing the GROUP BY loss seen in the repro.

5) Incorrect assumption: related-lookup single-column path can mutate rhs Query in-place. Location: django/db/models/fields/related_lookups.py: RelatedIn.as_sql (single-column branch). Evidence: this code path calls self.rhs.clear_select_clause() and self.rhs.add_fields([target_field]) on the original rhs Query, altering its select and thereby its GROUP BY when compiled as a subquery.

6) Related/muted-intent site: some code intentionally mutates inner queries for delete semantics. Location: django/db/models/sql/subqueries.py: DeleteQuery.delete_qs. Evidence: delete_qs clears and sets innerq.select to pk column for deletion; this mutation is intentional for delete flows and was noted as excluded from the immediate remediation but is a completeness consideration.

7) Practical fix pattern (investigative finding): callers that currently mutate Query.select should clone the Query before calling set_values()/clear_select_clause()/add_fields(), e.g. q = original.clone(); q.set_values(...); use q for compilation. This is a diagnostic finding (why mutation causes the bug) rather than a prescriptive code change in this report.
StepsToReproduce: In Django shell or a test environment, import django.contrib.auth.models.User as User.
Create a grouped Query: a = User.objects.filter(email__isnull=True).values('email').annotate(m=Max('id')).values('m')
Print the SQL for a: print(a.query) — observe GROUP BY "auth_user"."email".
Create a limited subquery slice: s = a[:1]; print(s.query) — observe LIMIT 1 with GROUP BY preserved.
Use the slice as a lookup RHS: b = User.objects.filter(id=s); print(b.query).
Observe that the inner subquery now contains GROUP BY on the pk/id (e.g. GROUP BY U0."id") instead of GROUP BY U0."email".
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Using a grouped Query as a subquery or lookup RHS must not mutate the original Query object's SELECT or GROUP BY. The compiled subquery should preserve the grouping column(s) (e.g. GROUP BY email) that were present on the original Query, while still producing a syntactically-correct subquery for the lookup.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The ORM mutates the original Query's SELECT list to force the pk/id column when preparing the subquery, which changes the compiled subquery's GROUP BY to use the pk/id (GROUP BY id) instead of the original grouping column (email). The original Query no longer compiles to the expected GROUP BY clause after being used as a RHS.

Workaround: from django.contrib.auth import models a = models.User.objects.filter(email__isnull=True).values('email').aggregate(Max('id'))['id_max'] b = models.User.objects.filter(id=a)
Thanks for tackling that one James! If I can provide you some guidance I'd suggest you have a look at lookups.Exact.process_rhs ​https://github.com/django/django/blob/ea25bdc2b94466bb1563000bf81628dea4d80612/django/db/models/lookups.py#L265-L267 We probably don't want to perform the clear_select_clause and add_fields(['pk']) when the query is already selecting fields. That's exactly what In.process_rhs ​does already by only performing these operations if not getattr(self.rhs, 'has_select_fields', True).
Thanks so much for the help Simon! This is a great jumping-off point. There's something that I'm unclear about, which perhaps you can shed some light on. While I was able to replicate the bug with 2.2, when I try to create a test on Master to validate the bug, the group-by behavior seems to have changed. Here's the test that I created: def test_exact_selected_field_rhs_subquery(self): author_1 = Author.objects.create(name='one') author_2 = Author.objects.create(name='two') max_ids = Author.objects.filter(alias__isnull=True).values('alias').annotate(m=Max('id')).values('m') authors = Author.objects.filter(id=max_ids[:1]) self.assertFalse(str(max_ids.query)) # This was just to force the test-runner to output the query. self.assertEqual(authors[0], author_2) And here's the resulting query: SELECT MAX("lookup_author"."id") AS "m" FROM "lookup_author" WHERE "lookup_author"."alias" IS NULL GROUP BY "lookup_author"."alias", "lookup_author"."name" It no longer appears to be grouping by the 'alias' field listed in the initial .values() preceeding the .annotate(). I looked at the docs and release notes to see if there was a behavior change, but didn't see anything listed. Do you know if I'm just misunderstanding what's happening here? Or does this seem like a possible regression?
It's possible that a regression was introduced in between. Could you try bisecting the commit that changed the behavior ​https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/dev/internals/contributing/triaging-tickets/#bisecting-a-regression
Mmm actually disregard that. The second value in the GROUP BY is due to the ordering value in the Author class's Meta class. class Author(models.Model): name = models.CharField(max_length=100) alias = models.CharField(max_length=50, null=True, blank=True) class Meta: ordering = ('name',) Regarding the bug in question in this ticket, what should the desired behavior be if the inner query is returning multiple fields? With the fix, which allows the inner query to define a field to return/group by, if there are multiple fields used then it will throw a sqlite3.OperationalError: row value misused. Is this the desired behavior or should it avoid this problem by defaulting back to pk if more than one field is selected?
I think that we should only default to pk if no fields are selected. The ORM has preliminary support for multi-column lookups and other interface dealing with subqueries doesn't prevent passing queries with multiple fields so I'd stick to the current __in lookup behavior.

problem_statement.Title: Migrations serialize Enum members by .value (coercing gettext_lazy) causing translation-dependent migration failures
problem_statement.Description: When an Enum member that uses gettext_lazy (or another runtime-evaluated value) is used as a default for a CharField, the migration writer serializes the enum member by its .value. The serializer coerces the Promise to a string at migration-generation time and embeds a language-specific literal into the migration file. Later, when translations change, importing that migration raises a ValueError because the literal no longer matches any Enum member.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Wrong assumption: EnumSerializer assumes an enum member's .value is a stable canonical reconstruction key. Location: django/db/migrations/serializer.py, class EnumSerializer, method serialize. Evidence/relationship: EnumSerializer calls serializer_factory(self.value.value) and emits a constructor-like form Module.EnumClass(<serialized value>) (i.e. returns "%s.%s(%s)" built from module, enum class name, and v_string). This is only correct if the .value is a stable identifier; it is not correct when .value is a gettext_lazy Promise or otherwise runtime-evaluated.

2) Wrong assumption: serializer_factory treats a top-level Promise as safely serializable by coercing it to str immediately. Location: django/db/migrations/serializer.py, function serializer_factory. Evidence/relationship: serializer_factory does 'if isinstance(value, Promise): value = str(value)', which converts gettext_lazy Promises to translated strings during migration generation, leaking translation state into generated source.

3) Wrong assumption: migration serialization should embed imports required to reconstruct the enum member's value (v_imports) rather than relying on importing the enum module. Location: django/db/migrations/serializer.py, EnumSerializer imports construction. Evidence/relationship: old code merged v_imports into imports (imports = {'import %s' % module, *v_imports}); because EnumSerializer serialized the .value, it included v_imports. This assumption couples the migration's correctness to textual reconstruction of the value rather than referencing the enum member by attribute.

4) Wrong assumption encoded in tests: unit tests expect the constructor-like serialized form for enum members and field defaults. Location: tests/migrations/test_writer.py. Evidence/relationship: tests assert outputs like "migrations.test_writer.TextEnum('a-value')" and field defaults like "default=migrations.test_writer.TextEnum('value-b')", reinforcing the incorrect serialization and masking the bug in CI.

5) Correct relationship (diagnostic conclusion based on findings): enum members should be serialized by attribute/name so the migration refers to the member itself rather than its .value. Expressed relationship: migration text should reference Module.EnumClass.MEMBER (e.g. "%s.%s.%s" using module, enum class name, and self.value.name) and imports should include at minimum 'import <module>' so the enum module is imported at runtime to resolve the member. This avoids coercing Promise -> str at migration-generation time.
StepsToReproduce: Define an Enum whose member values use gettext_lazy (from django.utils.translation import gettext_lazy as _) and implement __str__ to return the member name.
Use an Enum member as the default for a CharField in a model (e.g., status = models.CharField(default=Status.GOOD, max_length=128)).
Run manage.py makemigrations to generate a migration that includes the field default.
Switch the active language (or ensure gettext_lazy translates the value) so the Enum member's .value becomes a different, translated string.
Run manage.py migrate (or import the generated migration module). Observe the import-time error: ValueError: '<translated-string>' is not a valid <EnumClass>.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Generated migration files should reference enum members in a language- and runtime-stable way (by attribute/name) so migrations remain importable regardless of translation state. A migration created when the project language is X should still import and run correctly when the project language is Y.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Migration files embed the enum member's .value (coerced to a string at generation time), producing code like Module.EnumClass('translated-literal'). When translations change, importing the migration raises ValueError because the literal no longer matches any enum member.

hints_text:
Thanks for this report, however I'm not sure how translated values can brake migrations. Can you provide a sample project to reproduce this issue? Migrations with translatable strings works fine for me: >>> class TextEnum(enum.Enum): ... C = _('translatable value') ... >>> TextEnum(_('translatable value')) <TextEnum.C: 'translatable value'> >>> TextEnum('translatable value') <TextEnum.C: 'translatable value'>
To experience the bug: In any Django project, set the default value of a CharField as an enum object: class EnumClass(Enum): VALUE = _('Value') where: VALUE: is the constant enum object name 'Value': is the translatable enum object value In the model: field = models.CharField(default=EnumClass.VALUE, max_length=128) then run: python manage.py makemigrations In the generated migration file, you will notice that the default value of the field is set to: EnumClass('Value'), so it calls the enum object by its translatable value not it is constant name. (This is exactly the BUG, you can think of it without even continue) run: python manage.py migrate In the settings.py file: LANGUAGE_CODE = 'fr-FR' # set it to any language code other than English Run the project after generating, translating, and compiling the messages file (see: ​message-files) The project will raise the error: ValueError: 'Value' is not a valid EnumClass , on the generated migration file.
This use case looks quite niche for me, i.e. I would expect to store a unified values (the same for all languages) and translate only labels visible for users, however I agree that we can fix this.
Here is the diff based on the @oasl solution Shouldn't the code generated by the migration uses the name of the Status Enum 'GOOD', not the value of it, since it is changeable? It should be: ('status', models.CharField(default=Status['GOOD'], max_length=128)) diff --git a/django/db/migrations/serializer.py b/django/db/migrations/serializer.py index 27b5cbd379..b00c6f0df2 100644 --- a/django/db/migrations/serializer.py +++ b/django/db/migrations/serializer.py @@ -120,9 +120,9 @@ class EnumSerializer(BaseSerializer): def serialize(self): enum_class = self.value.__class__ module = enum_class.__module__ - v_string, v_imports = serializer_factory(self.value.value).serialize() + _, v_imports = serializer_factory(self.value.value).serialize() imports = {'import %s' % module, *v_imports} - return "%s.%s(%s)" % (module, enum_class.__name__, v_string), imports + return "%s.%s['%s']" % (module, enum_class.__name__, self.value), imports @felixxm, what do you think?
You cannot use a string representation of self.value i.e. 'EnumClass.GOOD', IMO we should use a name property: return "%s.%s[%r]" % (module, enum_class.__name__, self.value.name), imports

problem_statement.Title: parse_http_date uses a fixed two-digit-year pivot instead of RFC 7231 50-year rule
problem_statement.Description: django.utils.http.parse_http_date interprets two-digit years using a hard-coded pivot (0–69 → 2000–2069, 70–99 → 1970–1999) rather than the RFC 7231 requirement to interpret a two-digit year relative to the current UTC year and treat candidates more than 50 years in the future as belonging to the previous century.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Hard-coded two-digit-year pivot in django/utils/http.py::parse_http_date — the code assumes: if year < 100: if year < 70: year += 2000 else: year += 1900. This encodes the incorrect static mapping (0–69 → 2000–2069; 70–99 → 1970–1999).

2. Test enforces the wrong assumption in tests/utils_tests/test_http.py::test_parsing_year_less_than_70 — the test asserts parse_http_date('Sun Nov  6 08:49:37 0037') yields year 2037, thereby encoding and protecting the static pivot in CI.

3. Literal constants (70, 1900, 2000) present in parse_http_date encode a fixed pivot rather than computing the year relative to the current time; these literals are incorrect because the correct interpretation depends on the current UTC year.

4. Call sites that consume parse_http_date (observed: django/middleware/http.py, django/utils/cache.py, django/views/static.py) will observe different parsed timestamps for two-digit year inputs when the parsing behavior is changed; write-side http_date is unaffected.

5. The correct relationship (RFC 7231) that should govern two-digit-year interpretation is time-relative and must be computed (exact formula found during investigation):
   now = datetime.datetime.utcnow().year
   century = now - (now % 100)
   year = century + year
   if year - now > 50:
       year -= 100
   This formula describes how a two-digit year should be converted to a four-digit year instead of using fixed constants.

6. Test and investigation omissions that affect confidence in change: tests relying on parse_http_date do not mock/freeze datetime.utcnow() (making time-relative behavior non-deterministic in tests), and the investigation did not search globally for other occurrences of the century/pivot literals or verify whether external systems depend on the old static mapping. These omissions do not change the root cause but identify risks for rollout.
StepsToReproduce: In a Django environment, open a Python shell and run: from django.utils.http import parse_http_date
Call parse_http_date('Sun Nov  6 08:49:37 0037') and observe the parsed timestamp's year (e.g., datetime.utcfromtimestamp(parsed))
Run the unit test: tests/utils_tests/test_http.py::test_parsing_year_less_than_70 and observe the expected-year assertion
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When given an rfc850-date with a two-digit year, the parser interprets the year relative to the current UTC year per RFC 7231: compute the candidate four-digit year from the current century and the two-digit value, and if that candidate would be more than 50 years in the future, interpret it as the same two-digit year in the previous century. User-visible timestamps should reflect that time-relative interpretation.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Two-digit years are interpreted using a static pivot: years 0–69 are mapped to 2000–2069 and years 70–99 to 1970–1999. A unit test currently asserts the static mapping (e.g., '0037' → 2037), enforcing the incorrect behavior.

problem_statement.Title: isnull lookup accepts non-boolean RHS causing join-promotion mismatch and incorrect relation queries
problem_statement.Description: The __isnull lookup accepts non-boolean truthy/falsy RHS values (e.g. 1, "True") and uses Python truthiness when generating SQL, while the query builder promotes INNER → OUTER joins only when the RHS is the literal boolean True. This mismatch lets non-boolean truthy RHS produce IS NULL SQL without the required OUTER join for relation lookups, causing incorrect query results.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Incorrect truthiness check in SQL generation: django/db/models/lookups.py::IsNull.as_sql uses a truthiness test (if self.rhs) to choose between "IS NULL" and "IS NOT NULL", which accepts non-boolean direct RHS values (ints, strings) as valid inputs.

2. Strict identity check for join promotion: django/db/models/sql/query.py uses the identity test "condition.rhs is True" when computing require_outer (require_outer = lookup_type == 'isnull' and condition.rhs is True and not current_negated). This assumes condition.rhs is the literal boolean True to trigger OUTER join promotion.

3. Mismatch between (1) and (2): Because IsNull.as_sql treats any truthy RHS as True but the query builder requires the RHS object to be the boolean literal True (is True), truthy non-boolean RHS values produce "IS NULL" SQL without triggering OUTER join promotion, leading to incorrect results on relation lookups.

4. Lookup preparation left unchanged: django/db/models/lookups.py::IsNull sets prepare_rhs = False and does not override get_prep_lookup, so direct RHS values are left as originally passed (implicit allowance of non-bools), enabling the mismatch above.

5. None and expression exceptions to validate: Some lookups/subclasses set can_use_none_as_rhs = True (tests/lookup/models.py:IsNullWithNoneAsRHS) and expression RHS values (resolve_expression) are used in some call sites; any validation must allow None when permitted and allow expression objects to pass unchanged.

6. Admin parsing already coerces booleans: django/contrib/admin/utils.py parses URL params for "__isnull" into booleans, so admin-generated filters already supply True/False strings converted to booleans and are not the source of the bug. This means admin behavior is compatible with requiring boolean RHS.
StepsToReproduce: In a Django project with a relation lookup (e.g. a model with a ForeignKey), open the shell (manage.py shell).
Run a queryset using a non-boolean truthy RHS for __isnull, for example: Model.objects.filter(related_field__isnull=1) (or related_field__isnull='True'). Inspect the generated SQL and result set.

Compare with the boolean case: run Model.objects.filter(related_field__isnull=True), inspect the SQL and result set. Observe that the non-boolean truthy RHS generates IS NULL SQL but does not cause the same join promotion (INNER → OUTER) as the literal True case, leading to different/incorrect results.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Using __isnull in filters should accept only boolean True/False (and None when explicitly allowed by the lookup, or expression objects), and non-boolean direct RHS values should not be silently accepted. When RHS is the boolean True, relation lookups that require an OUTER join should promote the join so query results include rows with NULL related values; when RHS is False, the join behavior should remain consistent with filtering for NOT NULL.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Currently __isnull accepts non-boolean truthy/falsy RHS. IsNull.as_sql uses truthiness (if self.rhs) to emit "IS NULL" or "IS NOT NULL" for such RHS values, but the query builder only promotes to an OUTER join when condition.rhs is the boolean literal True (identity check). As a result, truthy non-boolean RHS (e.g. 1, "True") can produce "IS NULL" SQL without the OUTER join promotion, causing incorrect query results for relation lookups.

PR here: ​https://github.com/django/django/pull/11873
After the reconsideration I don't think that we should change this ​documented behavior (that is in Django from the very beginning). __isnull lookup expects boolean values in many places and IMO it would be confusing if we'll allow for truthy/falsy values, e.g. take a look at these examples field__isnull='false' or field__isnull='true' (both would return the same result). You can always call bool() on a right hand side. Sorry for my previous acceptation (I shouldn't triage tickets in the weekend).
Replying to felixxm: After the reconsideration I don't think that we should change this ​documented behavior (that is in Django from the very beginning). __isnull lookup expects boolean values in many places and IMO it would be confusing if we'll allow for truthy/falsy values, e.g. take a look at these examples field__isnull='false' or field__isnull='true' (both would return the same result). You can always call bool() on a right hand side. Sorry for my previous acceptation (I shouldn't triage tickets in the weekend). I understand your point. But is there anything we can do to avoid people falling for the same pitfall I did? The problem, in my opinion, is that it works fine for simple queries but as soon as you add a join that needs promotion it will break, silently. Maybe we should make it raise an exception when a non-boolean is passed? One valid example is to have a class that implements __bool__. You can see here ​https://github.com/django/django/blob/d9881a025c15d87b2a7883ee50771117450ea90d/django/db/models/lookups.py#L465-L470 that non-bool value is converted to IS NULL and IS NOT NULL already using the truthy/falsy values. IMO it would be confusing if we'll allow for truthy/falsy values, e.g. take a look at these examples fieldisnull='false' or fieldisnull='true' (both would return the same result). This is already the case. It just is inconsistent, in lookups.py field__isnull='false' will be a positive condition but on the query.py it will be the negative condition.
Maybe adding a note on the documentation? something like: "Although it might seem like it will work with non-bool fields, this is not supported and can lead to inconsistent behaviours"
Agreed, we should raise an error for non-boolean values, e.g. diff --git a/django/db/models/lookups.py b/django/db/models/lookups.py index 9344979c56..fc4a38c4fe 100644 --- a/django/db/models/lookups.py +++ b/django/db/models/lookups.py @@ -463,6 +463,11 @@ class IsNull(BuiltinLookup): prepare_rhs = False def as_sql(self, compiler, connection): + if not isinstance(self.rhs, bool): + raise ValueError( + 'The QuerySet value for an isnull lookup must be True or ' + 'False.' + ) sql, params = compiler.compile(self.lhs) if self.rhs: return "%s IS NULL" % sql, params I changed the ticket description.
Thanks, I'll work on it! Wouldn't that possibly break backward compatibility? I'm not familiar with how Django moves in that regard.
We can add a release note in "Backwards incompatible changes" or deprecate this and remove in Django 4.0. I have to thing about it, please give me a day, maybe I will change my mind :)
No problem. Thanks for taking the time to look into this!

An attempt to write WHERE (field IS NULL) = boolean_field as .filter(field__isnull=F('boolean_field')) didn't go as I expected. problem_statement.Title: makemigrations emits ForeignKey.to_field with old field name when renaming a PrimaryKey
problem_statement.Description: When renaming a primary-key field on a model, makemigrations generates a migration that sets related ForeignKey.to_field to the old field name instead of the new name. The incorrect value appears in the generated migration file.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) MigrationAutodetector.generate_altered_fields mutates live model Field instances (django/db/migrations/autodetector.py :: MigrationAutodetector.generate_altered_fields). The method assigns into the actual new_field used by loaded models: new_field.remote_field.model, new_field.remote_field.field_name, new_field.from_fields, new_field.to_fields, and then calls self._get_dependencies_for_foreign_key(new_field). These in-place mutations persist on the live Field instance.

2) The same generate_altered_fields then serializes the mutated Field instances: it calls old_field_dec = self.deep_deconstruct(old_field) and new_field_dec = self.deep_deconstruct(new_field). Because new_field was mutated in-place, deep_deconstruct(new_field) serializes the stale/old names (e.g., to_field='field_wrong') into the migration file.

3) ForeignKey.deconstruct emits to_field from the live Field attribute remote_field.field_name (django/db/models/fields/related.py :: ForeignKey.deconstruct). The exact relationship is: kwargs['to_field'] = self.remote_field.field_name if self.remote_field.field_name and self.remote_field.field_name != to._meta.pk.name. If remote_field.field_name was mutated to the old name by the autodetector, deconstruct writes that old name into the migration.

4) RenameField.state_forwards is not the source of the bug (django/db/migrations/operations/fields.py :: RenameField.state_forwards). It correctly updates the migration ProjectState remote references (remote_field.field_name and field.to_fields) to the new name; the incorrect value is introduced earlier by mutating the live model Field instances in the autodetector.

5) Field.clone() is not a safe substitute for creating a temporary, live-field-preserving copy (django/db/models/fields/__init__.py :: Field.clone). clone() is implemented via deconstruct() and reconstructing the Field from deconstructed kwargs, which does not preserve live remote_field.model class references; therefore relying on clone() would not preserve the same runtime model references needed by the autodetector inspection.

(Each numbered item names the incorrect assumption and the file/class/method location that encodes it. The critical relationships involved are: the autodetector's in-place mutations of new_field attributes; the subsequent calls deep_deconstruct(new_field) that serialize those mutated attributes; and ForeignKey.deconstruct's use of remote_field.field_name to populate to_field only when remote_field.field_name is set and differs from the target model's pk name.)
StepsToReproduce: Create app models: ModelA with primary_key=True on field_wrong, and ModelB with a ForeignKey to ModelA (no to_field explicitly set).
Run makemigrations and apply migrations (initial state recorded).
Rename ModelA.field_wrong to field_fixed in models.py (field_fixed remains primary_key=True).
Run makemigrations to generate the rename migration.
Inspect the generated migration: observe an AlterField entry for ModelB's ForeignKey containing to_field='field_wrong' (the old name) instead of 'field_fixed'.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: After renaming a primary-key field on ModelA, generated migrations should reference the new field name (e.g., to_field='field_fixed') for any ForeignKey that targets that field. Migration files should not contain stale/old field names for to_field.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: makemigrations generates migrations in which ForeignKey.to_field is set to the old field name (e.g., 'field_wrong'), because the autodetector mutated live Field instances and those mutated attributes were serialized into the migration file.

problem_statement.Title: Inconsistent types for Choice fields: enum member stored on assignment but primitive on DB load
problem_statement.Description: Assigning a TextChoices/IntegerChoices enum member to a model field leaves the enum.Enum instance on the freshly-created model instance, while loading the same field from the database returns the underlying primitive (str/int). This causes type and string-representation mismatches between newly created and retrieved instances.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Missing assignment normalization in DeferredAttribute (django/db/models/query_utils.py: class DeferredAttribute). The descriptor defines __get__ but no __set__, so setattr(instance, field_attname, value) writes the incoming value verbatim into instance.__dict__ instead of normalizing enum.Enum objects.

2) Assignment must normalize enum.Enum to its primitive value before storing. The relationship identified by the investigation is: if isinstance(value, enum.Enum): value = value.value; then instance.__dict__[self.field.attname] = value. This normalization is required so in-memory assigned values match values loaded from the DB.

3) TextChoices members are both enum.Enum and str subclasses (django/db/models/enums.py: TextChoices defined as class TextChoices(str, Choices)). CharField.to_python (django/db/models/fields/__init__.py:CharField.to_python) treats isinstance(value, str) as already-correct and returns the value unchanged. Because a TextChoices member is a str subclass and an Enum, CharField.to_python returns the enum object unchanged; enum.Enum.__str__ produces 'MyChoice.FIRST_CHOICE' rather than the underlying 'first', explaining the observed wrong string representation.

4) ChoicesMeta membership logic accepts raw primitives by comparing against {x.value for x in cls} (django/db/models/enums.py: ChoicesMeta.__contains__). This aids validation of primitive values but does not affect storage or address the assignment-time normalization problem.

5) Some descriptors already implement their own __set__ (e.g., django/db/models/fields/related_descriptors.py:ForeignKeyDeferredAttribute.__set__ and tests' CustomDeferredAttribute.__set__). These subclasses will continue to control assignment behavior; the missing base-class __set__ is the location-level cause for fields that use the default DeferredAttribute.
StepsToReproduce: Create an instance assigning the enum member: obj = MyObject.objects.create(my_str_value=MyChoice.FIRST_CHOICE)
Assert the in-memory value type and string: isinstance(obj.my_str_value, str) and str(obj.my_str_value) == 'first'
Retrieve the same object from the DB: obj2 = MyObject.objects.last(), then assert isinstance(obj2.my_str_value, str) and str(obj2.my_str_value) == 'first'
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Both newly-created and database-retrieved model instances expose the field value as the underlying primitive (str for TextChoices, int for IntegerChoices). str(field_value) should produce the primitive's string (e.g. 'first'), and isinstance(field_value, str/int) should be True as appropriate, with consistent types between created and retrieved instances.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When assigning a TextChoices/IntegerChoices enum member to a model field, the freshly-created instance holds the enum.Enum object so str(field_value) yields the enum representation (e.g. 'MyChoice.FIRST_CHOICE') and the type may differ from the primitive. Reading the same field from the database returns the primitive (e.g. 'first'), producing a type and representation mismatch between created and retrieved instances.

problem_statement.Title: Field.contribute_to_class unconditionally overwrites user-defined get_<field>_display (Django 2.2+)
problem_statement.Description: Model-defined overrides of get_<field>_display() (for example get_foo_bar_display) are ignored in Django 2.2+. During model class creation generated accessors are installed onto the class after user methods are attached, and the generated methods overwrite any user-defined methods of the same name.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption in django/db/models/fields/__init__.py :: Field.contribute_to_class — the code unconditionally does setattr(cls, 'get_%s_display' % self.name, partialmethod(cls._get_FIELD_display, field=self)). This encodes the assumption that it is always safe to overwrite an existing attribute named get_<field>_display on the model class.  
2) Incorrect assumption in django/db/models/fields/__init__.py :: DateField.contribute_to_class — the code unconditionally does setattr(cls, 'get_next_by_%s' % self.name, ...) and setattr(cls, 'get_previous_by_%s' % self.name, ...). This encodes the assumption that it is always safe to overwrite existing get_next_by_<field> and get_previous_by_<field> methods.  
3) Class-construction ordering in django/db/models/base.py :: ModelBase.__new__/add_to_class (and helper _has_contribute_to_class) results in user-defined methods being attached to the class (via type.__new__ / new_attrs) before calling value.contribute_to_class(cls, name) for field objects. Because the field code performs unconditional setattr after user methods exist, user overrides are overwritten.  
4) Correct relationship required for generated accessors (evidence-derived): the generated accessor named 'get_%s_display' (and 'get_next_by_%s' / 'get_previous_by_%s') corresponds to the field name self.name and therefore should only be installed onto cls when no attribute with that name already exists on cls (i.e., install only if getattr(cls, method_name, None) is None).
StepsToReproduce: Create and save an instance: FooBar.objects.create(foo_bar=1) or instantiate in the shell: FooBar(foo_bar=1).
Call print(str(instance)) or evaluate instance.__str__() / instance.get_foo_bar_display() and observe the returned value.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When a model defines a method named get_<field>_display (e.g. get_foo_bar_display), calls to that method or to code that uses it (such as __str__) should invoke the user-defined method and return its result (e.g. "something").
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: In Django 2.2+, the user-defined get_<field>_display method is ignored and the generated accessor installed by Field.contribute_to_class is called instead, returning the field's choice label (e.g. 'foo' or 'bar') rather than the user method's result.

hints_text:
Thanks for this report. Regression in a68ea231012434b522ce45c513d84add516afa60. Reproduced at 54a7b021125d23a248e70ba17bf8b10bc8619234.
OK, I have a lead on this. Not at all happy about how it looks at first pass, but I'll a proof of concept PR together for it tomorrow AM.
I don't think it should be marked as blocker since it looks like it was never supported, because it depends on the order of attrs passed in ModelBase.__new__(). So on Django 2.1 and Python 3.7: In [1]: import django ...: django.VERSION In [2]: from django.db import models ...: ...: class FooBar(models.Model): ...: def get_foo_bar_display(self): ...: return "something" ...: ...: foo_bar = models.CharField("foo", choices=[(1, 'foo'), (2, 'bar')]) ...: ...: def __str__(self): ...: return self.get_foo_bar_display() # This returns 'foo' or 'bar' in 2.2, but 'something' in 2.1 ...: ...: class Meta: ...: app_label = 'test' ...: ...: FooBar(foo_bar=1) Out[2]: <FooBar: foo> Before ​Python 3.6 the order of attrs wasn't defined at all.
Sergey, an example from the ticket description works for me with Django 2.1 and Python 3.6, 3.7 and 3.8.
In [2]: import django ...: django.VERSION Out[2]: (2, 1, 13, 'final', 0) In [3]: import sys ...: sys.version Out[3]: '3.5.7 (default, Oct 17 2019, 07:04:41) \n[GCC 8.3.0]' In [4]: from django.db import models ...: ...: class FooBar(models.Model): ...: foo_bar = models.CharField("foo", choices=[(1, 'foo'), (2, 'bar')]) ...: ...: def __str__(self): ...: return self.get_foo_bar_display() # This returns 'foo' or 'bar' in 2.2, but 'something' in 2.1 ...: ...: def get_foo_bar_display(self): ...: return "something" ...: ...: class Meta: ...: app_label = 'test' ...: ...: FooBar(foo_bar=1) Out[4]: <FooBar: foo>
OK, so there is a behaviour change here, but Sergey is correct that it does depend on attr order, so it's hard to say that this can be said to ever have been thought of as supported, with the exact example provided. This example produces the opposite result on 2.1 (even on >=PY36): def test_overriding_display_backwards(self): class FooBar2(models.Model): def get_foo_bar_display(self): return "something" foo_bar = models.CharField("foo", choices=[(1, 'foo'), (2, 'bar')]) f = FooBar2(foo_bar=1) # This returns 'foo' or 'bar' in both 2.2 and 2.1 self.assertEqual(f.get_foo_bar_display(), "foo") Because get_foo_bar_display() is defined before foo_bar is gets replaced in the the add_to_class() step. Semantically order shouldn't make a difference. Given that it does, I can't see that we're bound to maintain that behaviour. (There's a possible fix in Field.contribute_to_class() but implementing that just reverses the pass/fail behaviour depending on order...) Rather, the correct way to implement this on 2.2+ is: def test_overriding_display(self): class FooBar(models.Model): foo_bar = models.CharField("foo", choices=[(1, 'foo'), (2, 'bar')]) def _get_FIELD_display(self, field): if field.attname == 'foo_bar': return "something" return super()._get_FIELD_display(field) f = FooBar(foo_bar=1) self.assertEqual(f.get_foo_bar_display(), "something") This is stable for declaration order on version 2.2+. This approach requires overriding _get_FIELD_display() before declaring fields on 2.1, because otherwise Model._get_FIELD_display() is picked up during Field.contribute_to_class(). This ordering dependency is, ultimately, the same issue that was addressed in a68ea231012434b522ce45c513d84add516afa60, and the follow-up in #30254. The behaviour in 2.1 (and before) was incorrect. Yes, there's a behaviour change here but it's a bugfix, and all bugfixes are breaking changes if you're depending on the broken behaviour. I'm going to downgrade this from Release Blocker accordingly. I'll reclassify this as a Documentation issue and provide the working example, as overriding _get_FIELD_display() is a legitimate use-case I'd guess.
Replying to Carlton Gibson: (There's a possible fix in Field.contribute_to_class() but implementing that just reverses the pass/fail behaviour depending on order...) Doesn't this fix it? if not hasattr(cls, 'get_%s_display' % self.name): setattr(cls, 'get_%s_display' % self.name, partialmethod(cls._get_FIELD_display, field=self))

problem_statement.Title: admin_views.test_multidb fails with persistent per-alias SQLite test DBs: sqlite3.OperationalError: database is locked
problem_statement.Description: When using persistent per-alias SQLite test files (TEST['NAME']) together with --keepdb, tests that touch multiple database aliases can fail with sqlite3.OperationalError: database is locked because two distinct aliases are grouped as the same underlying test database file and both attempt concurrent writes.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. sqlite3 DatabaseCreation.test_db_signature assumes the production database NAME (connection.settings_dict['NAME']) uniquely identifies the test database and uses it as the sole signature component, ignoring the computed test database name. Location: django/db/backends/sqlite3/creation.py :: DatabaseCreation.test_db_signature.

2. The test runner groups database aliases by connection.creation.test_db_signature(); because the sqlite3 signature omitted the computed test DB name, aliases configured with different TEST['NAME'] values can receive identical signatures and are incorrectly treated as mirrors of one another. Location: django/test/utils.py :: get_unique_databases_and_mirrors and setup_databases.

3. This behavior deviates from the base-class contract: BaseDatabaseCreation.test_db_signature() uses self._get_test_db_name() when building signatures. The sqlite3 override's omission of the computed test DB name is inconsistent with the base class and is the incorrect assumption. Location: django/db/backends/base/creation.py :: BaseDatabaseCreation.test_db_signature and django/db/backends/sqlite3/creation.py :: DatabaseCreation.test_db_signature.

4. Concrete consequence in tests: when two aliases are mapped to the same physical file, TestCase setup code (setUpTestData / transactions) causes concurrent writes to the same SQLite file, producing sqlite3.OperationalError: database is locked during model.save(). Observed in tests/admin_views/test_multidb.MultiDatabaseTests.setUpTestData and the stack trace originating in django/contrib/auth/models.py -> model.save. Location: tests/admin_views/test_multidb.py :: MultiDatabaseTests.setUpTestData and the reported stack trace.

5. Correct signature relationship (what is missing): the test_db_signature must include both the configured production NAME (connection.settings_dict['NAME']) and the computed test database name returned by self._get_test_db_name(); additionally, the existing in-memory special-case (including the connection alias for in-memory test DBs) must be preserved. Location for intended relationship: django/db/backends/sqlite3/creation.py :: DatabaseCreation.test_db_signature and django/db/backends/base/creation.py :: BaseDatabaseCreation.test_db_signature.
StepsToReproduce: Define two sqlite DATABASES aliases (e.g. 'default' and 'other') and set distinct persistent test file names: TEST['NAME'] = 'test_default.sqlite3' and TEST['NAME'] = 'test_other.sqlite3'.
Run the test runner for the failing test with keepdb enabled and no parallelism: ./tests/runtests.py admin_views.test_multidb -v 3 --keepdb --parallel 1
Observe test setup attempts and the failure raised during setUpClass / setUpTestData (OperationalError: database is locked).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When separate DATABASES aliases are configured with distinct TEST['NAME'] persistent SQLite files, the test runner creates/uses separate test database files per alias so tests run without SQLite "database is locked" errors and without different aliases being treated as mirrors of the same physical file.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Aliases configured with different persistent TEST['NAME'] were grouped by the test runner as the same test database (treated as mirrors). Two aliases ended up pointing to the same SQLite file and concurrent writes during test setup produced sqlite3.OperationalError: database is locked (stack trace shows failure during User.save in setUpTestData).

problem_statement.Title: makemigrations serializes nested (inner) classes as top-level, dropping outer-class qualifier
problem_statement.Description: When a Field subclass or enum class is defined as an inner (nested) class and used on a Model, manage.py makemigrations produces migration code that refers to the nested class as if it were a top-level class in the module (e.g. test1.models.State or test1.models.Inner) instead of preserving the outer-class qualifier (test1.models.Thing.State or test1.models.Outer.Inner). The generated migration may also emit import statements that treat a class name as a module.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Type identity is built from module + __name__ instead of module + __qualname__ (django/db/migrations/serializer.py :: TypeSerializer.serialize). Evidence: TypeSerializer constructs "%s.%s" % (module, self.value.__name__), which drops outer-class qualifiers for nested classes.  
2) _serialize_path assumes the left-hand side of path.rsplit('.', 1) is an importable module (django/db/migrations/serializer.py :: DeconstructableSerializer._serialize_path). Evidence: code does module, name = path.rsplit('.', 1) and emits "import %s" % module; for a path like "test1.models.Thing.State" this sets module="test1.models.Thing" and emits an invalid "import test1.models.Thing" because the left side can include class qualifiers.  
3) Field.deconstruct already returns module + __qualname__, so the serializer must accept qualname-containing paths (django/db/models/fields/__init__.py :: Field.deconstruct). Evidence: Field.deconstruct uses self.__class__.__module__ and self.__class__.__qualname__, which is why serializer receives dotted qualnames such as "test1.models.Thing.State".  
4) Existing serializers show the intended pattern: FunctionTypeSerializer and EnumSerializer use __qualname__ and guard against '<locals>' (django/db/migrations/serializer.py :: FunctionTypeSerializer.serialize and EnumSerializer.serialize). Evidence: these serializers construct module + __qualname__ and reject local (non-importable) qualnames; TypeSerializer deviates from this pattern.  
5) Concrete string/formula relationships involved: TypeSerializer currently uses value.__name__ where value.__qualname__ is required; DeconstructableSerializer currently does module, name = path.rsplit('.', 1) and emits "import %s" % module unconditionally while Field.deconstruct produces path = <module> + '.' + <class.__qualname__> (e.g. "test1.models.Thing.State").
StepsToReproduce: Run: python manage.py makemigrations
Open the generated migration file and inspect the field definition — the migration references test1.models.Inner instead of test1.models.Outer.Inner.

Alternate reproduction (enum nested in a model):
Create:
import enum
from enumfields import Enum, EnumField
class Thing(models.Model):
    @enum.unique
    class State(Enum):
        on = 'on'
        off = 'off'
    state = EnumField(enum=State)
Run: python manage.py makemigrations and inspect the generated migration; it will reference test1.models.State instead of test1.models.Thing.State.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Generated migration code should refer to nested classes with their full qualified name including the outer-class qualifier (e.g. test1.models.Outer.Inner or test1.models.Thing.State). Import lines in the migration should import actual modules, not class-qualified names that treat classes as modules.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: makemigrations outputs migration code that drops the outer-class qualifier and references nested classes as if they were top-level (e.g. test1.models.Inner or test1.models.State). The serializer also emits import statements based on path.rsplit('.', 1) that can produce invalid imports like "import test1.models.Thing" (treating a class as a module).

hints_text:
This should be possible to do by relying on __qualname__ (instead of __name__) now that master is Python 3 only.
​PR
I think we should focus on using __qualname__ during migration serialization as well instead of simply solving the field subclasses case.
In fb0f987: Fixed #27914 -- Added support for nested classes in Field.deconstruct()/repr().
In 451b585: Refs #27914 -- Used qualname in model operations' deconstruct().
I am still encountering this issue when running makemigrations on models that include a django-enumfields EnumField. From tracing through the code, I believe the Enum is getting serialized using the django.db.migrations.serializer.TypeSerializer, which still uses the __name__ rather than __qualname__. As a result, the Enum's path gets resolved to app_name.models.enum_name and the generated migration file throws an error "app_name.models has no 'enum_name' member". The correct path for the inner class should be app_name.models.model_name.enum_name. ​https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/db/migrations/serializer.py#L266
Reopening it. Will recheck with nested enum field.

In 30271a47: [3.0.x] Refs #27914 -- Fixed serialization of nested enum.Enum classes in migrations. Backport of 6452112640081ac8838147a8ba192c45879203d8 from master
commit 6452112640081ac8838147a8ba192c45879203d8 does not resolve this ticket. The commit patched the EnumSerializer with __qualname__, which works for Enum members. However, the serializer_factory is returning TypeSerializer for the Enum subclass, which is still using __name__ With v3.0.x introducing models.Choices, models.IntegerChoices, using nested enums will become a common pattern; serializing them properly with __qualname__ seems prudent. Here's a patch for the 3.0rc1 build ​https://github.com/django/django/files/3879265/django_db_migrations_serializer_TypeSerializer.patch.txt

diff --git a/django/urls/resolvers.py b/django/urls/resolvers.py
--- a/django/urls/resolvers.py
+++ b/django/urls/resolvers.py
@@ -158,8 +158,9 @@ def match(self, path):
             # If there are any named groups, use those as kwargs, ignoring
             # non-named groups. Otherwise, pass all non-named arguments as
             # positional arguments.
-            kwargs = {k: v for k, v in match.groupdict().items() if v is not None}
+            kwargs = match.groupdict()
             args = () if kwargs else match.groups()
+            kwargs = {k: v for k, v in kwargs.items() if v is not None}
             return path[match.end():], args, kwargs
         return None
