problem_statement.Title: Optional named-capturing URL group with inner capture causes extra positional args (TypeError) in Django 3.0
problem_statement.Description: When a regex URL pattern defines an optional named group that contains inner (unnamed) capturing groups, resolving that pattern can produce unexpected positional arguments that are passed to the view. This manifests as a TypeError when calling views that expect only (request[, format]) — observed after upgrading to Django 3.0.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/urls/resolvers.py :: RegexPattern.match encodes a wrong decision predicate: it treats "no matched named-group values" as "pattern has no named groups". The code builds
   kwargs = {k: v for k, v in match.groupdict().items() if v is not None}
   args = () if kwargs else match.groups()
   return path[match.end():], args, kwargs
   This equates "kwargs == {}" (no matched values) with "pattern defines no named groups", so when an optional named group is present but did not match, inner unnamed captures leak out as positional args.

2) django/urls/resolvers.py :: URLResolver.resolve (and URLPattern.resolve) rely on match() signaling the presence of named groups by returning empty args. Resolver logic uses the emptiness of the merged kwargs dict to decide whether to prepend parent args to subpattern args (paraphrased):
   sub_match_dict = {...}
   sub_match_args = sub_match.args
   if not sub_match_dict:
       sub_match_args = args + sub_match.args
   If RegexPattern.match returns positional args for patterns that actually define named groups, those args are incorrectly propagated and passed to the view.

3) django/core/handlers/base.py :: BaseHandler._get_response assumes callback_args are only intended positional parameters for the view and forwards them directly:
   response = wrapped_callback(request, *callback_args, **callback_kwargs)
   Any stray positional args produced by the resolver become actual positional arguments to the view and can cause TypeError.

4) django/urls/resolvers.py :: RoutePattern.match demonstrates the intended semantics (evidence): it uses match.groupdict() to determine named-group presence and always returns args == (), i.e. positional groups are suppressed when the pattern defines named groups. RegexPattern.match should follow the same pattern-level decision, not the presence of matched values.

5) Correct relationship (preserved formula from investigation): compute
   groupdict = match.groupdict()
   kwargs = {k: v for k, v in groupdict.items() if v is not None}
   args = () if groupdict else match.groups()
   return path[match.end():], args, kwargs
   The decision to return empty args must be based on the pattern-level groupdict (presence of named-group keys), not on filtered matched values (kwargs).

6) Investigation gap: the diagnosing agent did not run the full test suite or perform an exhaustive search for other callers that may have depended on the previous (incorrect) behavior; there may be callers or third-party code relying on the old semantics.
StepsToReproduce: Use Django 3.0.
In URLConf add the regex pattern with an optional named group containing inner captures, e.g. re_path(r'^module/(?P<format>(html|json|xml))?/?$', views.modules, name='modules').
Define the view with a signature expecting only request and an optional format keyword/default, e.g. def modules(request, format='html'): ... return render(...).
Request the URL that omits the optional group, e.g. GET /module/.
Observe the server error (TypeError) during view invocation.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When the optional named URL group does not match, the view is called with only the request (and no unexpected extra positional arguments); the view's default for the optional parameter (e.g. format='html') is used or the parameter is provided as a keyword argument as intended by the URL pattern.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Resolving the pattern produces extra positional arguments derived from inner unnamed captures and the view is invoked like wrapped_callback(request, <unexpected_positional1>, <unexpected_positional2>, ...), causing a TypeError such as: "modules() takes from 1 to 2 positional arguments but 3 were given".

It seems to work if you remove the extra parentheses: re_path(r'^module/(?P<format>html|json|xml)?/?$', views.modules, name='modules'), It seems Django is getting confused by the nested groups.

problem_statement.Title: get_<field>_display returns raw value for subclass-added choice tuples when choices are overridden on an abstract base
problem_statement.Description: When a concrete model inherits an abstract base that defines a Field with choices, and the concrete model overrides that Field's choices by adding tuples, get_<field>_display() can return the raw value for the newly added tuples instead of the corresponding label. The incorrect behavior occurs because the generated get_<field>_display accessor can remain bound to the abstract base's Field instance and therefore uses the base class's choices.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Field.contribute_to_class uses hasattr(cls, 'get_%s_display' % self.name) to decide whether to install the generated accessor. Location: django/db/models/fields/__init__.py:Field.contribute_to_class. Incorrect assumption: treating an inherited generated accessor the same as an explicit user-defined override prevents rebinding the accessor for a copied Field on a subclass.

2) The generated accessor is installed as partialmethod(cls._get_FIELD_display, field=self), which binds Model._get_FIELD_display to a specific Field instance. Location: django/db/models/fields/__init__.py:Field.contribute_to_class. Incorrect assumption: assuming the existing accessor (possibly inherited) is already bound to the correct Field instance for the subclass.

3) Model._get_FIELD_display resolves labels solely from the bound Field instance's flatchoices using the relationship: choices_dict = dict(make_hashable(field.flatchoices)); return force_str(choices_dict.get(make_hashable(value), value), strings_only=True). Location: django/db/models/base.py:Model._get_FIELD_display. Implication: if the accessor is bound to the parent's Field instance, subclass-only choice tuples are not present in field.flatchoices and thus the raw value is returned.

4) Abstract-base field copying creates a new Field instance via copy.deepcopy(field) and registers it with new_class.add_to_class(field.name, new_field), which triggers Field.contribute_to_class for the copied Field. Location: django/db/models/base.py:ModelBase.__new__. Incorrect assumption: that add_to_class on a copied Field will always install/rebind generated accessors; the hasattr guard can prevent installation so the inherited accessor remains bound to the original Field instance.

5) Observable consequence encoded across the above locations: subclass-specific choices (new tuples) are ignored by get_<field>_display because the accessor resolves labels from the wrong Field.flatchoices, producing the raw field value instead of the expected label. Evidence: reported example where value "C" yields "C" instead of "output3".
StepsToReproduce: Define an abstract base model A with choices and a CharField using them, e.g. foo_choice = [("A","output1"),("B","output2")]; field_foo = models.CharField(..., choices=foo_choice); class Meta: abstract = True.

Define a concrete subclass B(A) that overrides foo_choice to add a new tuple, e.g. foo_choice = [("A","output1"),("B","output2"),("C","output3")]; and define field_foo = models.CharField(..., choices=foo_choice) on B.
Create and save an instance of B with field_foo = "C".
Call instance.get_field_foo_display() and observe the returned value.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: get_field_foo_display() returns the label corresponding to the current model's choices for the field (for the example above, calling on a B instance with value "C" returns "output3"). More generally, the generated get_<field>_display accessor should resolve labels using the Field.flatchoices of the Field instance defined on the concrete model.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: get_field_foo_display() returns the raw stored value (e.g. "C") for choice tuples that were added on the subclass, instead of returning the expected label (e.g. "output3").

Thanks for an extra info. I was able to reproduce this issue, e.g. >>> B.objects.create(field_foo='A').get_field_foo_display() output1 >>> B.objects.create(field_foo='B').get_field_foo_display() output2 >>> B.objects.create(field_foo='C').get_field_foo_display() C Regression in 2d38eb0ab9f78d68c083a5b78b1eca39027b279a (Django 2.2.7).
may i work on this?
After digging in, i have found that the choices of B model are the same with the A model, despiite them being the proper ones in init. Migration is correct, so now i must find why the choices of model B are ignored. Being my first issue, some hints would be appreciated. Thanks
​https://github.com/django/django/pull/12266
I think this ticket is very much related to the discussions on #30931. The line if not hasattr(cls, 'get_%s_display' % self.name) breaks the expected behaviour on model inheritance, which causing this bug. (see ​https://github.com/django/django/commit/2d38eb0ab9f78d68c083a5b78b1eca39027b279a#diff-bf776a3b8e5dbfac2432015825ef8afeR766) IMO there are three important points to discuss: 1- Obviously get_<field>_display() should work as expected with inheritance, so this line should be reverted/fixed: if not hasattr(cls, 'get_%s_display' % self.name) 2- I think developers should be able to override get_<field>_display() method on the model class: class Bar(models.Model): foo = models.CharField('foo', choices=[(0, 'foo')]) def get_foo_display(self): return 'something' b = Bar(foo=0) assert b.get_foo_display() == 'something' 3- I think Field should not check an attribute of model class and make a decision based on it. This check and set logic should be delegated to BaseModel with an abstraction to make it less magical and more clean. Maybe something like this: class ModelBase(type): .... def add_overridable_to_class(cls, name, value): // Set value only if the name is not already defined in the class itself (no `hasattr`) if name not in cls.__dict__: setattr(cls, name, value) class Field(RegisterLookupMixin): ... def contribute_to_class(self, cls, name, private_only=False): ... if self.choices is not None: cls.add_overridable_to_class('get_%s_display' % self.name, partialmethod(cls._get_FIELD_display, field=self))
Why would checking on fields class be a bad idea? If you are a field of a model that is not abstract but your parent is an abstract method, wouldn't you want to override your parent's method if you both have the same method?
Replying to George Popides: Why would checking on fields class be a bad idea? If you are a field of a model that is not abstract but your parent is an abstract method, wouldn't you want to override your parent's method if you both have the same method? Well it is not something I would prefer because it makes two classes tightly coupled to each other, which means it is hard to change one without touching to the other one and you always need to think about side effects of your change. Which eventually makes this two classes hard to test and makes the codebase hard to maintain. Your logic about overriding might/or might not be true. I would just execute this logic on ModelBase rather than Field.

problem_statement.Title: translation.E004 raised for sublanguage LANGUAGE_CODE (e.g. "de-at") even though base language exists in LANGUAGES
problem_statement.Description: When settings.LANGUAGE_CODE is a sublanguage tag (for example "de-at") and the corresponding base language ("de") is present in settings.LANGUAGES, Django's system check raises translation.E004 claiming LANGUAGE_CODE is not in LANGUAGES. This contradicts documented behavior that a base language should be used when a sublanguage is not provided.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Exact-membership requirement encoded in django/core/checks/translation.py::check_language_settings_consistent: the check builds available_tags = {i for i, _ in settings.LANGUAGES} | {'en-us'} and then returns E004 if settings.LANGUAGE_CODE not in available_tags. Incorrect assumption: LANGUAGE_CODE must be an exact entry in LANGUAGES.  
2) System check ignores configured LANG_INFO fallbacks in django/conf/locale.py: the check does not consult LANG_INFO.get(lang_code, {}).get('fallback', []) when deciding acceptability. Incorrect assumption: no fallbacks exist beyond literal LANGUAGES entries.  
3) Divergence from runtime fallback algorithm in django/utils/translation/trans_real.py::get_supported_language_variant: runtime builds possible_lang_codes = [lang_code] + (LANG_INFO fallbacks) + generic_lang_code (lang_code.split('-')[0]) and returns the first code that is both in supported_lang_codes and passes check_for_language(code). Evidence: runtime maps 'de-at' → 'de' when translation data exists. Incorrect assumption in the system check: runtime fallback behavior and LANG_INFO-based alternatives are irrelevant to settings validation.  
4) Hardcoded special-case: the system check injects {'en-us'} into available_tags in django/core/checks/translation.py. This encodes an exception policy that the check currently treats as implicitly present regardless of LANGUAGES.  
5) Missing test coverage: tests/check_framework/test_translation.py contains no explicit test that LANGUAGE_CODE='de-at' with LANGUAGES containing 'de' is accepted (no regression test proving the intended acceptance behavior), so the behavior change is unverified in tests.
StepsToReproduce: In a Django project settings.py set LANGUAGE_CODE = 'de-at'.
Set LANGUAGES to include the base language, for example LANGUAGES = [('de', 'German'), ('en', 'English')].
Run the system checks: python manage.py check (or start the server to trigger system checks).
Observe that Django raises a SystemCheckError listing (translation.E004) You have provided a value for the LANGUAGE_CODE setting that is not in the LANGUAGES setting.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: No system check error should be raised when LANGUAGE_CODE is a sublanguage (e.g. 'de-at') if the base language (e.g. 'de') is present in LANGUAGES (or an allowed documented fallback applies). Django should accept the configured LANGUAGE_CODE and proceed to use the base language as the effective language.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Django raises SystemCheckError with (translation.E004) stating the configured LANGUAGE_CODE is not in LANGUAGES when LANGUAGE_CODE is a sublanguage like 'de-at', even though 'de' exists in LANGUAGES. (Example: on Django 3.0.2, LANGUAGE_CODE='de-at' triggers E004 while LANGUAGE_CODE='es-ar' works when 'es-ar' is explicitly provided.)

problem_statement.Title: Admin readonly display renders JSONField Python repr ({'foo': 'bar'}) instead of JSON ({"foo": "bar"})
problem_statement.Description: When a model JSONField is shown readonly in the Django admin, its value is rendered using Python's dict/list repr (single quotes) instead of valid JSON (double quotes). This appears in admin list/detail readonly displays.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) display_for_field has no special-case for models.JSONField: django/contrib/admin/utils.py::display_for_field falls through to display_for_value which ultimately uses str(value), producing Python repr like {'foo': 'bar'}. (Wrong assumption: str(value) is an acceptable display for JSONField values.)

2) Admin display ignores the JSONField's encoder: django/db/models/fields/json.py::JSONField defines self.encoder and JSONField.get_prep_value uses json.dumps(value, cls=self.encoder). The admin should reuse the field's encoder when serializing for presentation. (Relationship to preserve: use json.dumps(value, cls=getattr(field, 'encoder', None)) to match field serialization.)

3) display_for_value fallback behavior: django/contrib/admin/utils.py::display_for_value falls back to str(value) for dict/list values, which yields Python repr with single quotes instead of JSON with double quotes. (Wrong assumption: the generic fallback is suitable for JSONField-stored Python objects.)

4) forms.JSONField.prepare_value and InvalidJSONInput were suggested as an alternative but were not exercised by the investigation: django/forms/fields.py::JSONField.prepare_value and class InvalidJSONInput may affect presentation of invalid JSON; the agent did not trace prepare_value with concrete values, so it's unknown whether admin should call prepare_value instead of json.dumps in some cases. (Gap in investigation: possible special-casing needed for InvalidJSONInput flows.)

5) Proposed serialization relationship and fallback for admin display (location for change): django/contrib/admin/utils.py::display_for_field should, for JSONField instances, serialize with json.dumps(value, cls=getattr(field, 'encoder', None)) and catch TypeError to fallback to str(value). (Preserved formula: json.dumps(value, cls=getattr(field, 'encoder', None)); fallback -> str(value).)

6) Tests missing: tests/admin_utils/tests.py contains display_for_field/display_for_value tests but has no test asserting that models.JSONField values are rendered as JSON; this lack of coverage permits the regression. (Location: tests/admin_utils/tests.py.)

7) Call sites assume current behavior: django/contrib/admin/helpers.py and django/contrib/admin/templatetags/admin_list.py call display_for_field(value, field, empty_value_display) and will surface whichever string display_for_field returns; the investigation did not verify template-level HTML-escaping or truncation implications for JSON output. (Wrong assumption: templates need no special handling for JSON output.)
StepsToReproduce: Define a model with a models.JSONField and save an instance with a value like {"foo": "bar"}.
Register the model in the Django admin (or ensure it's visible in the admin list/detail view).
View the model in the admin readonly display (list or detail) and observe the displayed JSONField value.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: JSONField values are displayed as valid JSON (e.g. {"foo": "bar"}) in the admin readonly views, using the field's expected JSON representation.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The admin displays Python dict/list representations with single quotes (e.g. {'foo': 'bar'}) for JSONField values instead of valid JSON.

--- a/django/db/backends/base/creation.py
+++ b/django/db/backends/base/creation.py
@@ -6,6 +6,7 @@
 from django.conf import settings
 from django.core import serializers
 from django.db import router
+from django.db.transaction import atomic
 
 # The prefix to put on the default database name when creating

@@ -126,8 +127,16 @@ def deserialize_db_from_string(self, data):
         the serialize_db_to_string() method.
         """
         data = StringIO(data)
-        for obj in serializers.deserialize("json", data, using=self.connection.alias):
-            obj.save()
+        # Load data in a transaction to handle forward references and cycles.
+        with atomic(using=self.connection.alias):
+            # Disable constraint checks, because some databases (MySQL) doesn't
+            # support deferred checks.
+            with self.connection.constraint_checks_disabled():
+                for obj in serializers.deserialize('json', data, using=self.connection.alias):
+                    obj.save()
+            # Manually check for any invalid keys that might have been added,
+            # because constraint checks were disabled.
+            self.connection.check_constraints()
 
     def _get_database_display_str(self, verbosity, database_name):
         """

problem_statement.Title: TransactionTestCase.serialized_rollback fails to restore DB snapshot due to missing transaction/constraint/forward-ref handling in BaseDatabaseCreation.deserialize_db_from_string
problem_statement.Description: When TransactionTestCase.serialized_rollback = True is used, restoring the serialized DB snapshot can fail: deserialize_db_from_string deserializes objects and calls obj.save() sequentially outside of a transaction and without handling forward/deferred foreign-key references or temporarily disabling constraint checks, which can raise IntegrityError and leave the DB inconsistent.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption: the serialized object order is sufficient to satisfy all foreign-key constraints. Location: django/db/backends/base/creation.py::BaseDatabaseCreation.deserialize_db_from_string. Evidence: serialize_db_to_string uses serializers.sort_dependencies which only orders by natural-key dependencies (django/core/serializers/__init__.py::sort_dependencies) and does not guarantee a FK topological order.

2) Incorrect assumption: deserializing and calling obj.save() sequentially outside a transaction is safe. Location: django/db/backends/base/creation.py::BaseDatabaseCreation.deserialize_db_from_string. Evidence: current code wraps no transaction; exact pattern present: for obj in serializers.deserialize("json", data, using=...): obj.save().

3) Incorrect assumption: constraint checks need not be temporarily disabled during bulk restore. Location: django/db/backends/base/creation.py::BaseDatabaseCreation.deserialize_db_from_string. Evidence: no use of connection.constraint_checks_disabled(); loaddata uses constraint_checks_disabled() to avoid transient FK failures (django/core/management/commands/loaddata.py::Command.loaddata).

4) Incorrect assumption: forward references encountered during deserialization will not occur or can be ignored. Location: django/db/backends/base/creation.py::BaseDatabaseCreation.deserialize_db_from_string. Evidence: serializers.deserialize supports handle_forward_references=True and yields objects with deferred_fields which must be saved via DeserializedObject.save_deferred_fields (django/core/serializers/*), but deserialize_db_from_string does not use handle_forward_references nor call save_deferred_fields.

5) Incorrect assumption: it is acceptable to skip final constraint verification after restore. Location: django/db/backends/base/creation.py::BaseDatabaseCreation.deserialize_db_from_string. Evidence: loaddata calls connection.check_constraints(table_names=...) after deferred saves; deserialize_db_from_string does not.

6) Scope observation: no backend-specific overrides of deserialize_db_from_string were found, so the problematic behavior is encoded in the single implementation at django/db/backends/base/creation.py::BaseDatabaseCreation.deserialize_db_from_string (changing this one location affects all core backends).
StepsToReproduce: Create a Django test project with at least one foreign-key relationship between models (e.g., Model B has a FK to Model A).
Create a TransactionTestCase subclass with serialized_rollback = True.
In setUpTestData or the test setup, create instances such that the serialized snapshot will include both objects (the serialization order depends on app/model registry order).

Run the test suite. During the test fixture setup (TransactionTestCase._fixture_setup) the stored JSON snapshot is deserialized via BaseDatabaseCreation.deserialize_db_from_string; if the serialized ordering places the FK-holder before the FK-target, the test run will raise an IntegrityError or fail to restore referenced objects.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When serialized_rollback = True is used, the test framework restores the DB snapshot deterministically and user-visible relationships are intact: no IntegrityError occurs during fixture setup, all serialized objects are present, and foreign-key references point to the expected target objects.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Deserialization can raise IntegrityError or leave referenced objects missing because deserialize_db_from_string deserializes and calls obj.save() sequentially outside a transaction and without handling forward/deferred foreign-key references or temporarily disabling constraint checks; the serialized ordering (which only accounts for natural keys) is insufficient to prevent FK constraint violations.

hints_text: I've run into a problem related to this one (just reported as #31051), so I ended up looking into this problem as well. The original report still seems accurate to me, with the proposed solution valid. I've been working on a fix and (most of the work), testcase for this problem. I'll do some more testing and provide a proper PR for this issue and #31051 soon. The testcase is not ideal yet (testing the testing framework is tricky), but I'll expand on that in the PR. Furthermore, I noticed that loaddata does not just wrap everything in a transaction, it also explicitly disables constraint checks inside the transaction: with connection.constraint_checks_disabled(): self.objs_with_deferred_fields = [] for fixture_label in fixture_labels: self.load_label(fixture_label) for obj in self.objs_with_deferred_fields: obj.save_deferred_fields(using=self.using) # Since we disabled constraint checks, we must manually check for # any invalid keys that might have been added table_names = [model._meta.db_table for model in self.models] try: connection.check_constraints(table_names=table_names) except Exception as e: e.args = ("Problem installing fixtures: %s" % e,) raise I had a closer look at how this works (since I understood that a transaction already implicitly disables constraint checks) and it turns out that MySQL/InnoDB is an exception and does *not* defer constraint checks to the end of the transaction, but instead needs extra handling (so constraint_checks_disabled() is a no-op on most database backends). See #3615.

problem_statement.Title: Inherited Parent.Meta.ordering = ['-pk'] is flipped to ASC when querying multi-table Child
problem_statement.Description: When a model (Parent) declares Meta.ordering = ['-pk'] and another model (Child) inherits from it (multi-table inheritance), querying Child produces an ORDER BY on the parent PK ascending (ASC) instead of descending (DESC). The ordering sign is lost/flipped during SQL compilation.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect recursive default propagation in SQLCompiler.find_ordering_name (django/db/models/sql/compiler.py::SQLCompiler.find_ordering_name): the method passes the outer resolved order string (variable 'order') as the default_order into recursive calls for string ordering items (conceptual call: self.find_ordering_name(item, opts, alias, order, already_seen)). This assumes composing an outer default with an inner explicit '-' prefix yields the correct final sign — that assumption is incorrect and causes double-negation/flipped direction.

2) API semantics of get_order_dir and ORDER_DIR (django/db/models/sql/query.py::get_order_dir and django/db/models/sql/constants.py::ORDER_DIR) make the above misuse harmful: get_order_dir(field, default) selects ORDER_DIR[default] to interpret '-' prefixes, and ORDER_DIR is {'ASC': ('ASC','DESC'), 'DESC': ('DESC','ASC')}. Because the meaning of a '-' prefix depends on the provided default, passing an outer default ('DESC') into an inner resolution changes how an explicit inner '-' is interpreted and can flip the intended direction.

3) Inherited ordering strings propagate as-is into the child Meta (django/db/models/base.py): new_class._meta.ordering = base_meta.ordering. This causes Child._meta.ordering to contain the literal '-pk' from Parent, which triggers the compiler path that expands relation-string ordering and exposes the recursive-default bug.

4) Correct relationship that is not implemented (diagnostic statement): string ordering items for a related/inner model should be resolved using the original default_order (the compiler's initial default), and then, if the outer ordering had a descending sign, that resolved set of OrderBy expressions should be reversed as a group before appending. The current implementation instead feeds the outer 'order' into inner resolution, causing incorrect composition of signs.
StepsToReproduce:
Open Django shell and run:
>>> print(Child.objects.all().query)
Observe the generated SQL ORDER BY clause.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Querying Child should return rows ordered by primary key descending, matching Parent.Meta.ordering (i.e., SQL should include ORDER BY ... DESC on the parent primary key).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Querying Child generates SQL with ORDER BY on the parent primary key ascending. Example observed SQL: SELECT "myapp_parent"."id", "myapp_child"."parent_ptr_id" FROM "myapp_child" INNER JOIN "myapp_parent" ON ("myapp_child"."parent_ptr_id" = "myapp_parent"."id") ORDER BY "myapp_parent"."id" ASC

problem_statement.Title: Incorrect hint text suggests ForeignKey and symmetrical=False for recursive many-to-many through models
problem_statement.Description: When an intermediary (through) model for a ManyToManyField contains more than two ForeignKey fields and no through_fields are set, Django's system check presents a misleading hint that recommends using ForeignKey("self", symmetrical=False, through="..."). The hint text is wrong and may mislead users.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect hardcoded hint literal in django/db/models/fields/related.py at ManyToManyField._check_relationship_model (two occurrences). The method constructs the message: 'If you want to create a recursive relationship, use ForeignKey("%s", symmetrical=False, through="%s").' This encodes the incorrect assumption that 'symmetrical' and 'through' are ForeignKey kwargs. The string is formatted with RECURSIVE_RELATIONSHIP_CONSTANT and relationship_model_name and those format variables must be preserved when changing the message.

2) Test encodes the same incorrect assumption in tests/invalid_models_tests/test_relative_fields.py:test_ambiguous_relationship_model. The test expects: 'If you want to create a recursive relationship, use ForeignKey("self", symmetrical=False, through="AmbiguousRelationship").' The test therefore validates and preserves the incorrect hint.

3) Inspection of related components (django/db/models/fields/reverse_related.py:ManyToManyRel and django/db/models/fields/related_descriptors.py:ManyToManyDescriptor) found no incorrect runtime semantics; these classes correctly handle rel.symmetrical and through/through_fields. This indicates the problem is strictly a UX/textual mistake, not a logic error in relationship handling.

4) Repository search for the exact phrase and for 'symmetrical=False, through' returned only the two occurrences in related.py and the single test, so all code locations encoding the incorrect hint are: django/db/models/fields/related.py (two places) and tests/invalid_models_tests/test_relative_fields.py (one place).

(Additional investigation gap: the test suite and i18n string extraction were not executed during the analysis, so potential translation/localization side effects were not validated.)
StepsToReproduce: Define a through (intermediary) model used by a ManyToManyField that contains more than two ForeignKey fields and do not set through_fields.
Use that through model on a ManyToManyField (e.g., via model definitions) so Django performs model validation.
Run Django's system checks (for example, manage.py check) or otherwise load the models to trigger the model validation that produces the hint.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The system-check hint for an ambiguous intermediary model should recommend using ManyToManyField("<recursive-constant>", through="<relationship_model>") (i.e., suggest ManyToManyField, not ForeignKey, and omit symmetrical=False).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The system-check hint currently recommends: 'If you want to create a recursive relationship, use ForeignKey("self", symmetrical=False, through="AmbiguousRelationship").', referencing ForeignKey and the obsolete symmetrical=False option instead of ManyToManyField.

diff --git a/django/db/models/sql/query.py b/django/db/models/sql/query.py
--- a/django/db/models/sql/query.py
+++ b/django/db/models/sql/query.py
@@ -1927,6 +1927,19 @@ def set_group_by(self, allow_aliases=True):
         primary key, and the query would be equivalent, the optimization
         will be made automatically.
         """
+        # Column names from JOINs to check collisions with aliases.
+        if allow_aliases:
+            column_names = set()
+            seen_models = set()
+            for join in list(self.alias_map.values())[1:]:  # Skip base table.
+                model = join.join_field.related_model
+                if model not in seen_models:
+                    column_names.update({
+                        field.column
+                        for field in model._meta.local_concrete_fields
+                    })
+                    seen_models.add(model)
+
         group_by = list(self.select)
         if self.annotation_select:
             for alias, annotation in self.annotation_select.items():
@@ -1940,7 +1953,7 @@ def set_group_by(self, allow_aliases=True):
                     warnings.warn(msg, category=RemovedInDjango40Warning)
                     group_by_cols = annotation.get_group_by_cols()
                 else:
-                    if not allow_aliases:
+                    if not allow_aliases or alias in column_names:
                         alias = None
                     group_by_cols = annotation.get_group_by_cols(alias=alias)
                 group_by.extend(group_by_cols)

problem_statement.Title: GROUP BY emits quoted annotation alias for Subquery, causing ambiguous column error (Django 3.0)
problem_statement.Description: Annotating a model with a Subquery using an alias (e.g. 'status') and then grouping/values by that alias causes the SQL compiler to emit the quoted alias ("status") in the GROUP BY clause. When a joined table also has a column with the same name the database raises a ProgrammingError: column reference "status" is ambiguous.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption: compiling a Ref(alias, source) into its quoted alias is always valid for GROUP BY. Location: django/db/models/sql/compiler.py:SQLCompiler.get_group_by. Evidence: compiler emits GROUP BY "status" leading to ambiguity.

2) Incorrect assumption: a Subquery with an alias can signal group-by by returning a Ref(alias, self). Location: django/db/models/expressions.py:Subquery.get_group_by_cols uses `if alias: return [Ref(alias, self)]`. Relationship preserved: Subquery.get_group_by_cols(alias) => [Ref(alias, Subquery(...))].

3) Incorrect assumption: a Ref should compile to a quoted identifier in all clauses. Location: django/db/models/expressions.py:Ref.as_sql returns connection.ops.quote_name(self.refs) (i.e. quoted alias). Evidence: Ref.as_sql produces "status" rather than the subquery SQL.

4) Incorrect assumption: building query.group_by via annotation.get_group_by_cols(alias=alias) is safe because the emitter will resolve any Ref correctly. Location: django/db/models/sql/query.py:Query.set_group_by calls annotation.get_group_by_cols(alias=alias) and places Ref(alias, Subquery) into query.group_by, relying on the compiler to emit a non-ambiguous SQL expression.

Collectively: these assumptions cause a Ref(alias, Subquery) to be compiled as the quoted alias ("status") in GROUP BY instead of the Subquery SQL ((SELECT ...)), producing ambiguous column references when other FROM/JOIN tables define the same column name.
StepsToReproduce: Define models A, B, AB (through model with IntegerField status), and C (ForeignKey to A with IntegerField status) as in the report.
Run on Django 3.0.x with PostgreSQL (example uses Postgres 11).
Execute the sequence from the report: ab_query = AB.objects.filter(a=OuterRef('pk'), b=1)
filter_conditions = Q(pk=1) | Q(ab_a__b=1)
query = A.objects.filter(filter_conditions).annotate(status=Subquery(ab_query.values('status')), c_count=Count('c'))
answer = query.values('status').annotate(total_count=Count('status'))
Evaluate/print answer (or run the query) and observe the error/printed SQL.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The generated SQL should GROUP BY the Subquery expression (e.g. GROUP BY (SELECT ...)) or otherwise produce an unambiguous GROUP BY entry so the database does not raise an ambiguous column error; the query should execute and return the grouped counts for the annotated 'status'.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: On Django 3.0.4 the compiler emits GROUP BY "status" and PostgreSQL raises django.db.utils.ProgrammingError: column reference "status" is ambiguous. Example failing SQL shows GROUP BY "status" while the equivalent Django 2.2.11 SQL used GROUP BY (SELECT ...).

problem_statement.Title: SafeExceptionReporterFilter.cleanse_setting does not recurse into iterables (lists/tuples), leaving nested secrets unmasked
problem_statement.Description: Sensitive values stored inside iterables (e.g., lists/tuples of dicts, nested lists) are not masked by SafeExceptionReporterFilter.cleanse_setting and therefore appear unmasked in get_safe_settings() output and on debug pages.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption: cleanse_setting only needs to recurse into dict instances. Location: django/views/debug.py, class SafeExceptionReporterFilter, method cleanse_setting. Effect: lists/tuples/frozensets containing dicts are returned unchanged so inner dicts keep raw secret values.

2) Incorrect assumption: wrapping callables only at the final cleansed value is sufficient. Location: django/views/debug.py, CallableSettingWrapper usage in SafeExceptionReporterFilter.cleanse_setting. Effect: callables nested inside iterables are not wrapped and may remain callable/unprotected.

3) Exposure point: ExceptionReporter and get_safe_settings assume cleanse_setting returns fully safe values. Location: django/views/debug.py, ExceptionReporter.get_traceback_data and SafeExceptionReporterFilter.get_safe_settings. Effect: any incompleteness in cleanse_setting is propagated to error output.

4) Tests gap: test suite assumes recursion into dicts is sufficient and lacks coverage for lists/tuples/frozenset-of-dicts and nested iterables. Location: tests/view_tests/tests/test_debug.py (e.g., test_cleanse_setting_recurses_in_dictionary). Effect: regression went undetected.

5) Masking constants/criteria are correct and not the cause: cleansed_substitute is the literal '********************' and hidden_settings uses the regex 'API|TOKEN|KEY|SECRET|PASS|SIGNATURE' (case-insensitive). Location: django/views/debug.py, SafeExceptionReporterFilter.cleansed_substitute and SafeExceptionReporterFilter.hidden_settings. Effect: masking pattern and substitute remain unchanged; the bug is traversal, not the masking logic.

6) Implementation evidence: cleanse_setting currently uses an isinstance(value, dict) branch to recurse and otherwise returns value unchanged. Location: django/views/debug.py, SafeExceptionReporterFilter.cleanse_setting. Effect: concrete code path that omits traversal of other iterable container types.
StepsToReproduce: In settings.py, define MY_SETTING as in the report: a dict with keys 'foo','secret','token' and nested iterables, e.g. 'something': [{'foo': 'value'}, {'secret': 'value'}, {'token': 'value'}], 'else': [[...], [...]]
Start a Django project using the version exhibiting the bug (3.0 and below example; note behavior reproduced on 3.1+ with SafeExceptionReporterFilter().get_safe_settings()).

From a Django shell or a view, call from django.views.debug import get_safe_settings (or SafeExceptionReporterFilter().get_safe_settings()) and inspect get_safe_settings()['MY_SETTING'] (e.g., pprint.pprint(...)).
Observe that top-level keys matching hidden_settings (e.g., 'secret', 'token') are masked, but 'secret'/'token' entries inside lists/tuples/nested lists remain unmasked.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: All sensitive values (keys matching the hidden_settings pattern) are masked in the settings output regardless of whether they appear at top-level dicts, inside nested dicts, or inside iterable containers such as lists, tuples, frozensets, or nested lists. Callables found anywhere in settings should be represented consistently (not left unwrapped) in the settings output.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Only dict instances are traversed and masked. Secrets contained inside lists/tuples/nested iterables (e.g., dicts inside a list) remain unmasked and are visible in get_safe_settings() output; callables nested inside those iterables are not wrapped by CallableSettingWrapper.

problem_statement.Title: Migration crashes when deleting index_together that shares fields with unique_together
problem_statement.Description: Deleting an index_together (via migrations) that covers the same columns as an existing unique_together raises a ValueError and aborts the migration. Observed on Django 1.11.10; failure occurs in BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor._delete_composed_index.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect single-constraint assumption in _delete_composed_index (django/db/backends/base/schema.py::BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor._delete_composed_index): the method computes
   meta_constraint_names = {c.name for c in model._meta.constraints}
   meta_index_names = {i.name for i in model._meta.indexes}
   columns = [model._meta.get_field(f).column for f in fields]
   constraint_names = self._constraint_names(model, columns, exclude=meta_constraint_names | meta_index_names, **constraint_kwargs)
and then requires len(constraint_names) == 1. When both a DB unique constraint and a DB index exist for the same columns (e.g. _uniq and _idx), constraint_names contains two entries and the code raises ValueError("Found wrong number (%s) of constraints...").

2) Unconditional index creation assumption in alter_index_together (django/db/backends/base/schema.py::BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.alter_index_together): the method unconditionally invokes self._create_index_sql(...) when adding an index_together, without checking for existing DB constraints that already cover the same columns. The correct relationship (used by detection logic elsewhere) is to derive columns = [f.column for f in fields], compute
   existing = self._constraint_names(model, columns, index=True, exclude=meta_constraint_names | meta_index_names)
and consider the existence of existing DB constraints before creating a new index (current code does not perform this check).

3) Reliance on introspection flags for disambiguation (django/db/backends/base/schema.py::_constraint_names and _delete_composed_index): any disambiguation between unique-backed constraints and plain indexes depends on connection.introspection.get_constraints(cursor, table) returning per-constraint infodicts with boolean keys such as 'unique' and 'index' (accessed as constraints[name].get('unique')). The investigation did not verify that all supported backends reliably populate these flags; the code implicitly assumes their presence and meaning when attempting to choose which DB object to delete.
StepsToReproduce: Create a Django model with two fields (e.g. a, b).
Add both (a, b) to Meta.unique_together and to Meta.index_together (or create equivalent migrations).
Apply migrations so the DB has both the unique constraint and the index on (a, b).
Remove index_together from the model (keep unique_together) and run the migration that deletes the index_together.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The migration completes successfully. Removing index_together that covers columns already constrained by unique_together should not crash; the unique constraint remains in place. Moving an index declaration (for example, from index_together to Meta.indexes) should not cause unnecessary index drop-and-recreate cycles visible to the user.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Migration aborts with ValueError: "Found wrong number (2) of constraints for ..." raised in django/db/backends/base/schema.py::_delete_composed_index because two DB objects (a unique constraint and an index) match the same column set. In practice, the deletion fails and, when index declarations are moved, this can cause unexpected index recreation behavior.

problem_statement.Title: QuerySet.delete() returns inconsistent per-model dict when zero objects deleted
problem_statement.Description: QuerySet.delete() returns a tuple (total_deleted, per_model_dict). When no rows are deleted total_deleted == 0, but the per-model dict is inconsistent: sometimes {} and sometimes contains model-label keys with value 0.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/db/models/deletion.py:Collector.delete (fast_deletes loop) — incorrect assumption: attempted fast deletes are recorded even when they affect 0 rows. The code does: deleted_counter[qs.model._meta.label] += count immediately after count = qs._raw_delete(...), which creates a zero-valued key when count == 0.
2) django/db/models/deletion.py:Collector.delete (per-model delete loop) — incorrect assumption: per-model delete batches unconditionally add counts to deleted_counter even when count == 0. The code does: count = query.delete_batch(...); deleted_counter[model._meta.label] += count, producing zero-valued keys.
3) django/db/models/deletion.py:Collector.delete (single-instance fast-delete early return) — incorrect assumption: the single-instance fast-path returns a per-model dict unconditionally. The method returns: return count, {model._meta.label: count}, which yields {'Label': 0} when count == 0.
4) django/db/models/deletion.py:Collector.collect (fast_deletes population) — incorrect assumption: QuerySets that can be fast-deleted are appended to self.fast_deletes even when they match no rows; later qs._raw_delete() may return count == 0 and the collector records a zero-valued key.
5) django/db/models/query.py:QuerySet.delete — consumer-side assumption: QuerySet.delete forwards Collector.delete()'s result and relies on its dict shape; it does not normalize the per-model dict, so the inconsistency is surfaced to callers.
6) tests/delete/tests.py:test_fast_delete_empty_no_update_can_self_select — test-level assumption: an existing test asserts that delete() returns (0, {'delete.User': 0}) for an empty fast-delete case; this encodes and depends on the current behavior of producing zero-valued per-model keys.
7) Relationship/evidence summary: the expression deleted_counter[model_label] += count (used in fast_deletes loop and per-model loop) and the early return return count, {model._meta.label: count} are the exact code paths that create zero-valued per-model entries when total deleted == 0.
StepsToReproduce: Ensure the database has no rows matching a queryset for a model that is fast-deletable (a model that can use QuerySet._raw_delete). Example: MyFastModel.objects.filter(pk=nonexistent_pk).delete().
Observe the return value from step 1: it may be (0, {'app.MyFastModel': 0}).

Ensure the database has no rows matching a queryset for a model that is deleted via per-instance or non-fast-delete path (e.g., a model with cascading FK deletes). Example: MyRelatedModel.objects.filter(pk=nonexistent_pk).delete().
Observe the return value from step 3: it may be (0, {}).
Compare the two results — one per-model dict is {} while the other contains a zero-valued key for the model label.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When total deleted == 0, the returned tuple should be (0, {}) (i.e., an empty per-model dict) in all cases so the API is consistent and does not include model-label keys with zero counts.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: For zero-total deletions, QuerySet.delete() sometimes returns (0, {}) and sometimes returns (0, {'app.Model': 0}) depending on the code path: fast-delete paths and the single-instance fast-delete shortcut produce zero-valued per-model keys, while non-fast-delete paths produce an empty dict.

hints_text: I guess we could adapt the code not to include any key if the count is zero in the second case.

problem_statement.Title: makemigrations does not validate UniqueConstraint.fields exist (no models.E012; AddConstraint missing FK deps)
problem_statement.Description: When a model adds a UniqueConstraint referring to non-existent fields, makemigrations and the system checks do not validate the constraint field names (no models.E012). This differs from unique_together, which is validated. As a result, invalid constraints slip through makemigrations and AddConstraint operations are emitted without recorded FK dependencies; missing-field errors surface only later at SQL generation/apply time.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption: constraint field names do not require local-field existence validation and can be deferred. Location: django/db/models/base.py::Model._check_constraints. Evidence: _check_constraints performs other checks but never calls cls._check_local_fields for constraint.fields (the check that produces models.E012 for unique_together).

2) Incorrect assumption: MigrationAutodetector can compute added constraints without validating referenced fields or recording FK dependencies. Location: django/db/migrations/autodetector.py::MigrationAutodetector.create_altered_constraints (and generate_added_constraints). Evidence: new constraints are detected (add_constraints = [c for c in new_constraints if c not in old_constraints]) but the code does not call model._meta.get_field on each constraint.fields nor call MigrationAutodetector._get_dependencies_for_foreign_key to collect dependencies before emitting AddConstraint.

3) Incorrect assumption: it's acceptable for UniqueConstraint to defer field existence checks until SQL generation. Location: django/db/models/constraints.py::UniqueConstraint.constraint_sql and create_sql. Evidence/relationship: these methods call model._meta.get_field(field_name) when producing SQL, so missing-field errors occur at apply/SQL time rather than earlier.

4) Incorrect assumption: AddConstraint.state_forwards may append constraints into state without validating fields. Location: django/db/migrations/operations/models.py::AddConstraint.state_forwards. Evidence: state_forwards inserts the cloned constraint into model_state.options and calls state.reload_model(..., delay=True) without validating constraint.fields itself.

5) Related positive precedent (shows expected relationship): for unique_together, MigrationAutodetector._generate_altered_foo_together calls _meta.get_field for each field and uses MigrationAutodetector._get_dependencies_for_foreign_key(field) to collect FK dependencies. Location: django/db/migrations/autodetector.py::_generate_altered_foo_together. Relationship to preserve: when validating/handling constraints, the code should (a) call model._meta.get_field(field_name) for each field in constraint.fields and (b) for FK fields compute dependencies via _get_dependencies_for_foreign_key(field) so those dependency tuples are attached to the AddConstraint operation.

6) Investigation gap (impacting confidence about dependency shape): the exact dependency tuple(s) produced by MigrationAutodetector._get_dependencies_for_foreign_key for a concrete FK field were not traced/recorded in the analysis, so the precise format/content of the dependencies that should be attached to AddConstraint remains unverified. Location: inspection of MigrationAutodetector._get_dependencies_for_foreign_key (not concretely exercised).
StepsToReproduce: Create a Django project and app with a model that defines a UniqueConstraint referencing a non-existent field, e.g. UniqueConstraint(fields=['missing_field'], name='uq_bad').
Run python manage.py makemigrations for the app.
Observe that makemigrations completes without raising models.E012 or failing on the missing field.
(Optional) Replace the UniqueConstraint with equivalent unique_together=['missing_field'] and run python manage.py makemigrations to observe models.E012 being raised for the missing field.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: makemigrations and the system checks should validate that every field named in a UniqueConstraint exists on the model and raise models.E012 if any field is missing. When a constraint references ForeignKey fields, the generated AddConstraint migration operation should include the correct FK dependency tuples so migrations order dependencies are recorded before applying the constraint.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: makemigrations does not validate UniqueConstraint.fields; no models.E012 is raised for constraints that name non-existent fields. AddConstraint operations are emitted without recorded FK dependencies, and missing-field errors are only raised later when SQL is generated or when applying migrations.

problem_statement.Title: QuerySet.distinct() silently ignored after combinator (union/intersection/difference)
problem_statement.Description: Calling QuerySet.distinct() after a combinator (.union/.intersection/.difference) does not raise and has no effect: distinct fields are recorded on the Query object but the SQL generated for combined queries ignores them, so the requested DISTINCT/DISTINCT ON is not applied.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) QuerySet.distinct assumes it can be used after combinator operations. Location: django/db/models/query.py :: QuerySet.distinct — it does not call self._not_support_combined_queries('distinct') and therefore allows callers to set distinct on a combined query.

2) Query.add_distinct_fields permits creating a Query with both a combinator and distinct fields. Location: django/db/models/sql/query.py :: Query.add_distinct_fields — it unconditionally does: self.distinct_fields = field_names; self.distinct = True, with no check for self.combinator, encoding the incorrect assumption that distinct state and combinator state can co-exist.

3) SQLCompiler.as_sql ignores distinct when a combinator is present. Location: django/db/models/sql/compiler.py :: SQLCompiler.as_sql (and get_combinator_sql) — control flow: if self.query.combinator: return get_combinator_sql(); else: distinct_fields, distinct_params = self.get_distinct(); ... build SELECT with DISTINCT/DISTINCT ON. Because the combinator branch is taken, any distinct_fields set on the Query are never used. (Relationship preserved: as_sql selects combinator path when query.combinator is set, bypassing get_distinct.)

4) Combined effect (state/invariant violation): the public API and lower-level query API allow an invalid internal state (query.combinator != None AND query.distinct_fields set) that the compiler ignores, producing a silent no-op rather than an error or applied DISTINCT.

5) Investigative gap: it was not exhaustively validated that no other internal or third-party code paths call Query.add_distinct_fields directly (e.g., raw query helpers or extensions), nor was the alternative approach (having the compiler wrap combinator SQL in an outer SELECT to apply DISTINCT) explored fully. These gaps affect the scope of required changes but do not alter the core incorrect assumptions above.
StepsToReproduce: Create test data: multiple Sample objects (including duplicate names).
qs = Sample.objects.filter(user=self.user)
qs1 = qs.filter(name='Dub').annotate(rank=Value(0, IntegerField()))
qs2 = qs.filter(name='Sam1').annotate(rank=Value(1, IntegerField()))
qs = qs1.union(qs2)
qs = qs.order_by('name').distinct('name')
Assert qs.count() == 2 (observe failure)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When a user calls QuerySet.distinct() after a combinator (union/intersection/difference), the API should not silently ignore the request. The user-visible outcome should be an immediate error indicating that DISTINCT is not supported with combined queries (i.e., a NotSupportedError or equivalent).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling .distinct() after a combinator records distinct fields on the Query object but has no effect on SQL generation: the compiler emits the combinator SQL (e.g. SELECT ... UNION SELECT ...) without an outer DISTINCT/DISTINCT ON, so duplicates are not removed and the call is silently ignored.

hints_text: distinct() is not supported but doesn't raise an error yet. As ​​per the documentation, "only LIMIT, OFFSET, COUNT(*), ORDER BY, and specifying columns (i.e. slicing, count(), order_by(), and values()/values_list()) are allowed on the resulting QuerySet.". Follow up to #27995.

problem_statement.Title: ASGIStaticFilesHandler: TypeError 'NoneType' object is not callable' when serving static files over ASGI
problem_statement.Description: When using django.contrib.staticfiles.handlers.ASGIStaticFilesHandler under ASGI (e.g., daphne), requests for static files cause a TypeError because the ASGI request path awaits an uninitialized middleware chain or missing async response path.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) ASGIStaticFilesHandler.__init__ (django/contrib/staticfiles/handlers.py): skipped calling super().__init__(), encoding the incorrect assumption that omitting the ASGIHandler initialization is safe. This prevented ASGIHandler.__init__ from running and left BaseHandler._middleware_chain unset (None).

2) StaticFilesHandlerMixin.load_middleware (django/contrib/staticfiles/handlers.py): defined as def load_middleware(self): pass, encoding the incorrect assumption that load_middleware requires no arguments. ASGIHandler.__init__ calls load_middleware(is_async=True); the signature mismatch is the reason the wrapper avoided calling super().__init__(). The correct relationship is that load_middleware must accept the is_async parameter when called by ASGIHandler.__init__.

3) StaticFilesHandlerMixin lacks an async response path (django/contrib/staticfiles/handlers.py): encoded is the incorrect assumption that a synchronous get_response(self, request) alone is sufficient for ASGI flows. BaseHandler.get_response_async expects either a middleware chain or an async get_response_async implementation. The investigation specifies the required async relationship/formula to mirror BaseHandler behavior:
   response = await sync_to_async(self.get_response, thread_sensitive=True)(request)
   response._resource_closers.append(request.close)
   return response
   (i.e., get_response_async must run the existing sync get_response via sync_to_async and register request.close on response._resource_closers.)

4) BaseHandler._middleware_chain default state and expectation (django/core/handlers/base.py): BaseHandler defines _middleware_chain = None and get_response_async does "response = await self._middleware_chain(request)"; the code incorrectly assumes _middleware_chain will be initialized before get_response_async is called. Because ASGIHandler.__init__ did not run (see #1) and the mixin's load_middleware is a no-op (see #2), _middleware_chain remained None and awaiting it produced the TypeError seen in the traceback.
StepsToReproduce: Configure Django to use django.contrib.staticfiles.handlers.ASGIStaticFilesHandler as the ASGI application (or otherwise instantiate and expose ASGIStaticFilesHandler via an ASGI server).
Start an ASGI server (example: daphne) pointing at that ASGI application.
Make an HTTP GET request for a static asset (e.g., GET /static/<existing-file>).
Observe the server raises TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not callable with a traceback showing get_response_async awaiting self._middleware_chain.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Static file requests served via the ASGI handler complete successfully and return a valid HTTP response (e.g., 200 FileResponse) without raising internal TypeError exceptions; request resource cleanup is performed after the response is returned.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: ASGI requests for static files cause the ASGI application to raise TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not callable. The traceback shows django/core/handlers/asgi.py calling await self.get_response_async(request) which in turn attempts to await self._middleware_chain(request) where _middleware_chain is None, and the file is not served.

problem_statement.Title: django.utils.text.slugify preserves leading underscores and trailing hyphens
problem_statement.Description: slugify(value) in django/utils/text.py leaves leading '_' characters and trailing '-' characters in the returned slug (e.g. "___This is a test ---" -> "___this-is-a-test-").
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption in django/utils/text.py::slugify(value, allow_unicode=False): the code assumes .strip() (whitespace-only) plus collapsing separators is sufficient to remove leading/trailing separators. Implementation sequence: value = re.sub(r"[^\w\s-]", "", value.lower()).strip() ; return re.sub(r"[-\s]+", "-", value). This preserves leading '_' and trailing '-'.

2) Regex assumption encoded in django/utils/text.py: r"[^\w\s-]" is treated as removing separator characters, but \w includes '_' so leading underscores survive the first substitution. The code relies on that regex and therefore preserves '_' from input.

3) Separator-collapsing assumption encoded in django/utils/text.py: re.sub(r"[-\s]+", "-", value) collapses runs of hyphens/whitespace but does not remove leading/trailing '-' produced or left in value. The final returned value is exactly re.sub(r"[-\s]+", "-", value), which can start or end with '-' or '_' depending on input.

4) Call-site inheritance: django/template/defaultfilters.py::slugify delegates directly to django.utils.text.slugify, so the same incorrect assumptions are propagated to the template filter (same location encodes the dependency).

5) Test coverage assumption encoded in tests/utils_tests/test_text.py and tests/template_tests/filter_tests/test_slugify.py: current tests do not include inputs with leading underscores or trailing hyphens, implicitly accepting existing behavior; this missing test coverage encodes an assumption that such inputs are handled correctly.

6) Correct final-value relationship (identified by investigation and necessary for diagnosis): the slug result should be computed by first removing disallowed chars and collapsing separators, then removing leading/trailing separator characters. Expressed as: value = re.sub(r"[^\w\s-]", "", value.lower()).strip(); value = re.sub(r"[-\s]+", "-", value); final_value = value.strip("-_").
StepsToReproduce: Start a Python shell with Django importable.
from django.utils import text
text.slugify("___This is a test ---")
Observe the returned string.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: slugify("___This is a test ---") returns "this-is-a-test" (no leading underscores, no trailing hyphens). The template filter defaultfilters.slugify shows the same behavior.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: slugify("___This is a test ---") returns "___this-is-a-test-": leading underscores from the input are preserved and a trailing hyphen remains. The same behavior is observed via the template slugify filter because it delegates to utils.slugify.

hints_text: The current version of the patch converts all underscores to dashes which (as discussed on the PR) isn't an obviously desired change. A discussion is needed to see if there's consensus about that change.

problem_statement.Title: Query.filter raises NotSupportedError when RHS object has instance attribute 'filterable' (e.g. model field named filterable)
problem_statement.Description: When calling QuerySet.filter(...) where the RHS value is an arbitrary object that has an instance attribute named 'filterable' (for example, a Django model instance with a BooleanField named filterable=False), Django raises NotSupportedError claiming the object's type is disallowed in the filter clause. This occurs during filter construction (Query.build_filter -> Query.check_filterable).
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Query.check_filterable performs an instance-level lookup for the flag using getattr(expression, 'filterable', True) in django/db/models/sql/query.py::Query.check_filterable. This treats objects with an instance attribute named 'filterable' (e.g. model instances where a BooleanField sets an instance attribute) as non-filterable, which is incorrect.

2) The intended semantics are that 'filterable' is a class-level marker on expression classes (e.g. BaseExpression.filterable = True; Window.filterable = False in django/db/models/expressions.py). The check should rely on the expression class-level attribute rather than an arbitrary instance attribute. Expressed relationship: class-level value is accessed as getattr(type(expression), 'filterable', True) (the investigation identifies this as the correct source-of-truth).

3) Query.build_filter and related call sites (django/db/models/sql/query.py::Query.build_filter and Query._add_q) call Query.check_filterable for both the referenced expression and the RHS value. Because check_filterable reads the instance attribute, passing a model instance with filterable=False as the RHS causes check_filterable to raise NotSupportedError with message '<ClassName> is disallowed in the filter clause.'
StepsToReproduce: Define a model type that includes a BooleanField named 'filterable' (e.g. ProductMetaDataType with filterable = models.BooleanField(default=False)).
Define a model having a ForeignKey to that type (e.g. ProductMetaData with metadata_type = ForeignKey(ProductMetaDataType)).
Create an instance of the type with filterable=False (e.g. brand_metadata = ProductMetaDataType.objects.create(label='brand', filterable=False)).
Run a filter using that instance as the RHS: ProductMetaData.objects.filter(value='Dark Vador', metadata_type=brand_metadata).
Observe the NotSupportedError raised during filter construction.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Filtering by a model instance value (RHS) should return a queryset of matching objects (or an empty queryset) without raising NotSupportedError simply because the model instance has an attribute named 'filterable'. Only expression classes that explicitly mark themselves as non-filterable at the class level should be disallowed from use in WHERE clauses.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: A NotSupportedError is raised during filter construction with message '<ProductMetaDataType> is disallowed in the filter clause.' when the RHS object has an instance attribute 'filterable=False' (for example, a model instance whose BooleanField is named 'filterable').

--- a/django/db/models/sql/compiler.py

@@ -727,7 +727,12 @@ def find_ordering_name(self, name, opts, alias=None, default_order='ASC',
         # If we get to this point and the field is a relation to another model,
         # append the default ordering for that model unless it is the pk
         # shortcut or the attribute name of the field that is specified.
-        if field.is_relation and opts.ordering and getattr(field, 'attname', None) != name and name != 'pk':
+        if (
+            field.is_relation and
+            opts.ordering and
+            getattr(field, 'attname', None) != pieces[-1] and
+            name != 'pk'
+        ):
             # Firstly, avoid infinite loops.
             already_seen = already_seen or set()
             join_tuple = tuple(getattr(self.query.alias_map[j], 'join_cols', None) for j in joins)

problem_statement.Title: order_by("record__root_id") appends related model ordering and adds extra JOIN due to attname comparison bug in SQLCompiler.find_ordering_name
problem_statement.Description: Calling order_by() with a multi-part lookup that terminates on a field attname on a related model (e.g. "record__root_id") results in an unexpected extra JOIN and uses the related model's Meta.ordering (including its direction) rather than the explicit ordering requested. The problem manifests in the generated SQL ORDER BY and JOINs.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption: SQLCompiler.find_ordering_name assumes the full ordering lookup string (name, e.g. "record__root_id") can be compared directly to a field's attname (e.g. "root_id"). Location: django/db/models/sql/compiler.py:SQLCompiler.find_ordering_name. Consequence: the attname check always fails for prefixed lookups and triggers appending the related model's opts.ordering.

2) Incorrect 'pk' special-case: the code compares the full lookup name to the 'pk' literal (name != 'pk') instead of comparing the terminal lookup token. Location: django/db/models/sql/compiler.py:SQLCompiler.find_ordering_name. Consequence: the 'pk' shortcut is not recognized when used as a terminal token in a multi-part lookup and contributes to the same mis-detection.

3) Wrong conditional logic that produces the symptom: the failing condition causes opts.ordering from the related model to be appended to ORDER BY and may introduce an extra LEFT OUTER JOIN and the related model's ordering direction into the final SQL. Location where opts.ordering is appended: django/db/models/sql/compiler.py:SQLCompiler.find_ordering_name (called by SQLCompiler.get_order_by).

4) Correct tokenization already exists elsewhere: Query.setup_joins / names_to_path parse lookups into tokens and resolve the terminal field name (pieces[-1]) and the 'pk' mapping. Location: django/db/models/sql/query.py:Query.setup_joins and Query.names_to_path. Relationship/formula that must hold: when evaluating an ordering lookup name, use the terminal token pieces[-1] for attname and 'pk' checks. I.e. replace the existing check
    getattr(field, 'attname', None) != name and name != 'pk'
with
    last_piece = pieces[-1]; getattr(field, 'attname', None) != last_piece and last_piece != 'pk'

5) Scope: the incorrect comparison appears only in SQLCompiler.find_ordering_name (single-site), so fixing the comparison to use the final lookup token will prevent improper appending of related model opts.ordering across compilers that inherit SQLCompiler.
StepsToReproduce:
Define models:
class OneModel(models.Model):
    class Meta:
        ordering = ("-id",)
    id = models.BigAutoField(primary_key=True)
    root = models.ForeignKey("OneModel", on_delete=models.CASCADE, null=True)
    oneval = models.BigIntegerField(null=True)

class TwoModel(models.Model):
    id = models.BigAutoField(primary_key=True)
    record = models.ForeignKey(OneModel, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
    twoval = models.BigIntegerField(null=True)

Run queryset and print SQL:
qs = TwoModel.objects.filter(record__oneval__in=[1,2,3]).order_by("record__root_id")
print(qs.query)
-- Observe an extra LEFT OUTER JOIN and ORDER BY using the related model alias (e.g. ORDER BY T3."id" DESC).

Compare with explicit deep lookup:
qs = TwoModel.objects.filter(record__oneval__in=[1,2,3]).order_by("record__root__id")
print(qs.query)
-- Observe no extra JOIN and ORDER BY "orion_onemodel"."root_id" ASC (correct single JOIN).

Optional workaround to show expected SQL:
qs = TwoModel.objects.filter(record__oneval__in=[1,2,3]).annotate(root_id=F("record__root_id")).order_by("root_id")
print(qs.query)
-- Observe single JOIN and ORDER BY on the annotated column.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When ordering by a related field attname using a multi-part lookup (e.g. order_by("record__root_id") or order_by("-record__root_id")): the ORM should generate SQL that orders by the terminal column (record.root_id) in the direction explicitly requested, should not append the related model's Meta.ordering, and should not introduce an extra JOIN beyond those required to reference the terminal column.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Ordering by a multi-part lookup that ends on a related field attname (e.g. order_by("record__root_id") or order_by("record__root")) can produce SQL that: 1) includes an unnecessary extra LEFT OUTER JOIN to the related table, and 2) appends the related model's Meta.ordering (including its ASC/DESC direction) to the ORDER BY. Example: order_by("record__root_id") produced ORDER BY T3."id" DESC (extra JOIN and DESC from related model Meta.ordering) while order_by("record__root__id") produced the expected single-JOIN ORDER BY "orion_onemodel"."root_id" ASC.

hints_text:
This is with a postgres backend. Fairly vanilla Django. Some generic middleware installed (cors, csrf, auth, session). Apps are: INSTALLED_APPS = ( "django.contrib.contenttypes", "django.contrib.auth", "django.contrib.admin", "django.contrib.sessions", "django.contrib.messages", "django.contrib.staticfiles", "corsheaders", "<ours>" )
Something is definitely wrong here. order_by('record__root_id') should result in ORDER BY root_id order_by('record__root') should use OneModel.Meta.ordering because that's what the root foreign key points to ​as documented. That should result in ORDER BY root_join.id DESC order_by('record__root__id') should result in ORDER BY root_join.id
Thanks for this report. A potential fix could be: diff --git a/django/db/models/sql/compiler.py b/django/db/models/sql/compiler.py index abbb1e37cb..a8f5b61fbe 100644 --- a/django/db/models/sql/compiler.py +++ b/django/db/models/sql/compiler.py @@ -727,7 +727,7 @@ class SQLCompiler: # If we get to this point and the field is a relation to another model, # append the default ordering for that model unless it is the pk # shortcut or the attribute name of the field that is specified. - if field.is_relation and opts.ordering and getattr(field, 'attname', None) != name and name != 'pk': + if field.is_relation and opts.ordering and getattr(field, 'attname', None) != pieces[-1] and name != 'pk': # Firstly, avoid infinite loops. already_seen = already_seen or set() join_tuple = tuple(getattr(self.query.alias_map[j], 'join_cols', None) for j in joins) but I didn't check this in details.
FWIW, I did apply the suggested fix to my local version and I do now get what looks like correct behavior and results that match what Simon suggested should be the sort results. Also my annotate "workaround" continues to behave correctly. Looks promising.
Jack, would you like to prepare a patch?
​PR
I did some additional work on this to verify the scope of the change. I added the following test code on master and ran the entire test suite: +++ b/django/db/models/sql/compiler.py @@ -727,6 +727,11 @@ class SQLCompiler: # If we get to this point and the field is a relation to another model, # append the default ordering for that model unless it is the pk # shortcut or the attribute name of the field that is specified. + if (field.is_relation and opts.ordering and name != 'pk' and + ((getattr(field, 'attname', None) != name) != + (getattr(field, 'attname', None) != pieces[-1]))): + print(f"JJD <{getattr(field, 'attname', '')}> <{name}> <{pieces[-1]}>") + breakpoint() if field.is_relation and opts.ordering and getattr(field, 'attname', None) != name and name != 'pk': # Firstly, avoid infinite loops. already_seen = already_seen or set() The idea being to display every time that the change from name to pieces[-1] in the code would make a difference in the execution. Of course verified that when running the reproducer one does go into the test block and outputs: JJD <root_id> <record__root_id> <root_id>. The code is not triggered for any other test across the entire test suite, so the scope of the change is not causing unexpected changes in other places. This seems reassuring.

--- a/django/db/models/sql/query.py
+++ b/django/db/models/sql/query.py
@@ -305,6 +305,7 @@ def clone(self):
             obj.annotation_select_mask = None
         else:
             obj.annotation_select_mask = self.annotation_select_mask.copy()
+        obj.combined_queries = tuple(query.clone() for query in self.combined_queries)
         # _annotation_select_cache cannot be copied, as doing so breaks the
         # (necessary) state in which both annotations and
         # _annotation_select_cache point to the same underlying objects.
@@ -1777,6 +1778,8 @@ def split_exclude(self, filter_expr, can_reuse, names_with_path):
 
     def set_empty(self):
         self.where.add(NothingNode(), AND)
+        for query in self.combined_queries:
+            query.set_empty()
 
     def is_empty(self):
         return any(isinstance(c, NothingNode) for c in self.where.children)

problem_statement.Title: QuerySet.none() on combined QuerySets (union/intersection/difference) is not truly empty; empty form submission adds all matching objects
problem_statement.Description: When a QuerySet is built using combinator set operations (union(), intersection(), difference()) and then QuerySet.none() is called, the resulting QuerySet is treated as empty at the Query.where level but still contains combinator metadata that causes the SQL compiler to include the original subqueries. In a form using such a queryset (e.g. ModelMultipleChoiceField), submitting the form empty can cause all objects matching the original combined query to be added instead of none.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/db/models/query.py::QuerySet.none — incorrect assumption: calling clone.query.set_empty() (adding a NothingNode to the where clause) is sufficient to make a previously-combinator QuerySet truly empty. In reality none() leaves clone.query.combinator and clone.query.combined_queries referencing the original non-empty subqueries. (Attributes implicated: query.combinator, query.combined_queries, Query.set_empty())

2) django/db/models/sql/compiler.py::SQLCompiler.get_combinator_sql — compiler behavior: it builds combinator SQL from the combined_queries list using the pattern: [subquery.get_compiler(...) for subquery in self.query.combined_queries if not subquery.is_empty()]. Because the compiler inspects self.query.combined_queries (and checks is_empty() on those subqueries) it will include SQL for subqueries still present in combined_queries even if the outer Query.where contains a NothingNode.

3) django/db/models/sql/query.py::Query.set_empty and ::Query.is_empty — relationship: Query.set_empty inserts a NothingNode into self.where and Query.is_empty returns true when where contains NothingNode. This is insufficient when combinator metadata (combined_queries) remain, because the compiler does not rely solely on outer query.is_empty() when combinator is present.

4) django/forms/models.py::ModelMultipleChoiceField.clean — incorrect expectation: returning self.queryset.none() from clean() will yield an iterable that evaluates to no objects. Because none() does not clear combinator/composed queries, the returned queryset can still produce results when iterated.

5) django/db/models/fields/related_descriptors.py::ManyRelatedManager.set — behavior that exposes the bug: set() forces evaluation of the provided iterable via objs = tuple(objs), so an incorrectly non-empty queryset returned from clean() is iterated, triggering SQL compilation of the still-present combined subqueries and causing all matching objects to be added.
StepsToReproduce: Define models: Publication and Article with publications = ManyToManyField(Publication, blank=True, null=True).

Create a ModelForm (or admin form) with a ModelMultipleChoiceField using a combined queryset built by union(), for example: Publication.objects.filter(id__lt=2).union(Publication.objects.filter(id__gt=5)).
Open the form and submit it without selecting any publications (empty submission, required=False).
Save the form (or save the Article instance).
Observe that all Publication objects matching the combined queryset have been added to the Article's publications relation.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Submitting the form without selecting any publications should result in no publications being associated with the Article (no objects added).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Submitting the form empty results in all Publication objects that match the original combined queryset (the union/intersection/difference) being added to the Article's publications relation.

hints_text: Thanks for the report. QuerySet.none() doesn't work properly on combined querysets, it returns all results instead of an empty queryset.
