problem_statement.Title: TypeError: cannot pickle 'dict_keys' when combining Q objects with | operator
problem_statement.Description: Applying the | operator to a Q object that contains a non-pickleable leaf value (e.g. dict_keys) raises TypeError: "cannot pickle 'dict_keys' object". The exception occurs when Q._combine attempts to duplicate an operand using copy.deepcopy.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption in django/db/models/query_utils.py:Q._combine: the method returns copy.deepcopy(self) when other is empty (and copy.deepcopy(other) when self is empty). This encodes the assumption that a deep copy of a Q's children is always safe (file: django/db/models/query_utils.py, class Q, method _combine). Formula encoded: if not other -> return copy.deepcopy(self); elif not self -> return copy.deepcopy(other).

2) Incorrect assumption in django/utils/tree.py:Node.__deepcopy__: Node.__deepcopy__ calls copy.deepcopy(self.children, memodict) and therefore assumes every child is deepcopyable/pickleable. When a child is a non-pickleable object (e.g. dict_keys), copy.deepcopy(Q_instance) triggers a TypeError here (file: django/utils/tree.py, class Node, method __deepcopy__).

3) Mismatch between Q and intended tree-node cloning semantics: Q relies on Node.__deepcopy__ deep-copying raw children, but the safe pattern demonstrated by django/db/models/sql/where.py:WhereNode.clone is to recursively clone node children and append raw leaf children unchanged. The Q implementation does not follow this pattern (file: django/db/models/sql/where.py, class WhereNode, method clone vs django/db/models/query_utils.py, class Q).

4) Presence of non-deepcopyable leaf values in Q.children (example: Q(x__in={}.keys()) contains a dict_keys leaf) invalidates the assumptions in (1) and (2), producing the observed TypeError at runtime. (evidence: reproduction in original report and Node.__deepcopy__ behavior).

5) Risk/gap: other call-sites that perform copy.deepcopy(Q_instance) or rely on Node.__deepcopy__ semantics were not exhaustively audited; changing Q's duplication behavior may affect callers that expect deep-copied leaf values (note: FilteredRelation.__init__ uses a default condition=Q() which can propagate Q instances at import time).
StepsToReproduce: Open a Python shell with Django available.
>>> from django.db.models import Q
Q: (AND: ('x__in', dict_keys([])))
Traceback (most recent call last): ... TypeError: cannot pickle 'dict_keys' object
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Combining Q objects with the | operator should succeed even when a Q contains non-pickleable leaf values (such as dict_keys). The result should be a Q object representing the combined condition, not a TypeError.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Q() | Q(x__in={}.keys()) raises TypeError: cannot pickle 'dict_keys' object (originating from copy.deepcopy on Q which triggers Node.__deepcopy__ to deepcopy children).

hints_text: Thanks for this report. Regression in bb0b6e526340e638522e093765e534df4e4393d2.

problem_statement.Title: Q() & Exists(...) raises TypeError; Q/Exists boolean combine is not commutative
problem_statement.Description: Combining a Q() object on the left with an Exists(...) expression on the right (Q() & Exists(...)) raises a TypeError, while the reverse order (Exists(...) & Q()) succeeds. The boolean/bitwise combinators (& and |) are not behaving commutatively for Q versus conditional Expression operands.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect type assumption in Q._combine — django/db/models/query_utils.py::Q._combine enforces if not isinstance(other, Q): raise TypeError(other). This encodes the assumption that the right-hand operand must already be a Q instance and rejects expression-like conditional operands (e.g. Exists).

2) Asymmetry explained by Combinable.__and__ wrapping conditional expressions — django/db/models/expressions.py::Combinable.__and__ contains the behavior: if getattr(self, 'conditional', False) and getattr(other, 'conditional', False): return Q(self) & Q(other). Because Combinable.__and__ wraps conditional expressions into Q on the left-hand path, Exists & Q works, exposing the asymmetry when Q._combine refuses non-Q operands on the right.

3) Exists is a boolean/conditional Expression and therefore should be combinable with Q — django/db/models/expressions.py::Exists sets output_field = fields.BooleanField() and implements expression semantics (resolve_expression). The correct relationship is that conditional Expressions (those with boolean output_field / conditional semantics) are valid operands for boolean combinations and can be treated as Q(expression) when combining.

4) Node internals accept Qs containing Expression children — django/utils/tree.py::Node.add and related Q/Node handling accept children that are expressions or Q nodes; wrapping an Expression into Q(other) is compatible with Node internals. This means the rejection in Q._combine is unnecessary and not justified by Node limitations.

5) Mirror/missing methods do not explain the bug — Combinable.__rand__ / __ror__ behaviors and existing Combinable.__and__ behavior show that a local change in Q._combine (to accept/wrap conditional Expressions) is the needed correction; the hardcoded isinstance(other, Q) check is the concrete faulty assumption.
StepsToReproduce: Open Django shell / interactive environment with the project models loaded.
From django.db.models.expressions import Exists; from django.db.models import Q; from myapp.models import Product (or equivalent model).
Evaluate: Exists(Product.objects.all()) & Q()  # succeeds and returns a combined Q.
Evaluate: Q() & Exists(Product.objects.all())  # raises TypeError: <django.db.models.expressions.Exists object at ...>.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Boolean combination operators (& and |) between Q objects and boolean/conditional Expression operands (such as Exists) are commutative: both Q() & Exists(...) and Exists(...) & Q() should return equivalent combined Q/where objects representing the same boolean combination, not raise TypeError.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Exists(...) & Q() returns a combined Q object, but Q() & Exists(...) raises TypeError because Q._combine rejects the Exists object (TypeError is raised with the Exists instance as the argument).

hints_text:
Reproduced on 3.1.6. The exception is raised by this two lines in the Q._combine, which are not present in the Combinable._combine from which Exists inherit. if not isinstance(other, Q): raise TypeError(other)

+            'app_names=%r, namespaces=%r, route=%r)' % (

+                self.app_names, self.namespaces, self.route,
+            )

 @override_settings(ROOT_URLCONF='urlpatterns_reverse.erroneous_urls')
 class ErroneousViewTests(SimpleTestCase):

problem_statement.Title: ResolverMatch.__repr__ shows functools.partial as 'functools.partial' and omits partial pre-bound args/kwargs
problem_statement.Description: When a functools.partial object is used as the resolved view, ResolverMatch reports the function path as 'functools.partial' and __repr__ does not include the partial's pre-bound args/kwargs. This makes debugging output (repr(resolve(...)), URL display, and some debug pages unhelpful.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Mis-classification of functools.partial in ResolverMatch.__init__ (django/urls/resolvers.py:ResolverMatch.__init__): code assumes the passed callable should be represented by func.__module__ + '.' + func.__name__ when hasattr(func, '__name__') else func.__class__.__module__ + '.' + func.__class__.__name__. functools.partial lacks __name__, so _func_path becomes 'functools.partial' instead of the underlying function's dotted path.

2) __repr__ omits partial's pre-bound args/kwargs (django/urls/resolvers.py:ResolverMatch.__repr__): code formats only self.args and self.kwargs for display and does not include functools.partial.args / functools.partial.keywords, so pre-bound arguments are invisible in repr.

3) Runtime contract must be preserved (django/core/handlers/base.py:BaseHandler._get_response and django/urls/base.py:reverse): ResolverMatch.__getitem__ must continue to yield (func, args, kwargs) suitable for invocation; therefore any handling of partials for display must not permanently merge partial's pre-bound args/kwargs into self.args/self.kwargs used at runtime.

4) Existing, inconsistent precedent (django/urls/resolvers.py:URLPattern.lookup_str): URLPattern.lookup_str already special-cases functools.partial by unwrapping callback.func for display. ResolverMatch behavior is inconsistent with this existing logic, causing divergent display output.

5) Debug consumers still inspect resolver_match.func directly (django/views/debug.py:technical_404_response): code that derives caller name from resolver_match.func via __name__ / __class__.__name__ will continue to show 'functools.partial' unless those consumers prefer ResolverMatch-provided function-path metadata.

6) Missing test coverage for partial in repr (tests/urlpatterns_reverse/tests.py::test_repr absent partial case): there is a test for normal function repr but no test asserting correct repr when the view is a functools.partial, allowing this behavior to remain unnoticed.

7) Precise relationships/formulas observed and required for correct display (evidence/formula):
   - When func is functools.partial: unwrapped = func.func
   - Record partial pre-bound values: self._partial_args = func.args or (), self._partial_kwargs = func.keywords or {}
   - For display only (do not mutate runtime args): display_args = self._partial_args + tuple(self.args)
   - For display only: display_kwargs = {**self._partial_kwargs, **self.kwargs}
   - Compute _func_path from unwrapped: if not hasattr(unwrapped, '__name__'): _func_path = unwrapped.__class__.__module__ + '.' + unwrapped.__class__.__name__ else: _func_path = unwrapped.__module__ + '.' + unwrapped.__name__
StepsToReproduce: In a Django project, define a normal view function, e.g. def view(request, a, b): pass
Create a functools.partial wrapping the view with pre-bound args/kwargs, e.g. partial_view = functools.partial(view, 1, b=2)
Add a URL pattern that uses partial_view as the view and resolve that URL, e.g. match = resolve('/path/to/that/url/')
Print or inspect repr(match) or print(match) and observe the function display and shown args/kwargs
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When a functools.partial is used as the resolved view, debug/display output should show the underlying function's dotted path (module.function) rather than 'functools.partial', and the representation should include both the partial's pre-bound args/kwargs and the URL-resolved args/kwargs merged for human-readable inspection.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The function path is shown as 'functools.partial' (the partial object's class name) and the __repr__ output shows only the URL-resolved args/kwargs, omitting any pre-bound arguments/keywords from the functools.partial.

problem_statement.Title: DEFAULT_AUTO_FIELD rejects user subclasses of BigAutoField/SmallAutoField
problem_statement.Description: When DEFAULT_AUTO_FIELD is set to a user-defined field class that subclasses models.BigAutoField or models.SmallAutoField, Django raises a ValueError stating the class must subclass AutoField during app/model import.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/db/models/fields/__init__.py :: AutoFieldMeta.__subclasscheck__ encodes an incorrect identity check: it uses `subclass in self._subclasses` (observed as `return subclass in self._subclasses or super().__subclasscheck__(subclass)`). This assumes only the exact classes in `_subclasses` are valid AutoField subclasses; it therefore returns False for user subclasses like `class MyBigAutoField(models.BigAutoField): ...`.

2) django/db/models/options.py :: Options._get_default_pk_class performs `if not issubclass(pk_class, AutoField): raise ValueError(...)`. That call relies on `issubclass(..., AutoField)` producing True for classes that semantically inherit from AutoField (including subclasses of BigAutoField/SmallAutoField). Because AutoFieldMeta.__subclasscheck__ implements identity membership, valid user subclasses are rejected and the ValueError is raised.

3) django/db/models/fields/__init__.py :: AutoFieldMeta._subclasses defines the tuple `(BigAutoField, SmallAutoField)` as the canonical built-in auto-field base classes. The tuple itself represents the correct set of base classes, but when combined with the identity check in __subclasscheck__ it is treated as an exact set rather than as base classes for transitive subclassing.

4) django/db/models/fields/__init__.py :: AutoFieldMeta.__instancecheck__ already uses `isinstance(instance, self._subclasses)` and therefore accepts instances of subclasses. This shows an inconsistency between instance-level checks (which accept subclasses via isinstance with a tuple) and class-level checks (which currently only accept exact class objects via `in`), and identifies the class-level check as the incorrect assumption location.

(Notes from investigation: the failing expression at runtime is the combination of `pk_class = import_string(pk_class_path)` followed by `issubclass(pk_class, AutoField)`, where `issubclass` delegates to AutoFieldMeta.__subclasscheck__.)
StepsToReproduce: In Django settings, set DEFAULT_AUTO_FIELD = "example.core.models.MyBigAutoField".
Run a Django command that imports apps (for example, `./manage.py runserver` or `./manage.py check`).
Observe the import-time crash with a ValueError about DEFAULT_AUTO_FIELD not subclassing AutoField.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Django accepts a DEFAULT_AUTO_FIELD value that points to a user-defined class which subclasses models.BigAutoField or models.SmallAutoField; app/model import completes normally and models are prepared without raising a ValueError.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: During app/model import Django raises: "ValueError: Primary key 'example.core.models.MyBigAutoField' referred by DEFAULT_AUTO_FIELD must subclass AutoField." Models fail to load and Django crashes at startup.

problem_statement.Title: django-admin startapp fails when target directory ends with trailing slash
problem_statement.Description: When a user supplies a target path that ends with a trailing path separator (e.g. "directory/" via shell tab-completion), django-admin startapp (and startproject) raises CommandError because the code extracts an empty basename and treats it as the app directory name.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption: os.path.basename(target) always yields the intended directory name. Location: django/core/management/templates.py, TemplateCommand.handle (call: self.validate_name(os.path.basename(target), 'directory')). Evidence: os.path.basename('directory/') -> '' which is passed to validate_name and triggers the observed CommandError. Correct relationship for extracting the name is: os.path.basename(os.path.normpath(target)) (minimal alternative: os.path.basename(target.rstrip(os.sep)) but rstrip(os.sep) is not robust for mixed separators on Windows).

2) Incorrect assumption: TemplateCommand.validate_name will only receive a non-empty identifier. Location: django/core/management/templates.py, TemplateCommand.validate_name. Evidence: validate_name enforces name.isidentifier() and rejects '' (the empty string produced by basename on a trailing-separator path), which causes the user-visible CommandError.

3) Scope/propagation: The faulty basename extraction in TemplateCommand.handle is the single source affecting multiple commands that delegate to it. Location: django/core/management/commands/startapp.py and django/core/management/commands/startproject.py (both pass the target into TemplateCommand.handle), so both commands exhibit the failure.

4) Missing defensive/normalization handling vs. other modules: TemplateCommand.handle lacks the defensive check or normalization used elsewhere. Location: comparison with django/core/files/utils.py which explicitly guards empty basenames (if os.path.basename(name) in {'', '.', '..'}: raise SuspiciousFileOperation). Evidence: other modules handle empty basename defensively, but TemplateCommand.handle does not.

5) Cross-platform gap in proposed minimal fix: Using target.rstrip(os.sep) is insufficient on Windows if the user supplies '/' as separator; normalization must account for mixed separators. Location: analysis of fix alternatives in django/core/management/templates.py; recommended canonical relationship uses os.path.normpath(target) before basename.

6) Testing gap: There is no regression test covering a valid existing directory provided with a trailing separator. Location: tests/admin_scripts/tests.py (tests exercise invalid names and other cases but omit the trailing-slash valid-directory case), so regressions may go undetected.
StepsToReproduce: Create an existing directory named 'myapp' inside a project (e.g., mkdir myapp).
Run the startapp command with a trailing slash: django-admin startapp myapp/  (or python manage.py startapp myapp/).
Observe the command fail with a validation error.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The startapp (and startproject) command accepts a valid existing directory path whether or not it ends with a trailing path separator and proceeds to create files for the app/project without raising a validation error about the directory name.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: CommandError is raised with the message: "'' is not a valid app directory. Please make sure the directory is a valid identifier." — caused by os.path.basename(target) returning the empty string when target ends with a trailing separator.

 class AdminPasswordChangeFormTest(TestDataMixin, TestCase):

problem_statement.Title: Label for ReadOnlyPasswordHashWidget points to non-labelable element
problem_statement.Description: In the admin / user-change form, the <label> for the ReadOnlyPasswordHashWidget includes a for="id_..." attribute that points at a non-labelable element (the widget renders static text, e.g. a <div>), producing an invalid label association.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) ReadOnlyPasswordHashWidget (django/contrib/auth/forms.py::ReadOnlyPasswordHashWidget) sets read_only = True and renders static summary markup (tests show a <div id="id_password">) but does not override id_for_label. Incorrect assumption: the widget is labelable.  

2) Base Widget.id_for_label (django/forms/widgets.py::Widget.id_for_label) returns the supplied id_ by default. Incorrect assumption encoded: every widget maps 1:1 to a single labelable control, so returning id_ signals a label 'for' is appropriate. (Relationship: default id_for_label(id_) -> return id_.)  

3) BoundField.label_tag (django/forms/boundfield.py::BoundField.label_tag) uses the widget-provided id_for_label to decide whether to emit a for attribute. Exact relationship used: id_ = widget.attrs.get('id') or self.auto_id; id_for_label = widget.id_for_label(id_); if id_for_label: emit for="...". Because Widget.id_for_label returns id_ by default, BoundField.label_tag emits for="id_password" even when the widget renders non-labelable markup.  

4) AdminReadonlyField (django/contrib/admin/helpers.py::AdminReadonlyField) does special-case read-only widgets by avoiding the for-logic, but ReadOnlyPasswordHashWidget is also used in contexts (e.g. UserChangeForm.password in django/contrib/auth/forms.py) where the normal BoundField.label_tag path runs. Incorrect assumption exposed: presence of admin read-only handling guarantees no invalid for attribute; in practice the widget is rendered via BoundField.label_tag in some paths.  

5) Investigation gap: the template auth/widgets/read_only_password_hash.html was not located in the checkout used by the investigator; tests assert the widget renders a <div id="id_password"> but the template contents should be confirmed to verify it contains no labelable element. This gap does not change the logical relationships above but should be verified.
StepsToReproduce: In a Django project with contrib.auth enabled, open the admin User change page for a user and inspect the HTML for the password field label (or instantiate a Form using ReadOnlyPasswordHashField and call f['password'].label_tag()).
Observe the generated <label> element for the password field and note whether it contains a for="id_password" attribute.
Inspect the element with that id and confirm it is a non-labelable element (e.g. <div id="id_password">...).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The password field's label should not include a for attribute when the widget does not render a labelable control; the label should be rendered without an associated for="..." attribute.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The label includes for="id_password" (or similar) pointing at an element rendered by ReadOnlyPasswordHashWidget (tests show a <div id="id_password">), creating a for attribute that references a non-labelable element.

problem_statement.Title: BoundWidget.id_for_label ignores per-option id produced by ChoiceWidget (labels point to wrong id for CheckboxSelectMultiple)
problem_statement.Description: When rendering subwidgets (e.g. CheckboxSelectMultiple), the label 'for' attribute is synthesized by BoundWidget.id_for_label using a fixed pattern and ignores the per-option id placed into the subwidget data['attrs']['id'] by the widget layer. This causes label 'for' attributes to reference a different id than the corresponding input element.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) BoundWidget always synthesizes the label id and ignores widget-provided ids: django/forms/boundfield.py::BoundWidget.id_for_label returns the hardcoded pattern 'id_%s_%s' % (self.data['name'], self.data['index')], encoding the incorrect assumption that the canonical id must be rebuilt from name and index instead of using any id already present in self.data['attrs']['id'].

2) BoundField.subwidgets supplies a base id expecting the widget layer to compute per-option ids: django/forms/boundfield.py::BoundField.subwidgets sets id_ = self.field.widget.attrs.get('id') or self.auto_id and passes attrs = {'id': id_} into widget.subwidgets, encoding the expectation that the widget will place the final per-option id into each option's data['attrs']['id'] (but BoundWidget ignores that).  Formula: id_ = self.field.widget.attrs.get('id') or self.auto_id; attrs = {'id': id_} if id_ else {}.

3) ChoiceWidget produces authoritative per-option ids by transforming the passed base id: django/forms/widgets.py::ChoiceWidget.create_option sets option_attrs = self.build_attrs(self.attrs, attrs) and then, when 'id' in option_attrs, does option_attrs['id'] = self.id_for_label(option_attrs['id'], index). ChoiceWidget.id_for_label applies the relationship (when id_ and add_id_index is True): id_ = '%s_%s' % (id_, index). Therefore option_attrs['id'] is already the final per-option id and must be consumed as authoritative.

4) Re-delegating to widget.id_for_label on an already-computed id would double-suffix the id: calling parent_widget.id_for_label(option_attrs['id'], index) after ChoiceWidget already produced option_attrs['id'] would append the index again (example: base 'prefix_field' -> ChoiceWidget produces 'prefix_field_0'; re-calling would produce 'prefix_field_0_0'), demonstrating that BoundWidget must not re-call id_for_label on attrs['id'] but instead use attrs['id'] directly.

5) The literal fallback pattern in BoundWidget ('id_%s_%s') is only correct as a legacy fallback when no attrs['id'] exists: hardcoding this pattern in preference to widget-computed ids encodes an incorrect precedence. The correct relationship is to prefer self.data['attrs']['id'] (widget-produced per-option id) and only fall back to 'id_%s_%s' % (self.data['name'], self.data['index']) when attrs['id'] is absent.
StepsToReproduce: Create a form field that uses ChoiceWidget subclass CheckboxSelectMultiple (e.g. a MultipleChoiceField with widget=CheckboxSelectMultiple).
Provide a non-default base id via widget.attrs['id'] or by passing a custom auto_id when constructing the form.
Render the form (or the field) so the subwidgets (checkbox inputs and their labels) are output.
Inspect a rendered option: compare the input element's id attribute (from the element's attrs) with the corresponding label's for attribute.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Each option's label 'for' attribute exactly matches the id attribute on the corresponding input element (i.e. the per-option id produced by the widget layer, respecting the provided widget.attrs['id'] or form.auto_id).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Label 'for' attributes are generated from the synthesized pattern 'id_<name>_<index>' (e.g. 'id_field_0') instead of using the widget-computed per-option id stored in data['attrs']['id'], causing labels to reference a different id than the input elements for CheckboxSelectMultiple and similar widgets.

problem_statement.Title: makemigrations generates migration that references models.Model but omits 'from django.db import models' causing NameError
problem_statement.Description: makemigrations can produce a migration file whose bases tuple contains models.Model (e.g. bases=(app.models.MyMixin, models.Model)) but the file does not include the import 'from django.db import models', causing a NameError when the migration module is imported.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption in django/db/migrations/serializer.py::TypeSerializer.serialize (special_cases entry): the serializer encodes the special-case tuple (models.Model, "models.Model", []) which is converted to ("models.Model", set()) and therefore emits the string "models.Model" without reporting the required import. The wrong assumption is that emitting the textual name "models.Model" does not require adding "from django.db import models" to the serializer's imports set.  

2) Dependency on serializer-provided imports in django/db/migrations/writer.py::MigrationWriter.as_string: the writer contains import-merge logic that only emits a combined import when the serializers reported "from django.db import models". Concretely: if "from django.db import models" in imports then writer replaces it with "from django.db import migrations, models"; otherwise it emits only "from django.db import migrations". The writer assumes serializers will supply the models import and does not synthesize it itself.  

3) Inconsistent serialization patterns within serializer.py: django/db/migrations/serializer.py::DeconstructableSerializer._serialize_path correctly returns name "models.<ClassName>" together with imports {"from django.db import models"}. TypeSerializer should follow the same pattern for the specific type object django.db.models.Model but currently does not.  

4) Trigger point in django/db/migrations/operations/models.py::CreateModel.__init__ (bases handling): CreateModel sets self.bases = bases or (models.Model,), and when the bases differ from the default (for example a mixin plus models.Model) the bases are serialized and TypeSerializer is invoked for models.Model. This is not a faulty assumption by itself but explains why the missing import surfaces for mixin+Model bases.  

5) Hardcoded imports list in the special-case is the immediate fault to change: the tuple (models.Model, "models.Model", []) encodes an empty imports list; the correct relationship is that the serializer must include the import, i.e. (models.Model, "models.Model", ["from django.db import models"]) so that MigrationWriter.as_string can produce the merged import line.
StepsToReproduce: Create a Django app with models.py containing: from django.db import models; class MyField(models.TextField): pass; class MyBaseModel(models.Model): class Meta: abstract = True; class MyMixin: pass; class MyModel(MyMixin, MyBaseModel): name = MyField(primary_key=True)
Run python manage.py makemigrations for that app
Open the generated migration (e.g. app/migrations/0001_initial.py) and observe bases contains (app.models.MyMixin, models.Model) but file imports lack 'from django.db import models'
Import the generated migration module or run the Django project; observe NameError: name 'models' is not defined at import time
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: makemigrations generates a valid Python migration file that includes the necessary import for django.db.models when the generated code references models.Model (for example by including 'from django.db import models' or the merged 'from django.db import migrations, models'), so importing the migration does not raise a NameError.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The generated migration file references models.Model in its bases tuple but does not include 'from django.db import models' (only 'from django.db import migrations' and 'import app.models'), causing a NameError: name 'models' is not defined when the migration module is imported.

problem_statement.Title: FormSet non-form errors are not tagged with 'nonform' CSS class
problem_statement.Description: FormSet-level (non-form) errors are rendered without a distinguishing CSS class. Forms add 'nonfield' to non-field ErrorList instances, but FormSets construct their non-form ErrorList without the equivalent 'nonform' marker, preventing renderers from distinguishing form errors, form non-field errors, and formset non-form errors.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) django/forms/formsets.py:BaseFormSet.full_clean encodes the assumption that formset non-form errors do not need a distinguishing CSS marker — it constructs self._non_form_errors using self.error_class() and self.error_class(e.error_list) with no error_class argument. 2) django/forms/utils.py:ErrorList.__init__/as_ul defines how CSS is composed: if error_class is supplied, the class string becomes 'errorlist <error_class>', otherwise 'errorlist' (relationship: passing error_class='nonform' yields '<ul class="errorlist nonform">'). 3) django/forms/forms.py:BaseForm.non_field_errors and BaseForm.add_error demonstrate the intended pattern — forms pass error_class='nonfield' when constructing non-field ErrorList instances; FormSets do not mirror this. 4) django/contrib/admin/helpers.py:AdminErrorList merges error lists; in certain admin rendering contexts an outer ErrorList may mask nested item classes, so even if a nested 'nonform' existed it might not be visible in merged output. 5) Compatibility assumption/gap: changing callers to pass error_class='nonform' assumes all custom ErrorList subclasses accept an error_class keyword in __init__; this was not verified and could cause runtime TypeError in third-party/custom implementations.
StepsToReproduce: Create a FormSet instance that produces a formset-level (non-form) error (for example, raise ValidationError in BaseFormSet.clean() or otherwise cause non_form_errors to be populated).
Render the formset non-form errors (e.g., call formset.non_form_errors().as_ul() or include {{ formset.non_form_errors }} in a template).
Inspect the resulting HTML for the <ul> element that contains the non-form errors.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: FormSet-level non-form errors are rendered with a distinguishing CSS class in addition to 'errorlist' (user-visible example: <ul class="errorlist nonform">...), so renderers can differentiate form field errors, form non-field errors, and formset non-form errors.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: FormSet non-form errors render as <ul class="errorlist"> (no 'nonform' class). Forms already render non-field errors as <ul class="errorlist nonfield">, so FormSets lack the symmetric 'nonform' marker.

problem_statement.Title: QuerySet.defer() doesn't clear deferred field when chaining with only()
problem_statement.Description: Chaining QuerySet.only(...).defer(...) can produce SQL that selects all model columns instead of only the expected minimal columns (e.g. only the primary key). Example: Company.objects.only("name").defer("name") yields SELECT id, name, trade_number, country instead of SELECT id.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect encoding of deferred-loading state in django/db/models/sql/query.py: Query.add_deferred_loading — the code represents the immediate (only) set and defer-flag as self.deferred_loading = (existing_set, defer_bool) and allows a transition to (frozenset(), False). That representation is used elsewhere as evidence of "no restrictions", but here it results from removing the last name from an immediate-only set, which is a semantic mismatch.  

2) Misinterpretation of empty immediate set in django/db/models/sql/query.py: Query.get_loaded_field_names and deferred_to_data — these functions treat an empty field set (frozenset()) with defer==False as "no restriction" (load all columns). Concretely: deferred_loading == (frozenset(), False) -> deferred_to_data/get_loaded_field_names => no columns suppressed -> SQL selects all fields.  

3) Missing cache invalidation in django/db/models/sql/query.py: Query.add_deferred_loading, Query.add_immediate_loading, Query.clear_deferred_loading — these methods mutate self.deferred_loading but do not always delete self._loaded_field_names_cache, allowing stale loaded-field mappings to be reused by later compilation steps.  

4) Representation/relationship that must hold but currently does not: when the immediate-only set becomes empty as a result of removing names, the effective meaning should be explicit deferred-mode with a concrete deferred set corresponding to "all non-primary-key concrete fields (plus any related-field prefixed concrete non-pk fields)" rather than (frozenset(), False). The investigator recorded the concrete construction used by the analysis: deferred_fields = { f.name for f in opts.concrete_fields if not f.primary_key } and, for prefixed related lookups, prefixed entries like 'rel__<concrete_non_pk_field>'. The current code fails to convert the empty immediate set into that concrete deferred set.  

5) Compiler reliance: django/db/models/sql/compiler.py: SQLCompiler.deferred_to_columns assumes the mapping returned by Query.deferred_to_data/get_loaded_field_names is authoritative; therefore any incorrect encoding or stale cache from the query layer propagates directly to selected SQL columns.
StepsToReproduce: Define a Company model with fields: id (primary key), name, trade_number, country.
Run Company.objects.only("name").defer("name") and inspect the generated SQL (e.g., print(query) or str(query.query)).
Observe the SQL: it shows SELECT "company"."id", "company"."name", "company"."trade_number", "company"."country" instead of SELECT "company"."id".
Run Company.objects.only("name").defer("name").defer("country") and inspect the SQL; it also selects all fields.
Run Company.objects.only("name", "country").defer("name") and inspect the SQL; this produces the expected SELECT "company"."id", "company"."country" (contrasting behavior).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When fields are first restricted by only(...) and then the same fields are deferred via defer(...), the resulting query should load only the remaining explicitly permitted fields (for the base example, only the primary key) — e.g. Company.objects.only("name").defer("name") should produce SQL selecting only the model primary key: SELECT "company"."id".
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: In the failing case, chaining only(...).defer(...) produces SQL that selects all model columns (SELECT id, name, trade_number, country) because an internal deferred-loading representation becomes (frozenset(), False) and is interpreted as "no restriction"; cached loaded-field mappings may also be stale and contribute to the incorrect column selection.

hints_text:
Replying to Manuel Baclet: Considering a simple Company model with four fields: id, name, trade_number and country. If we evaluate a queryset containing a .defer() following a .only(), the generated sql query selects unexpected fields. For example: Company.objects.only("name").defer("name") loads all the fields with the following query: SELECT "company"."id", "company"."name", "company"."trade_number", "company"."country" FROM "company" This is an expected behavior, defer() removes fields from the list of fields specified by the only() method (i.e. list of fields that should not be deferred). In this example only() adds name to the list, defer() removes name from the list, so you have empty lists and all fields will be loaded. It is also ​documented: # Final result is that everything except "headline" is deferred. Entry.objects.only("headline", "body").defer("body") Company.objects.only("name").defer("name").defer("country") also loads all the fields with the same query: SELECT "company"."id", "company"."name", "company"."trade_number", "company"."country" FROM "company" I agree you shouldn't get all field, but only pk, name, and trade_number: SELECT "ticket_32704_company"."id", "ticket_32704_company"."name", "ticket_32704_company"."trade_number" FROM "ticket_32704_company" this is due to the fact that defer() doesn't clear the list of deferred field when chaining with only(). I attached a proposed patch.
Draft.
After reading the documentation carefully, i cannot say that it is clearly stated that deferring all the fields used in a previous .only() call performs a reset of the deferred set. Moreover, in the .defer() ​section, we have: You can make multiple calls to defer(). Each call adds new fields to the deferred set: and this seems to suggest that: calls to .defer() cannot remove items from the deferred set and evaluating qs.defer("some_field") should never fetch the column "some_field" (since this should add "some_field" to the deferred set) the querysets qs.defer("field1").defer("field2") and qs.defer("field1", "field2") should be equivalent IMHO, there is a mismatch between the doc and the actual implementation.
calls to .defer() cannot remove items from the deferred set and evaluating qs.defer("some_field") should never fetch the column "some_field" (since this should add "some_field" to the deferred set) Feel-free to propose a docs clarification (see also #24048). the querysets qs.defer("field1").defer("field2") and qs.defer("field1", "field2") should be equivalent That's why I accepted Company.objects.only("name").defer("name").defer("country") as a bug.
I think that what is described in the documentation is what users are expected and it is the implementation that should be fixed! With your patch proposal, i do not think that: Company.objects.only("name").defer("name").defer("country") is equivalent to Company.objects.only("name").defer("name", "country")
Replying to Manuel Baclet: I think that what is described in the documentation is what users are expected and it is the implementation that should be fixed! I don't agree, and there is no need to shout. As documented: "The only() method is more or less the opposite of defer(). You call it with the fields that should not be deferred ...", so .only('name').defer('name') should return all fields. You can start a discussion on DevelopersMailingList if you don't agree. With your patch proposal, i do not think that: Company.objects.only("name").defer("name").defer("country") is equivalent to Company.objects.only("name").defer("name", "country") Did you check this? with proposed patch country is the only deferred fields in both cases. As far as I'm aware that's an intended behavior.
With the proposed patch, I think that: Company.objects.only("name").defer("name", "country") loads all fields whereas: Company.objects.only("name").defer("name").defer("country") loads all fields except "country". Why is that? In the first case: existing.difference(field_names) == {"name"}.difference(["name", "country"]) == empty_set and we go into the if branch clearing all the deferred fields and we are done. In the second case: existing.difference(field_names) == {"name"}.difference(["name"]) == empty_set and we go into the if branch clearing all the deferred fields. Then we add "country" to the set of deferred fields.
Hey all, Replying to Mariusz Felisiak: Replying to Manuel Baclet: With your patch proposal, i do not think that: Company.objects.only("name").defer("name").defer("country") is equivalent to Company.objects.only("name").defer("name", "country") Did you check this? with proposed patch country is the only deferred fields in both cases. As far as I'm aware that's an intended behavior. I believe Manuel is right. This happens because the set difference in one direction gives you the empty set that will clear out the deferred fields - but it is missing the fact that we might also be adding more defer fields than we had only fields in the first place, so that we actually switch from an .only() to a .defer() mode. See the corresponding PR that should fix this behaviour ​https://github.com/django/django/pull/14667

problem_statement.Title: ManyToManyRel.identity inserts raw through_fields (list) into identity causing TypeError when hashed
problem_statement.Description: ManyToManyRel.identity places self.through_fields directly into the relation identity tuple. When through_fields is a list, the identity tuple contains an unhashable element, causing a TypeError during operations that hash relations (for example, model checks).
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption encoded in django/db/models/fields/reverse_related.py::ManyToManyRel.identity: the code appends self.through_fields directly to the identity tuple (identity = super().identity + (self.through, self.through_fields, self.db_constraint)). This assumes through_fields is already hashable; that assumption is false when through_fields is a list.  

2) Requirement exposed by parent class encoded in django/db/models/fields/reverse_related.py::ForeignObjectRel.__hash__ and identity: __hash__ returns hash(self.identity), therefore every element of identity must be hashable. ForeignObjectRel.identity already canonicalizes limit_choices_to with make_hashable(limit_choices_to), demonstrating the intended pattern for identity elements.  

3) Canonicalization helper and relationship that must hold (location: django/utils/hashable.py::make_hashable): elements placed into identity must be made hashable (e.g. make_hashable(self.through_fields)) because hash(self.identity) is called by ForeignObjectRel.__hash__. The current ManyToManyRel.identity violates this relationship by not applying make_hashable to through_fields.  

4) Implicit equality/signature assumption encoded in django/db/models/base.py::_check_m2m_through_same_relationship: signatures are built using f.remote_field.through_fields directly (signature = (f.remote_field.model, cls, f.remote_field.through, f.remote_field.through_fields)). This assumes through_fields will compare/evaluate consistently (tuple vs list); using raw through_fields can produce inconsistent equality comparisons between logically identical relations when one uses a list and another a tuple. The logical relationship is that signature elements that participate in hashing/equality comparisons must be canonicalized (e.g. make_hashable(f.remote_field.through_fields)).
StepsToReproduce: Create models exactly as in the repro: define Parent (with a CharField), ProxyParent (Meta: proxy = True) inheriting Parent, Child with a ForeignKey to Parent and a ManyToManyField to Parent using through='ManyToManyModel' and through_fields=['child', 'parent'], and define ManyToManyModel with parent = ForeignKey(Parent, related_name='+'), child = ForeignKey(Child, related_name='+'), plus an extra FK field.
Run Django management command that triggers system checks (for example: python manage.py check or any manage.py command that runs app checks).
Observe the process raising TypeError: unhashable type: 'list' from django/db/models/fields/reverse_related.py when hashing a relation identity.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Model checks and other operations that compare or hash relation objects complete without error. Many-to-many relationship objects are comparable and hashable regardless of whether through_fields was supplied as a list or tuple; logically equivalent through_fields representations do not cause TypeError or inconsistent equality results.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Django raises TypeError: unhashable type: 'list' during model checks (stack trace points to django/db/models/fields/reverse_related.py.__hash__). The error occurs because ManyToManyRel.identity contains a list in its identity tuple (self.through_fields), which cannot be hashed when hash(self.identity) is called.

     def _check_relationship_model(self, from_model=None, **kwargs):

problem_statement.Title: Developer-supplied related_name is silently ignored for symmetrical self ManyToManyField (no system check)
problem_statement.Description: When a ManyToManyField is symmetrical and points to 'self' (self-referential), Django does not create a reverse accessor for the relation, but user-supplied related_name values are accepted and silently ignored/overwritten. Developers can therefore provide related_name and be misled because no error or warning is raised.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) ManyToManyRel.get_accessor_name (django/db/models/fields/reverse_related.py) intentionally suppresses reverse accessors for symmetrical self relations: if self.symmetrical and model == self.model: return None. This means no reverse accessor will exist for that relation.

2) ManyToManyField.contribute_to_class (django/db/models/fields/related.py) unconditionally overwrites or generates an internal related_name for symmetrical/self relations, e.g. when remote_field.symmetrical and (remote_field.model == RECURSIVE_RELATIONSHIP_CONSTANT or remote_field.model == cls._meta.object_name): self.remote_field.related_name = "%s_rel_+" % name. This assignment hides or discards any developer-supplied related_name.

3) ManyToManyField.check (django/db/models/fields/related.py) contains no validation that rejects an explicit related_name for a symmetrical self relation. Its returned checks list currently lacks a check of the form that would detect "related_name set while remote_field.symmetrical and relation resolves to the same model".

4) The codebase relies on RECURSIVE_RELATIONSHIP_CONSTANT == 'self' and the '+' sentinel in related_name strings to denote hidden/internal relations. These constants encode the semantics used by contribute_to_class and get_accessor_name and are part of why the developer-supplied related_name is ignored.

5) The explicit condition and relationship that must be validated (as identified by the investigation) is: to_model = resolve_relation(from_model, self.remote_field.model); if to_model == from_model and self._related_name is not None then this situation should be treated as an error (proposed error id 'fields.E341'). This precise formula is required to avoid false positives for abstract/unresolved models (skip when from_model is None).

6) The combined effect of (1) and (2) plus the missing check (3) encodes the incorrect assumption "accepting a related_name here is harmless". In reality the related_name will not produce a reverse accessor and is therefore misleading to developers.
StepsToReproduce: Run the project checks: python manage.py check — observe no errors or warnings about the supplied related_name.

Start a Django shell (python manage.py shell), import the model and inspect the target model for the reverse accessor (e.g. hasattr(Person, 'buddies') or dir(Person)) — observe that the 'buddies' reverse accessor is not present.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The framework must not silently ignore a developer-supplied related_name for a symmetrical self ManyToManyField. In user-visible terms either (a) the provided related_name should produce the corresponding reverse accessor on the model named by the developer, or (b) the framework should raise a clear, early validation error (e.g. during manage.py check or Model.check) informing the developer that related_name is not applicable for symmetrical self relations.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Developers can pass related_name to a symmetrical self ManyToManyField and no error or warning is raised; internally contribute_to_class generates/overwrites the related_name (using formats like "%s_rel_+" or names ending with '+') and ManyToManyRel.get_accessor_name returns None for symmetrical self relations, so no reverse accessor is created and the developer-supplied related_name is ignored.

hints_text:
I have a PR that implements this incoming.
​https://github.com/django/django/pull/14730
OK, I guess we can do something here — it probably is a source of confusion. The same issue was raised in #18021 (but as an invalid bug report, rather than suggesting improving the messaging). Looking at the PR — I'm sceptical about just raising an error — this will likely break code in the wild. Can we investigate adding a system check here instead? There are several similar checks for related fields already: ​https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/3.2/ref/checks/#related-fields
Same issue also came up in #12641
Absolutely. A system check is a much better approach than my initial idea of the error. I have changed the patch to use a system check.
Unchecking patch needs improvement as instructed on the page, (pending reviewer acceptance of course).

--- a/tests/admin_views/test_autocomplete_view.py
+++ b/tests/admin_views/test_autocomplete_view.py
@@ -1,3 +1,4 @@
+import datetime
 import json
 from contextlib import contextmanager
 
@@ -293,6 +294,29 @@ class PKOrderingQuestionAdmin(QuestionAdmin):
             'pagination': {'more': False},
         })
 
+    def test_serialize_result(self):
+        class AutocompleteJsonSerializeResultView(AutocompleteJsonView):
+            def serialize_result(self, obj, to_field_name):
+                return {
+                    **super().serialize_result(obj, to_field_name),
+                    'posted': str(obj.posted),
+                }
+
+        Question.objects.create(question='Question 1', posted=datetime.date(2021, 8, 9))
+        Question.objects.create(question='Question 2', posted=datetime.date(2021, 8, 7))
+        request = self.factory.get(self.url, {'term': 'question', **self.opts})
+        request.user = self.superuser
+        response = AutocompleteJsonSerializeResultView.as_view(**self.as_view_args)(request)
+        self.assertEqual(response.status_code, 200)
+        data = json.loads(response.content.decode('utf-8'))
+        self.assertEqual(data, {
+            'results': [
+                {'id': str(q.pk), 'text': q.question, 'posted': str(q.posted)}
+                for q in Question.objects.order_by('-posted')
+            ],
+            'pagination': {'more': False},
+        })
+
 
 @override_settings(ROOT_URLCONF='admin_views.urls')
 class SeleniumTests(AdminSeleniumTestCase):

problem_statement.Title: AutocompleteJsonView requires full get() override to add extra per-item fields
problem_statement.Description: Developers cannot add extra fields to each item in the admin autocomplete JSON response without overriding AutocompleteJsonView.get(), forcing copy/paste of the entire get() implementation and creating maintenance overhead as get() evolves.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Inline per-item serialization is hardcoded in AutocompleteJsonView.get(): the code builds the response as
'results': [
    {'id': str(getattr(obj, to_field_name)), 'text': str(obj)}
    for obj in context['object_list']
],
'pagination': {'more': context['page_obj'].has_next()},
Location: django/contrib/admin/views/autocomplete.py :: AutocompleteJsonView.get().
This encodes the incorrect assumption that per-item JSON cannot be customized without overriding the whole get() method.

2) The intended per-item representation (the exact relationship to preserve) is:
serialize_result(self, obj, to_field_name) -> {'id': str(getattr(obj, to_field_name)), 'text': str(obj)}
Location (proposed/required): django/contrib/admin/views/autocomplete.py :: AutocompleteJsonView.serialize_result (new additive hook). The absence of this hook is a root cause of the maintenance problem.

3) Server-side pagination size is a hardcoded class attribute: AutocompleteJsonView.paginate_by = 20. Location: django/contrib/admin/views/autocomplete.py :: AutocompleteJsonView.paginate_by. Tests and consumers reference this value (tests/admin_views/test_autocomplete_view.py) so any change to response shape must preserve pagination behavior.

4) AdminSite constructs the view via AutocompleteJsonView.as_view(admin_site=self), so changes to AutocompleteJsonView must remain backward-compatible with how AdminSite registers the autocomplete endpoint. Location: django/contrib/admin/sites.py :: AdminSite.autocomplete_view.

5) Client-side (select2 / autocomplete widget) expects each result to contain 'id' and 'text' (and will typically ignore extra keys). Location: django/contrib/admin/widgets.py :: Autocomplete widget data attributes / client expectation. This assumption explains why adding extra keys is safe for clients but also clarifies that 'id'/'text' must remain present.

6) Test coverage gap: there is no existing unit test demonstrating a subclass overriding a (proposed) serialize_result hook to add extra fields while preserving default behavior. Location: tests/admin_views/test_autocomplete_view.py. The lack of such a test is a gap in verification rather than the primary bug, but it contributes to the maintenance risk if the API is modified without a test.
StepsToReproduce: Configure a Django admin site with a model that uses an autocomplete foreign-key field.

Create a CustomAutocompleteJsonView subclass of AutocompleteJsonView and override get() to return JsonResponse with additional per-item keys (for example, copying the implementation and changing the list comprehension to {'id': str(getattr(obj, to_field_name)), 'text': str(obj), 'notes': obj.notes}).
Register that view (or replace the autocomplete URL) and trigger the admin autocomplete endpoint in a browser or HTTP client.
Inspect the JSON response; observe that adding extra fields required copying the entire get() implementation.
Confirm that the overridden get() duplicates permission checks, pagination, and other logic from the upstream AutocompleteJsonView (demonstrating maintenance burden when upstream get() changes).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: It should be possible to add custom fields to each item in the admin autocomplete JSON response without overriding the entire AutocompleteJsonView.get(). The default response must continue to include at least 'id' and 'text' and preserve pagination behavior (server-side page size and pagination.more flag).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: AutocompleteJsonView.get() constructs per-item dictionaries inline with only 'id' and 'text' (using {'id': str(getattr(obj, to_field_name)), 'text': str(obj)} in a list comprehension) so developers who need extra per-item fields must override the entire get() method and duplicate its logic, causing maintenance overhead as upstream get() changes.

hints_text: Makes sense to me.

diff --git a/django/utils/decorators.py b/django/utils/decorators.py
--- a/django/utils/decorators.py
+++ b/django/utils/decorators.py
@@ -37,7 +37,7 @@ def _wrapper(self, *args, **kwargs):
         # 'self' argument, but it's a closure over self so it can call
         # 'func'. Also, wrap method.__get__() in a function because new
         # attributes can't be set on bound method objects, only on functions.
-        bound_method = partial(method.__get__(self, type(self)))
+        bound_method = wraps(method)(partial(method.__get__(self, type(self))))
         for dec in decorators:
             bound_method = dec(bound_method)
         return bound_method(*args, **kwargs)
test_patch:
diff --git a/tests/decorators/tests.py b/tests/decorators/tests.py
--- a/tests/decorators/tests.py
+++ b/tests/decorators/tests.py
@@ -425,6 +425,29 @@ class Test:
                 def __module__(cls):
                     return "tests"
 
+    def test_wrapper_assignments(self):
+        """@method_decorator preserves wrapper assignments."""
+        func_name = None
+        func_module = None
+
+        def decorator(func):
+            @wraps(func)
+            def inner(*args, **kwargs):
+                nonlocal func_name, func_module
+                func_name = getattr(func, '__name__', None)
+                func_module = getattr(func, '__module__', None)
+                return func(*args, **kwargs)
+            return inner
+
+        class Test:
+            @method_decorator(decorator)
+            def method(self):
+                return 'tests'
+
+        Test().method()
+        self.assertEqual(func_name, 'method')
+        self.assertIsNotNone(func_module)
+
 
 class XFrameOptionsDecoratorsTests(TestCase):
     """

problem_statement.Title: method_decorator() passes functools.partial to decorators, causing AttributeError when decorators expect function metadata
problem_statement.Description: Applying a function decorator to an instance method via method_decorator causes an AttributeError because _multi_decorate constructs and passes a functools.partial (bound_method) into the decorator. functools.partial objects do not expose function metadata (e.g. __name__, __module__), so decorators that inspect the callable (for example decorators using functools.wraps) fail.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Incorrect assumption in django/utils/decorators.py::_multi_decorate: a functools.partial can stand in for a bound function for the purposes of decorator introspection. This is encoded by the exact code: bound_method = partial(method.__get__(self, type(self))). Partial objects lack attributes like __name__ and __module__ and therefore break decorators that read those attributes.

2. Incorrect assumption in django/utils/decorators.py::_update_method_wrapper: copying decorator-added attributes onto the class-level wrapper after applying decorators is sufficient to satisfy decorator code that inspects the callable it receives. In reality such copying happens too late to prevent decorators (or functools.wraps inside them) from failing at decoration-time.

3. Incorrect assumption in django/utils/decorators.py::method_decorator: converting function decorators into method decorators by leaving the bound callable as-is (a partial) is sufficient. method_decorator/_multi_decorate currently passes the partial to dec(...), which lets decorators operate on an object that is not a real function and lacks function metadata.

4. Test coverage gap in tests/decorators/tests.py: existing tests check that class-level wrapper preserves attributes, but do not cover decorators that inspect the callable passed into them (e.g. decorators that call functools.wraps on the supplied func). The reported failure demonstrates an uncovered case where decorator internals require the bound callable to expose function metadata.
StepsToReproduce: Define a decorator that uses functools.wraps on its input and references func.__name__ (example 'logger' from the original report).
Create a class with an instance method decorated with @method_decorator(logger).
Instantiate the class and call the decorated method.
Observe the exception raised during the call.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Decorating an instance method via method_decorator should not raise exceptions when the supplied decorator inspects the wrapped callable. Decorators that use functools.wraps or otherwise access function metadata (e.g. __name__, __module__, __doc__) should see those attributes and the method call should succeed.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling the decorated instance method raises AttributeError: 'functools.partial' object has no attribute '__name__' because the decorator received a functools.partial rather than an object exposing function metadata.

problem_statement.Title: Wrong admin URL namespace for readonly related-object links and Recent actions in custom AdminSite
problem_statement.Description: When using a custom AdminSite, readonly related-object links rendered in the admin (and 'Recent actions' links) are generated using the default 'admin' namespace instead of the custom AdminSite namespace, so links point to /admin/... rather than the custom site's URL prefix (e.g. /custom-admin/...).
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/contrib/admin/helpers.py :: AdminReadonlyField.get_admin_url — Incorrect assumption: calling reverse(url_name, args=[quote(remote_obj.pk)]) without supplying current_app will resolve to the active AdminSite namespace. The code constructs url_name as: "admin:%s_%s_change" % (remote_field.model._meta.app_label, remote_field.model._meta.model_name) but omits the AdminSite namespace. Because AdminReadonlyField has access to self.model_admin (which provides admin_site.name), the correct relationship is to reverse the named URL using the AdminSite namespace: reverse(url_name, args=[quote(remote_obj.pk)], current_app=self.model_admin.admin_site.name).  

2) django/contrib/admin/models.py :: LogEntry.get_admin_url — Incorrect assumption: calling reverse(url_name, args=(quote(self.object_id),)) without current_app will produce a link appropriate for the AdminSite being rendered. The method builds url_name as: "admin:%s_%s_change" % (self.content_type.app_label, self.content_type.model) but has no request/context; therefore it must resolve using the AdminSite namespace(s) (for example, by attempting reverse(..., current_app=admin_site.name) for registered admin sites) rather than relying on the default 'admin' namespace.  

3) Inconsistency evidence: other admin code (e.g. django/contrib/admin/options.py ModelAdmin.response_add, django/contrib/admin/widgets.py RelatedFieldWidgetWrapper, and AdminSite.index) already set request.current_app or pass current_app=self.admin_site.name when reversing admin URLs; the two locations above deviate from that established pattern.
StepsToReproduce: Create and register a custom AdminSite with a non-default name (for example, name='custom-admin') and mount it at a custom URL prefix (e.g. /custom-admin/).
Register a model that has a ForeignKey to another model; include that ForeignKey field in readonly_fields of the model's ModelAdmin.
Visit the change (or view) page for an instance of the model in the custom AdminSite and inspect the rendered readonly related-object link.
Also open the AdminSite index page and inspect links in the "Recent actions" section.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Readonly related-object links and "Recent actions" links generated by the admin should point to the same AdminSite namespace currently being used (the custom AdminSite), producing URLs under the custom admin prefix (e.g. /custom-admin/<app>/<model>/<pk>/change/).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Readonly related-object links and "Recent actions" links are generated under the default admin namespace (URLs beginning with /admin/...) even when viewing pages served by a custom AdminSite (e.g. links point to /admin/<app>/<model>/<pk>/change/ instead of /custom-admin/<app>/<model>/<pk>/change/).

test_patch: diff --git a/tests/model_forms/test_modelchoicefield.py b/tests/model_forms/test_modelchoicefield.py --- a/tests/model_forms/test_modelchoicefield.py +++ b/tests/model_forms/test_modelchoicefield.py @@ -2,7 +2,7 @@ from django import forms from django.core.exceptions import ValidationError -from django.forms.models import ModelChoiceIterator +from django.forms.models import ModelChoiceIterator, ModelChoiceIteratorValue from django.forms.widgets import CheckboxSelectMultiple from django.template import Context, Template from django.test import TestCase @@ -341,6 +341,12 @@ class CustomModelMultipleChoiceField(forms.ModelMultipleChoiceField): """ % (self.c1.pk, self.c2.pk, self.c3.pk), ) + def test_choice_value_hash(self): + value_1 = ModelChoiceIteratorValue(self.c1.pk, self.c1) + value_2 = ModelChoiceIteratorValue(self.c2.pk, self.c2) + self.assertEqual(hash(value_1), hash(ModelChoiceIteratorValue(self.c1.pk, None))) + self.assertNotEqual(hash(value_1), hash(value_2)) + def test_choices_not_fetched_when_not_rendering(self): with self.assertNumQueries(1): field = forms.ModelChoiceField(Category.objects.order_by('-name'))

problem_statement.Title: ModelChoiceIteratorValue is unhashable causing TypeError in widget create_option / dict membership after Django 3.1
problem_statement.Description: After upgrading to Django 3.1, ModelChoiceField option values are ModelChoiceIteratorValue instances that are unhashable; code that performs membership tests or uses option values as dict/set keys (for example, in widget create_option overrides) raises TypeError: "unhashable type: 'ModelChoiceIteratorValue'".
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) django/forms/models.py:ModelChoiceIteratorValue — The class defines __eq__ (equality compares to the wrapped .value) but does not define __hash__. Because __eq__ is present and __hash__ is absent, Python marks instances unhashable, causing TypeError when code relies on hashing. 2) django/forms/models.py:ModelChoiceIterator.choice and django/forms/fields:ModelChoiceField.prepare_value — ModelChoiceIterator wraps the prepared value as ModelChoiceIteratorValue(self.field.prepare_value(obj), obj). Equality is implemented to compare against the wrapped .value, so hashing must be consistent with that equality (i.e., hash(instance) must equal hash(instance.value)). 3) django/forms/widgets.py:create_option and user create_option overrides — Consumers assume the received "value" is a hashable prepared primitive (e.g., int/str PK) and use it in membership tests or as dict/set keys (e.g., "if value in self.show_fields" where show_fields is a dict keyed by ints). That assumption is encoded in widget/user code and is violated when ModelChoiceIteratorValue is unhashable. 4) docs/releases/3.1.txt (informational) — The release introduced ModelChoiceIteratorValue with the implicit expectation of backward compatibility for code that treated option values as raw primitives; the implementation did not preserve the hashability semantic. 5) Edge-case assumption in ModelChoiceIteratorValue / ModelChoiceField.prepare_value — The implicit requirement that the wrapped prepared value be hashable is not guaranteed for all custom prepare_value implementations; if prepare_value returns an inherently unhashable object (e.g., list), then computing a hash based on the wrapped value would raise TypeError. The invariant that must hold is: objects considered equal to their wrapped .value must have consistent hash behavior (hash(instance) == hash(instance.value)).
StepsToReproduce: Use Django 3.1 (or a version where ModelChoiceIteratorValue is used for ModelChoiceField choices).
Create a ModelChoiceField on a form and attach the default select widget (or a custom widget).

Override the widget's create_option method and perform a membership check using the provided value against a dict keyed by primary-key ints, e.g.:
  def create_option(...):
    context = super().create_option(...)
    if not value:
      return context
    if value in self.show_fields:  # self.show_fields is a dict keyed by ints
      context['attrs']['data-fields'] = json.dumps(self.show_fields[value])
Render the form or otherwise invoke create_option for the ModelChoiceField choices. Observe the TypeError.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Option values delivered to widgets and user overrides behave like the prepared primitive values (hashable when the prepared value is hashable). Membership tests ("if value in dict"), use as dict keys, and inclusion in sets work without raising TypeError.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When rendering choices for a ModelChoiceField, the option "value" argument is a ModelChoiceIteratorValue instance that is unhashable; operations that require hashing (membership tests, dict/set insertion or lookup) raise TypeError: "unhashable type: 'ModelChoiceIteratorValue'".

hints_text:
Thanks for the ticket. Agreed, we could make ModelChoiceIteratorValue hashable by adding: def __hash__(self): return hash(self.value) For now you can use value.value as ​documented in the "Backwards incompatible changes in 3.1" section. Would you like to prepare a patch?
Replying to Mariusz Felisiak: Thanks for the ticket. Agreed, we could make ModelChoiceIteratorValue hashable by adding: def __hash__(self): return hash(self.value) For now you can use value.value as ​documented in the "Backwards incompatible changes in 3.1" section. Would you like to prepare a patch? Yes, sure.
Patch: ​https://github.com/django/django/pull/14915

diff --git a/django/db/backends/ddl_references.py b/django/db/backends/ddl_references.py
--- a/django/db/backends/ddl_references.py
+++ b/django/db/backends/ddl_references.py
@@ -212,11 +212,7 @@ def __init__(self, table, expressions, compiler, quote_value):
     def rename_table_references(self, old_table, new_table):
         if self.table != old_table:
             return
-        expressions = deepcopy(self.expressions)
-        self.columns = []
-        for col in self.compiler.query._gen_cols([expressions]):
-            col.alias = new_table
-        self.expressions = expressions
+        self.expressions = self.expressions.relabeled_clone({old_table: new_table})
         super().rename_table_references(old_table, new_table)
 
     def rename_column_references(self, table, old_column, new_column):
test_patch:
diff --git a/tests/backends/test_ddl_references.py b/tests/backends/test_ddl_references.py
--- a/tests/backends/test_ddl_references.py
+++ b/tests/backends/test_ddl_references.py
@@ -5,6 +5,7 @@
 from django.db.models import ExpressionList, F
 from django.db.models.functions import Upper
 from django.db.models.indexes import IndexExpression
+from django.db.models.sql import Query
 from django.test import SimpleTestCase, TransactionTestCase
 
 from .models import Person
@@ -229,6 +230,27 @@ def test_rename_table_references(self):
             str(self.expressions),
         )
 
+    def test_rename_table_references_without_alias(self):
+        compiler = Query(Person, alias_cols=False).get_compiler(connection=connection)
+        table = Person._meta.db_table
+        expressions = Expressions(
+            table=table,
+            expressions=ExpressionList(
+                IndexExpression(Upper('last_name')),
+                IndexExpression(F('first_name')),
+            ).resolve_expression(compiler.query),
+            compiler=compiler,
+            quote_value=self.editor.quote_value,
+        )
+        expressions.rename_table_references(table, 'other')
+        self.assertIs(expressions.references_table(table), False)
+        self.assertIs(expressions.references_table('other'), True)
+        expected_str = '(UPPER(%s)), %s' % (
+            self.editor.quote_name('last_name'),
+            self.editor.quote_name('first_name'),
+        )
+        self.assertEqual(str(expressions), expected_str)
+
     def test_rename_column_references(self):
         table = Person._meta.db_table
         self.expressions.rename_column_references(table, 'first_name', 'other')

problem_statement.Title: Remaking table with UniqueConstraint crashes on SQLite due to table-qualified column in deferred index expression
problem_statement.Description: When applying a migration that remodels a table which has a UniqueConstraint, Django's deferred CREATE UNIQUE INDEX statement is generated with table-qualified column names (using a "." operator). SQLite prohibits the "." operator in index expressions, causing the migration to fail during the table-remake flow.
StepsToReproduce: Create a Django model with two fields and a UniqueConstraint on both, e.g. Tag(name=SlugField, value=CharField) and Meta.constraints = [UniqueConstraint('name', 'value', name='unique_name_value')].
Create an initial migration that creates the model and adds the UniqueConstraint (value max_length=200 in initial migration).
Create a second migration that alters the field (e.g. change value max_length from 200 to 150).
Using the sqlite backend, run: python manage.py migrate
Observe the migration fails during the table-remake step for the alter_field migration.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The migration completes successfully: the field alteration is applied, the table is remade if necessary, and the unique index/constraint is created without causing an SQLite OperationalError.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: During migrate, the alter_field migration fails with sqlite OperationalError: "the '.' operator prohibited in index expressions" when executing deferred CREATE UNIQUE INDEX SQL. The failure occurs in the SQLite table-remake flow after rename_table_references has produced qualified column names in the index expression.

Thanks for the report! Looks like we don't check if an alias is set on the Col before we update it to new_table in Expressions.rename_table_references when running _remake_table.

problem_statement.Title: RenameModel with db_table should be a noop
problem_statement.Description: A RenameModel migration that only renames the model class but the model Meta.db_table is explicitly set (so the effective DB table name does not change) should not perform any database schema changes. Currently RenameModel triggers schema edits: on Postgres it drops and recreates foreign key constraints; on SQLite it recreates the table.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/db/migrations/operations/models.py::RenameModel.database_forwards — encodes the assumption that it is safe to run schema updates for a model rename unconditionally. The implementation unconditionally computes new_model and old_model and then calls schema_editor.alter_db_table(...) and proceeds to alter related fields (schema_editor.alter_field) and M2M names even when the effective DB table name is identical.  

2) django/db/backends/base/schema.py::BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor.alter_db_table — this method already treats identical table names as a no-op (it returns early if old_db_table == new_db_table or, on case-insensitive backends, old_db_table.lower() == new_db_table.lower()). Relying on this guard is insufficient because RenameModel continues to call related-field alterations after alter_db_table returns.  

3) django/db/backends/base/schema.py::BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor._field_should_be_altered and alter_field — these methods determine whether to alter a field by comparing old_field.deconstruct() to new_field.deconstruct(); some attribute differences (field type, primary_key, unique, to_field targets, etc.) cause alter_field to drop/recreate foreign-key constraints. When RenameModel invokes alter_field for related objects (even if table names are identical), differences between Field instances from different state registries can trigger destructive schema changes.  

4) django/db/migrations/operations/models.py::AlterModelTable — this operation is the explicit path for changing db_table and demonstrates the intended behavior for when the effective table name actually changes; RenameModel should avoid performing DB work when db_table is unchanged to avoid conflict/duplication.  

5) Exact equality check and semantics that must be used when deciding noop: the decision must be based on the effective table names computed from state: old_db_table = old_model._meta.db_table and new_db_table = new_model._meta.db_table, and use the same case-insensitivity rule as alter_db_table: treat as identical when old_db_table == new_db_table or (schema_editor.connection.features.ignores_table_name_case and old_db_table.lower() == new_db_table.lower()). This relationship/formula was identified in the investigation and must be preserved when determining a noop.  

6) Investigation gaps (locations to validate before finalizing a patch): the Postgres backend schema editor implementation (e.g. django/db/backends/postgresql/schema.py) was not inspected to confirm exact FK SQL behavior or backend-specific overrides; tests and migration autodetector behavior covering RenameModel with explicit db_table were not reviewed; and concrete tracing of alter_field with representative Field instances was not performed. These gaps may affect corner cases of the noop decision.
StepsToReproduce: Create a Django app with a model that sets Meta.db_table explicitly (e.g. class MyModel(Model): class Meta: db_table = 'custom_table').
Create a migration that renames the model class (RenameModel) but leaves Meta.db_table unchanged (old_name -> new_name).
Run the migration against a Postgres database and observe schema activity (or run against SQLite and observe schema activity).
Observe that on Postgres foreign key constraints are dropped and recreated; on SQLite the table is recreated.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Applying a RenameModel migration where the model's Meta.db_table is unchanged should be a no-op at the database level: no tables should be recreated or renamed, and no foreign-key constraints should be dropped or recreated. The migration should only update migration/state metadata.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: RenameModel triggers database schema changes despite db_table being unchanged: on Postgres foreign key constraints are dropped and recreated; on SQLite the table is recreated.
