problem_statement.Title: MultiWidget.id_for_label unconditionally appends '_0', forcing labels to target first subwidget
problem_statement.Description: When rendering a MultiWidget, the widget's id_for_label implementation unconditionally appends '_0' to the base id, causing the label's for attribute to point at the first subwidget (e.g. for="id_field_0") regardless of per-widget semantics or configuration.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption: "label should always target the first subwidget" encoded in django/forms/widgets.py:MultiWidget.id_for_label. Evidence: id_for_label returns id_ with '_0' appended (id_ -> id_ + '_0'). The hardcoded literal '_0' represents an index of the first subwidget and is only correct if every MultiWidget label must target index 0. The correct relationship is that id_for_label should not unconditionally append '_0' — the label target should be determined by widget-level semantics (see items 2 and 3).

2) Encoded subwidget id generation: django/forms/widgets.py:MultiWidget.get_context sets subwidget ids using the formula '%s_%s' % (id_, i). Evidence: each rendered subwidget receives an id like 'id_field_0', 'id_field_1', etc. This explains why an author might choose to reference index 0, but it does not justify forcing the label to that index for all MultiWidget uses.

3) Per-widget id/index semantics are implemented elsewhere (correct pattern): django/forms/widgets.py:ChoiceWidget.id_for_label encodes conditional id suffixing using the formula: if id_ and self.add_id_index: id_ = '%s_%s' % (id_, index). Evidence: ChoiceWidget uses add_id_index to decide whether to append '%s_%s' % (id_, index). The incorrect MultiWidget behavior duplicates/overrides this per-widget decision.

4) BoundField uses the widget-provided id_for_label verbatim: django/forms/boundfield.py:BoundField.label_tag calls widget.id_for_label(widget.attrs.get('id') or self.auto_id). Evidence: whatever widget.id_for_label returns becomes the label's for attribute (empty string means omit 'for'). MultiWidget.id_for_label therefore directly controls rendered label HTML and overrides widget-specific expectations.

5) Tests and sibling widgets assume other behavior and will be affected: tests/forms_tests/field_tests/test_multivaluefield.py assert labels like '<label for="id_field1_0">...' and contrib.postgres SplitArrayWidget mirrors the '_0' pattern. Evidence: identified test expectations and a similar implementation in SplitArrayWidget. These tests encode the old, hardcoded assumption and must be updated or verified if id_for_label semantics change.

6) Investigation gap (impact note encoded in analysis): The agent did not run a full test-suite search or accessibility review; there may be additional tests, templates, or accessibility expectations (e.g., whether labels should point to base id, suffixed id, or be omitted) that rely on the current hardcoded behavior.
StepsToReproduce: Create a form field that uses MultiWidget (e.g., a MultiValueField or any field rendered with a MultiWidget) and ensure the field has an auto_id or explicit id (for example, 'id_field').
Render the bound field (e.g., form.as_p(), form['field'], or render the field's label via BoundField.label_tag).
Inspect the rendered label element and observe the for attribute value.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The label for a MultiWidget field should not always point to the first subwidget (id ending with '_0'). The label's for attribute should follow the widget's intended semantics: either reference the base id (e.g., 'id_field'), be omitted where appropriate for accessibility, or include an index only when the specific widget's configuration requires it (for example, widgets that opt into index-suffixed ids).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Labels for MultiWidget fields are rendered with a for attribute that is always suffixed with '_0' (for="id_field_0"), forcing the label to target the first subwidget regardless of widget configuration or intent.

hints_text:
I agree that we should remove for from MultiWidget's <label> but not because "It has not sense" but to improve accessibility when using a screen reader, see also #32338. It should be enough to return an empty string: def id_for_label(self, id_): return ''

problem_statement.Title: forms.URLField.clean raises ValueError("Invalid IPv6 URL") instead of ValidationError
problem_statement.Description: Calling forms.URLField().clean('////]@N.AN') allows urllib.parse.urlsplit to raise ValueError("Invalid IPv6 URL") from inside django.core.validators.URLValidator.__call__, which propagates out of field cleaning instead of being converted to a ValidationError.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption in django/core/validators.py :: URLValidator.__call__: the method assumes urlsplit(value) will not raise ValueError in later validation steps (IPv6 detection and hostname-length checks). This assumption is encoded by calling urlsplit(value) unguarded in multiple places.

2) Unguarded repeated urlsplit calls in URLValidator.__call__: expressions such as re.search(..., urlsplit(value).netloc) and len(urlsplit(value).hostname) > 253 call urlsplit(value) again without try/except protection. These specific unprotected calls allow urllib.parse.urlsplit(ValueError("Invalid IPv6 URL")) to escape as a ValueError instead of a ValidationError.

3) Inconsistent handling between validator and field: django/forms/fields.py :: URLField.to_python (split_url helper) already wraps urlsplit(url) in try/except and converts ValueError into ValidationError, demonstrating the defensive behavior that URLValidator.__call__ fails to replicate. The inconsistency is encoded by leaving URLValidator.__call__ unprotected while URLField.to_python is protected.

4) Reliance on urllib.parse behavior without translation: URLValidator.__call__ relies on urlsplit to parse/validate host/netloc/hostname and therefore must translate urlsplit's ValueError("Invalid IPv6 URL") into a Django ValidationError for the forms/models API. The current code does not perform that translation in all call sites. This affects any user path that invokes URLValidator.__call__ directly (including models.URLField default_validators).

5) Required relationships/formula (as identified by investigation): the validator must obtain a single SplitResult from urlsplit(value) and reuse its attributes (split_result.netloc, split_result.hostname) for subsequent checks; when an IDN/punycode normalization branch reconstructs a normalized url, that normalized value must be used for parsing. Any ValueError raised by urlsplit should be converted to ValidationError(self.message, code=self.code, params={'value': value}). These relationships are documented in the investigation as the correct mapping between urlsplit behavior and validator error reporting.
StepsToReproduce: Open a Django shell or run a Python script with Django available.
Execute: from django import forms
Execute: forms.URLField().clean('////]@N.AN')
Observe the traceback showing ValueError: Invalid IPv6 URL originating from urllib.parse.urlsplit bubbling up through django.core.validators.URLValidator.__call__.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Calling forms.URLField().clean with a malformed URL should raise django.core.exceptions.ValidationError indicating the field value is invalid, not a ValueError. The error should be presented as a form/field validation error (i.e., a ValidationError with the validator's message and code).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: A ValueError("Invalid IPv6 URL") raised by urllib.parse.urlsplit propagates out of django.core.validators.URLValidator.__call__ and causes field.clean to raise ValueError instead of ValidationError; the call stack shows urlsplit as the origin of the uncaught exception.

problem_statement.Title: ExpressionWrapper(~Q(pk__in=[])) produces malformed SELECT (empty left-hand) leading to invalid SQL
problem_statement.Description: Annotating a queryset with ExpressionWrapper(~Q(pk__in=[]), output_field=BooleanField()) generates invalid SQL (an empty expression in the SELECT list) instead of a boolean literal, causing a broken query.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption in django/db/models/expressions.py:ExpressionWrapper.as_sql that compiler.compile(self.expression) always returns a usable SQL fragment for a SELECT clause; it currently returns compiler.compile(self.expression) unchanged. When the inner expression is a WhereNode that signals "matches everything" by returning sql == '', ExpressionWrapper forwards that '' into the SELECT, producing malformed SQL (e.g. "SELECT AS \"foo\"\n").

2) WhereNode sentinel semantics are context-dependent and are encoded in django/db/models/sql/where.py:WhereNode.as_sql: it returns ('', []) for "matches everything", (None, []) for an empty node, and raises EmptyResultSet when the node cannot match anything. These sentinel values are correct for WHERE context but must be interpreted differently for SELECT context.

3) Incorrect assumption in django/db/models/sql/compiler.py:SQLCompiler.get_select that compile(col) will either raise EmptyResultSet or return a non-empty SQL fragment. get_select handles EmptyResultSet by substituting the column's empty_result_set_value or the literal '0' (false), but it does not special-case sql == '' so an empty string passes through and yields an empty LEFT side of the SELECT column ("SELECT AS ...").

4) Triggering behavior originates in django/db/models/lookups.py:In where In.lookup raises EmptyResultSet for an empty RHS (e.g. pk__in=[]). For a plain Q(pk__in=[]), that EmptyResultSet is observed upstream and get_select substitutes '0'. For the negated ~Q(pk__in=[]), WhereNode.as_sql converts the child EmptyResultSet into ('', []) (match-everything), and ExpressionWrapper forwards that '' into the SELECT context unchanged, producing the invalid SQL.

5) Hardcoded literal relationship: SQLCompiler.get_select already uses the hardcoded '0' as the false fallback when compile raises EmptyResultSet. There is no symmetric handling for a WhereNode 'matches everything' sentinel (''), which in boolean SELECT contexts should be mapped to a TRUE literal (e.g. '1') or otherwise handled so the SELECT column is a valid SQL expression. The missing mapping of '' -> TRUE in SELECT contexts is a core part of the failure.
StepsToReproduce: Create a queryset for a model (e.g. Model.objects.all()).
Annotate with a boolean ExpressionWrapper of a negated empty-IN condition, e.g.: queryset.annotate(foo=ExpressionWrapper(~Q(pk__in=[]), output_field=BooleanField())).values('foo').query
Print or execute the generated SQL (e.g. print(queryset.annotate(...).values('foo').query)). Observe the generated SQL.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Annotating with ExpressionWrapper(~Q(pk__in=[]), output_field=BooleanField()) should produce a valid SELECT with a boolean literal (e.g. SELECT 1 AS "foo" FROM "table" or an equivalent DB-specific TRUE literal) for the annotation column, or otherwise produce a valid SQL expression that represents the boolean value.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The generated SQL is malformed with an empty left-hand expression for the SELECT column, for example: SELECT AS "foo" FROM "table", causing an invalid query.

problem_statement.Title: MigrationRecorder ignores db_router.allow_migrate and creates/writes django_migrations on disallowed databases
problem_statement.Description: MigrationRecorder (django/db/migrations/recorder.py) creates the django_migrations table and writes/deletes migration rows on any DB connection it is given, ignoring database router allow_migrate rules. This manifests when running migrations or test database creation in a multi-db setup with a router that disallows migrations on non-default connections.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. ensure_schema assumes the migration table may always be created on the given connection — location: django/db/migrations/recorder.py:MigrationRecorder.ensure_schema. The method calls the schema editor create_model(self.Migration) when the table is missing without consulting the DB router.

2. record_applied assumes migration records may be created on any connection — location: django/db/migrations/recorder.py:MigrationRecorder.record_applied. It calls self.ensure_schema() and then self.migration_qs.create(...) using the connection alias with no router.allow_migrate check.

3. record_unapplied assumes migration records may be deleted on any connection — location: django/db/migrations/recorder.py:MigrationRecorder.record_unapplied. It calls self.ensure_schema() and then migration_qs.filter(...).delete() with no router.allow_migrate check.

4. flush assumes it may delete all migration records on any connection — location: django/db/migrations/recorder.py:MigrationRecorder.flush. It executes self.migration_qs.all().delete() without consulting the router.

5. The Migration inner model hardcodes canonical routing identifiers that must be used when consulting the router — location: django/db/migrations/recorder.py:Migration (Meta). The model defines app_label = 'migrations' and db_table = 'django_migrations'; when deciding whether migrations are allowed on a connection the router should be queried with these identifiers (for example: db_router.allow_migrate(self.connection.alias, 'migrations', model=self.Migration, model_name=self.Migration._meta.model_name)).

6. MigrationExecutor/Loader instantiate and rely on MigrationRecorder — location: django/db/migrations/executor.py:MigrationExecutor.__init__ and django/db/migrations/loader.py. They pass a connection into MigrationRecorder; because the recorder unconditionally creates/writes/deletes migration state, the executor/loader end up operating on connections the router intends to be migration-free.

7. Missing router guard overall: no methods in MigrationRecorder currently call the DB router (db_router.allow_migrate) before creating the table or applying/removing migration rows; applied_migrations() and other read/write helpers also depend on ensure_schema/recorder behavior and are affected.
StepsToReproduce: Configure Django with multiple DATABASES, e.g. 'default' and 'other'.
Run manage.py migrate or run the test suite (which creates test databases) so Django initializes test DBs for each configured connection.
Inspect the non-default database(s) and observe that a django_migrations table exists and migration rows have been inserted.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Django creates the django_migrations table and records migration application only on database connections for which the project's DB router allows migrations (i.e. allow_migrate returns True for app_label 'migrations' / the Migration model). Non-migrated connections must remain free of django_migrations and migration records.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: During test DB creation / migration, MigrationRecorder creates the django_migrations table and inserts/deletes migration records on every connection it is given, including connections for which the project's DB router's allow_migrate would return False (non-default connections).

hints_text:
I don't think you've implemented your router correctly, but I'd need to check the router code to see if it's called multiple times (num_dbs*num_models) to be sure. This is how we implement our routers for allow_migrate: def allow_migrate(self, db, model): if db == 'other': return model._meta.app_label == 'legacy_app' # allow migration for new django models implemented in legacy db elif model._meta.app_label == 'legacy_app': # do not allow migration for legacy on any other db return False return None # this router not responsible So, I'm not sure if there is a bug or not (I'll let someone more familiar answer that), but this is what works for us.
#22583 is somewhat related. It deals with the inability to skip RunSQL/RunPython operations on given database.
@jarshwah: I don't think it is the router. Mainly because the router is not called at this point. Which is what I believe is the bug. @akaariai: I agree that there are similarities. Surely I should be able to manage which connections I actually run migrations against. That seems to be what the db_router is trying to do. I thought that something like the following would solve our problem: from django.db import router . . . def ensure_schema(self): """ Ensures the table exists and has the correct schema. """ # If the table's there, that's fine - we've never changed its schema # in the codebase. if self.Migration._meta.db_table in self.connection.introspection.get_table_list(self.connection.cursor()): return # Make the table # Change below, similar to how allowed_to_migrate in django/db/migrations/operations/base.py works if router.allow_migrate(self.connection, self.Migration): with self.connection.schema_editor() as editor: editor.create_model(self.Migration) But this means that changes to applied_migrations, record_applied, and record_unapplied need to be made, so that it doesn't try to write to a none existent table. For us this is not an urgent issue, as we have appropriate permissions to those connections that are not our core connection. But I do have a concern for any time that we are using a true read-only connection, where we do not have the CREATE TABLE permission. Because this code, as it exists, will blow up in this situation. I tested that with a read-only connection in our db setup, and got an insufficient permissions error. Thanks, Dylan
This is an issue, but bear in mind that migrate must be separately executed for each database alias, so this isn't going to happen unless you specifically run migrate on a database that you know isn't supposed to have migrations on it. I think the best fix would be to have migrations refuse to run entirely and just exit if the migration model is not allowed to be created on the target database.
I see what you mean about the needing to run migarte for each database. I noticed this with the django test runner, where it is trying to run a migration against every connection alias that we have. So that might be an issue with the test runner as much as with the migrations stuff. Thanks, Dylan
Just stumbled on this issue. With a properly written router, I expected the migrations of each app to be executed in the right database by just using : manage.py migrate Could this be the behavior ? Instead it's assuming I'm using the default database, OK, no, but ok :-)... and it doesn't follow the allow_migrate rule and creates in the default database the tables that are supposed to live exclusively in the another one (NOT OK !). So for now the workaround for me is to use a shell script where the app and the database are specified.: ./manage.py migrate inapppurchase --database=iap ./manage.py migrate myapp --database=default
dperetti: Just like syncdb, migrate only runs on one database at a time, so you must execute it individually for each database, as you suggest. This is by design. froomzy: Yes, this is likely a test runner issue, that would be doing this kind of migrate-on-everything. I think the suggested fix of refusing to migrate databases where allow_migrate on the migration model returns False will still work, as long as it errors in a way we can catch in the test runner.
Replying to andrewgodwin: dperetti: Just like syncdb, migrate only runs on one database at a time, so you must execute it individually for each database, as you suggest. This is by design. The question is : is it the best design ? When I run migrate, I don't want to know about how the router is configured. I just want to migrate the app. If the router dispatches the tables of an app to different databases, then I want migrate to operate on those. In other words, I think it would make sense to make migrate database agnostic.
Another side issue : we cannot just manage.py migrate someapp if the someapp is a "django <1.7" app without migration : the old syncdb behavior is not applied when an application is specified. So if want the old apps to sync, I have to just run manage.py migrate, without argument... which will create unwanted tables when we have multiple databases.
Hi guys, Just wondering if there is any chance this will make it into 1.8? Or soon there after? Thanks, Dylan
It's not clear to me what fixing this issue involves exactly. Anyway, it doesn't appear anyone is working on it so there's no timetable for its resolution.
I wanted to chime in here to broaden the scope of the bug, as I was affected by it recently in a different context. The bigger issue is not just that the framework tries to create the migrations table where it's not needed, but that it marks as applied migrations that in fact were not. That puts the database in an inconsistent state, at least as far as migrations are concerned. It's a harmless inconsistency for the specific settings file used at that specific moment in time, but it lays the seed for big problems down the line. For example, if you later decide to change your routing scheme, or (as happened in my case) if you have multiple projects with different settings using the same app on the same database. In terms of a solution, what seems straightforward to my untrained eye is for the migration framework to simply not record migrations as applied that it didn't apply (and as a corollary, it shouldn't try to create the migration table on a database if it doesn't need to record any migrations there). The fix suggested above ("to have migrations refuse to run entirely and just exit if the migration model is not allowed to be created on the target database") doesn't address the broader issue.
Let me amend my last comment: I think that having migrate blow up in this situation would in fact solve the problem with having an inconsistent migrations table, which is the most important thing. My question is, since allow_migrate() operates at the model level and the migrate command operates at the app level, wouldn't this make it impossible to migrate some models of an app but not others?
I can confirm marfire's findings. 1/ For example even with this router applied: class testerouter(object): def allow_migrate(self, db, app_label, model_name=None, **hints): return False And then executing: migrate --database=replica All apps and models migrations get applied to this replica database, while according to the router nothing should happen at all. 2/ From the documentation it is not clear whether it is possible to isolate certain models from certain databases, or whether isolation can only be done on the app-level. From the description in the documentation, it seems possible to use the model_name argument for this, but by the way django stores its migrations, I don't see how that could work.
Just to further confirm: I've been wrestling with this problem for a couple days. I have multiple databases and multiple apps. When I'm running tests, the router allow_migrate works properly to control which migrations can be applied to which database/app. The actual migration portion works fine, but when it comes time to actually write the migration history, it seems to only have a concept of the default database. When it runs ensure_schema, it's expecting that tables should exist on the default DB when they really only exist for certain other apps/databases. This leads to a ProgrammingError where it can't find tables that it shouldn't be checking for in the first place.
Can you please have a look at ​http://stackoverflow.com/questions/40893868/using-redshift-as-an-additional-django-database?noredirect=1#comment69004673_40893868, which provides a decent use case for this bug.
Since 3.1 you can set 'TEST': {'MIGRATE': False} to avoid running migrations on a given db connection, so that solves the test runner issue. Still, even if you do that, apps are still synced (see fix to #32012), Django ends up calling migrate to do the syncing, and this will cause queries in MigrationRecorder.ensure_schema() that might create tables (or fail with permission errors, see #27141). I plan to open a PR to do roughly this from comment:13: it shouldn't try to create the migration table on a database if it doesn't need to record any migrations there

problem_statement.Title: Subquery.as_sql unconditionally strips first/last characters of inner SQL, producing invalid SQL
problem_statement.Description: Subquery.as_sql in django/db/models/expressions.py unconditionally removes the first and last characters of the inner query SQL (using subquery_sql[1:-1]). When the inner Query.as_sql does not return a parenthesized SQL string this slicing drops real SQL characters (e.g. leading 'S' of 'SELECT'), producing malformed SQL.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Incorrect unconditional slicing in Subquery.as_sql (django/db/models/expressions.py :: Subquery.as_sql): the code sets template_params['subquery'] = subquery_sql[1:-1], which assumes the inner SQL is always wrapped in parentheses. That assumption is false when the inner Query.as_sql returns an unparenthesized string, so the slice removes real characters instead of trimming parentheses.

2. Subquery.__init__ fails to establish the Query.subquery invariant (django/db/models/expressions.py :: Subquery.__init__): the constructor assigns self.query = getattr(queryset, 'query', queryset) but does not set query.subquery = True and does not clone the incoming Query. Query.as_sql only parenthesizes when Query.subquery is True (Query.as_sql: if self.subquery: sql = '(%s)' % sql). Because the constructor does not set the subquery flag (and mutates caller state would be possible if it did), the inner as_sql often returns unparenthesized SQL and the Subquery.as_sql slice breaks it.

3. set_source_expressions breaks the same invariant on reassignment (django/db/models/expressions.py :: Subquery.set_source_expressions): when source expressions are replaced the code assigns self.query = exprs[0] without cloning or setting a subquery flag, allowing a non-subquery Query to reach Subquery.as_sql and again trigger the unsafe slice.

4. Subquery.as_sql is not defensive against callers passing unparenthesized queries (django/db/models/expressions.py :: Subquery.as_sql, usages such as Exists.as_sql): other call paths (e.g. Exists.as_sql) may pass Query objects that are not parenthesized. The implementation assumes parenthesized output and thus the relationship template_params['subquery'] = subquery_sql[1:-1] is fragile; the correct relationship is that either the inner SQL must be parenthesized by Query.as_sql (Query.subquery == True) or Subquery.as_sql must not unconditionally remove characters. The documented contract in django/db/models/sql/query.py is: if self.subquery: sql = '(%s)' % sql, so Subquery must rely on that contract or not assume parentheses.

5. Mutating caller-owned Query objects is avoided elsewhere (in Query.resolve_expression / Query.get_aggregation they set clone.subquery = True on clones): Subquery currently does not follow the established cloning pattern, so ensuring the subquery flag must be done without mutating external Query/QuerySet.query objects. The invariant needed is: a Query used as a Subquery must be in the parenthesized form that Query.as_sql produces when Query.subquery == True, and that flag should be set on a clone to avoid side effects.
StepsToReproduce: from django.db import connection
from apps.models import App
from django.db.models.expressions import Subquery
q = Subquery(App.objects.all())
print(str(q.query))
print(q.as_sql(q.query.get_compiler('default'), connection))
q.query.subquery = True
print(q.as_sql(q.query.get_compiler('default'), connection))
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Calling Subquery.as_sql(...) returns a valid, parenthesized SQL fragment for the inner query (user-visible example: ('(SELECT "apps_app"."id", "apps_app"."name" FROM "apps_app")', ())), i.e. the SQL text is complete and not missing leading or trailing characters.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Subquery.as_sql(...) returns malformed SQL with the first and/or last characters removed from the inner query, e.g. ('(ELECT "apps_app"."id", "apps_app"."name" FROM "apps_app)', ()), where the leading 'S' from 'SELECT' and the final '"' are missing. Setting q.query.subquery = True before calling as_sql produces the correct parenthesized result.

problem_statement.Title: Messages: extra_tags empty string lost during serialization ('' -> None on decode)
problem_statement.Description: When a Message with extra_tags == "" is serialized and then deserialized using the built-in storage backends, extra_tags is converted to None. The serializer omits empty-string extra_tags from the serialized payload, and the decoder reconstructs a Message without an extra_tags argument, causing the value to become None.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/contrib/messages/storage/cookie.py:MessageEncoder.default — The encoder builds the serialized list as [message_key, is_safedata, level, message] and uses a truthiness test (if obj.extra_tags) to decide whether to append extra_tags. This treats the empty string ('') as falsy and omits extra_tags from the serialized payload (incorrect assumption: falsy == absent).

2) django/contrib/messages/storage/cookie.py:MessageDecoder.process_messages — The decoder accepts both serialized shapes and reconstructs the Message with Message(*obj[2:]). The two possible serialized forms are: [key, is_safedata, level, message] (no extra_tags) or [key, is_safedata, level, message, extra_tags]. If extra_tags is omitted, Message(*obj[2:]) calls Message.__init__ without the extra_tags argument, so the default extra_tags=None is applied. This explains the observed '' -> None conversion (relationship: missing 5th element => Message.__init__ default extra_tags=None).

3) django/contrib/messages/storage/base.py:Message.__init__ and Message._prepare — The Message API distinguishes None vs ''. Message.__init__ signature uses extra_tags=None and Message._prepare sets self.extra_tags = str(self.extra_tags) if self.extra_tags is not None else None, showing the intended semantics that '' is a valid value distinct from None (evidence the encoder should preserve empty string rather than treating it as absent).

4) django/contrib/messages/storage/session.py and cookie.py — All built-in storage backends use the same MessageEncoder/MessageDecoder, so the encoder's truthiness-based omission affects both session and cookie storage (scope: bug impacts all standard message APIs that default extra_tags to '').

5) Test coverage gap — The investigation did not include a regression/unit test asserting that Message(level, text, extra_tags='') round-trips through the encoder/decoder with decoded.extra_tags == ''.
StepsToReproduce: from django.conf import settings
settings.configure()
from django.contrib.messages.storage.base import Message
from django.contrib.messages.storage.cookie import MessageEncoder, MessageDecoder
original_message = Message(10, 'Here is a message', extra_tags='')
encoded_message = MessageEncoder().encode(original_message)
decoded_message = MessageDecoder().decode(encoded_message)
assert original_message.extra_tags == ''
assert decoded_message.extra_tags is None  # demonstrates the bug
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: After serializing and deserializing a Message whose extra_tags == "", the decoded Message.extra_tags remains the empty string (''), preserving the original value.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: A Message with extra_tags == "" is serialized without an extra_tags entry and, after deserialization, decoded_message.extra_tags is None instead of an empty string.

problem_statement.Title: Dev server autoreloader stops restarting when BASE_DIR is added to TEMPLATES[0]['DIRS']
problem_statement.Description: When BASE_DIR (the project root) is added to TEMPLATES[0]['DIRS'] in settings.py, the development server's autoreloader stops performing restarts on file saves. The problem manifests while running ./manage.py runserver: saving files no longer triggers the dev server to restart until it is manually restarted or BASE_DIR is removed from TEMPLATES DIRS.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption in django/template/autoreload.py::template_changed — it treats any changed file that is located under any template_dir as a template-only change and therefore calls reset loaders and returns a truthy value. Encoded relationship: (template_dir ∈ file_path.parents) => handler returns True (treated as handled).

2) get_template_directories (django/template/autoreload.py::get_template_directories) can legitimately include the project root when a user places BASE_DIR in settings.TEMPLATES[*]['DIRS']. Encoded relationship: entries from backend.engine.dirs (user-provided DIRS) are normalized and returned as template directories; placing BASE_DIR there makes template_dir == project root.

3) Autoreloader semantics in django/utils/autoreload.py::notify_file_changed — any file_changed signal handler returning a truthy value prevents the reloader from calling trigger_reload. Encoded relationship: if any(handler_result) is truthy -> do not trigger reload.

4) Combined effect (1+2+3): when BASE_DIR is in TEMPLATES DIRS, template_changed returns True for changes to all files under the project root (including .py files), and notify_file_changed therefore suppresses restarts for code changes. This is the observed failure mode described in the report.

5) The correct decision criterion for distinguishing template-only changes from code changes is based on the set of watched Python-module files that the reloader already monitors (django/utils/autoreload.py::iter_all_python_module_files). The intended relationship from the investigation is: if resolved_file ∈ iter_all_python_module_files() then the change is a Python-module change and must not be treated as template-only (i.e., template_changed should not return True for such files). This membership test is the authoritative way to detect python-module files versus template files.

6) Verification gap: the investigation did not paste/verify the exact implementation of django/utils/autoreload.py::iter_all_python_module_files(); the fix relies on that function returning a frozenset (or set) of resolved Path objects comparable to file_path.resolve().absolute(). This must be confirmed to ensure the membership test behaves correctly.
StepsToReproduce: pip install -U django
django-admin startproject <name>
Open settings.py, copy the BASE_DIR variable and paste it into the empty TEMPLATES[0]['DIRS'] list
./manage.py runserver
In your IDE, save any project file and observe that the dev server does NOT restart
Remove BASE_DIR from TEMPLATES[0]['DIRS'], restart the dev server, then re-save a settings.py file and observe the dev server restarts again
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: While running the development server (./manage.py runserver), saving a source file (including .py files) or templates should be detected and the server should automatically restart/reload appropriately regardless of whether BASE_DIR is listed in TEMPLATES[*]['DIRS']. Template-only edits may avoid full reloads, but edits to Python modules must trigger the normal autoreloader restart behavior.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: After adding BASE_DIR to TEMPLATES[0]['DIRS'], saving files anywhere under the project root (including .py source files) does not cause the development server to restart. The server appears to treat all changes as template-only and suppresses the normal autoreloader restart until the server is manually restarted or BASE_DIR is removed from TEMPLATES DIRS.

hints_text:
I don't think this is a bug, really. Adding BASE_DIR to the list of template directories causes the entire project directory to be marked as a template directory, and Django does not watch for changes in template directories by design.
I think I encountered this recently while making examples for #33461, though I didn't get fully to the bottom of what was going on. Django does not watch for changes in template directories by design. It does, via the template_changed signal listener, which from my brief poking around when I saw it, is I believe the one which prevented trigger_reload from executing. But that mostly led to my realising I don't know what function is responsible for reloading for python files, rather than template/i18n files, so I moved on. I would tentatively accept this, personally.
Replying to Keryn Knight: Django does not watch for changes in template directories by design. It does, via the template_changed signal listener My bad, I meant that Django does not watch for changes in template directories to reload the server. The template_changed signal listener returns True if the change occurs in a file located in a designated template directory, which causes notify_file_changed to not trigger the reload. AFAIK from browsing the code, for a python file (or actually any file not in a template directory), the template_changed signal listener returns None, which causes notify_file_changed to trigger the reload, right? So could we fix this by checking if the changed file is a python file inside the template_changed signal listener, regardless of whether it is in a template directory? def template_changed(sender, file_path, **kwargs): if file_path.suffix == '.py': return # Now check if the file was a template file This seems to work on a test project, but I have not checked for side effects, although I don't think there should be any.
I would tentatively accept this, personally. 😀 I was thinking I'd tentatively wontfix, as not worth the complication — but let's accept for review and see what the consensus is. Hrushikesh, would you like to prepare a PR based on your suggestion? Thanks!

problem_statement.Title: LazyObject/SimpleLazyObject missing reflected operator methods (e.g. __radd__)
problem_statement.Description: The LazyObject implementation in django/utils/functional.py omits reflected binary/bitwise/unary special methods (notably __radd__), so expressions where a SimpleLazyObject appears on the right-hand side (e.g. other + SimpleLazyObject(...)) can fail when the left operand's __add__ returns NotImplemented.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption encoded at django/utils/functional.py:class LazyObject — LazyObject uses a small, hardcoded set of proxied magic methods and assumes that is sufficient. This class-level list omits binary and reflected operator dunders (e.g. __add__/__radd__, __sub__/__rsub__, etc.), so Python's reflected-dispatch is never provided by the proxy.  
2) Incorrect assumption encoded at django/utils/functional.py:new_method_proxy — operator/reflected special methods must be present as class-level attributes so new_method_proxy can delegate with lazy initialization; LazyObject did not declare the reflected dunders even though new_method_proxy is the intended delegation mechanism.  
3) Hardcoded omission encoded at django/utils/functional.py:class LazyObject — the class-level proxy declarations include __bytes__, __str__, __bool__, __dir__, __eq__, __len__, __getitem__, etc., but not arithmetic/bitwise/reflected methods; this missing set is the root omission affecting all LazyObject subclasses (SimpleLazyObject, LazySettings, DefaultStorage, etc.).  
4) Inconsistency evidenced at django/utils/functional.py:Promise/lazy proxy — the Promise-style proxy already implements __radd__ and corresponding operator methods, demonstrating that proxy semantics in Django expect reflected operators; LazyObject's omission contradicts that precedent.  
5) Required delegation relationships/formulas (evidence of correct delegation semantics): class-level proxies must delegate via new_method_proxy. Examples shown in the codebase and investigation:  
   - __add__ = new_method_proxy(operator.add)  
   - def _radd(wrapped, other): return other + wrapped  
   - __radd__ = new_method_proxy(_radd)  
   Repeat this pattern for other operators (operator.sub/operator.mul/operator.matmul/operator.truediv/operator.floordiv/operator.mod/divmod/operator.pow/operator.lshift/operator.rshift/operator.and_/operator.xor/operator.or) and unary ops using new_method_proxy(operator.neg), new_method_proxy(operator.pos), new_method_proxy(abs), new_method_proxy(operator.invert).  
6) Test-coverage gap encoded at tests/utils_tests/test_lazyobject.py — existing tests for LazyObject do not include binary/reflected operator scenarios, so the omission is not caught by the test suite and is a regression risk.
StepsToReproduce: Create a left-hand operand whose __add__ always returns NotImplemented (e.g. class L: def __add__(self, other): return NotImplemented).
Create a SimpleLazyObject that wraps a concrete value (e.g. an int or string) using the project's SimpleLazyObject API.
Evaluate the expression: left_instance + simple_lazy_instance and observe the operation fails when left.__add__ returns NotImplemented (the proxy's reflected method is not invoked).
For control, evaluate simple_lazy_instance + other to show the wrapped object's __add__ is reachable when the proxy is on the left.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Binary operations where a SimpleLazyObject is the right-hand operand behave identically to having the wrapped object on the right-hand side: when the left operand's __add__ returns NotImplemented, Python should call the wrapped object's reflected method so other + SimpleLazyObject(...) yields the same result as other + wrapped.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Expressions like other + SimpleLazyObject(...) can fail (operation returns NotImplemented or raises an error) because LazyObject does not implement the reflected operator methods (for example __radd__), so the proxy's wrapped object's reflected method is not invoked.

hints_text:
Could you please give some sample code with your use case?
In a boiled-down nutshell: def lazy_consumer(): # something more complex, obviously. return [1, 3, 5] consumer = SimpleLazyObject(lazy_consumer) # inside third party code ... def some_func(param): third_party_code = [...] # then, through parameter passing, my value is provided to be used. # param is at this point, `consumer` third_party_code_plus_mine = third_party_code + param which ultimately yields: TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for +: 'list' and 'SimpleLazyObject'
Seems okay, although I'm not an expert on the SimpleLazyObject class.
Replying to kezabelle: def lazy_consumer(): # something more complex, obviously. return [1, 3, 5] consumer = SimpleLazyObject(lazy_consumer) If you know what is the resulting type or possible resulting types of your expression, I think you better use django.utils.functional.lazy which will provide all the necessary methods.
Replying to kezabelle: As far as I can tell, the implementation can't just be __radd__ = new_method_proxy(operator.radd) because that doesn't exist, which is rubbish. __radd__ = new_method_proxy(operator.attrgetter("__radd__")) also won't work because types may not have that attr, and attrgetter doesn't supress the exception (correctly) Wouldn't the following code work? __add__ = new_method_proxy(operator.add) __radd__ = new_method_proxy(lambda a, b: operator.add(b, a)) I have tested this and it seems to work as excepted.

--- a/django/views/static.py

@@ -129,12 +129,14 @@ def was_modified_since(header=None, mtime=0, size=0):
         if header is None:
             raise ValueError
         matches = re.match(r"^([^;]+)(; length=([0-9]+))?$", header, re.IGNORECASE)
+        if matches is None:
+            raise ValueError
         header_mtime = parse_http_date(matches[1])

         if header_len and int(header_len) != size:
             raise ValueError
         if int(mtime) > header_mtime:

+    except (ValueError, OverflowError):
         return True
     return False

problem_statement.Title: Empty If-Modified-Since header raises exception in static file view
problem_statement.Description: An empty or whitespace-only HTTP_IF_MODIFIED_SINCE header ("" or all-whitespace) now causes an exception when serving static files. Previously such headers were ignored; regression introduced since commit d6aff369ad3.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Incorrect assumption in django/views/static.py:was_modified_since that only header is None means "missing". The function treats non-None headers (including "") as matching the expected pattern and proceeds to parse them. (Location: django/views/static.py:was_modified_since)

2. Unsafe use of regex match results in was_modified_since: code performs matches = re.match(r"^([^;]+)(; length=([0-9]+))?$", header, re.IGNORECASE) and then indexes matches[1] and matches[3] without checking matches is not None, causing a failure when header is "" or otherwise does not match the pattern. (Location: django/views/static.py:was_modified_since; exact relationship: matches = re.match(..., header); then uses matches[1]/matches[3] assumes matches != None.)

3. Direct call to parse_http_date with potentially empty/invalid input leads to exceptions: parse_http_date raises ValueError("%r is not in a valid HTTP date format" % date) when the input does not match RFC date regexes (an empty string does not match). was_modified_since calls the parser in a context that assumes a valid, non-empty header. (Location: django/utils/http.py:parse_http_date and its use by django/views/static.py:was_modified_since)

4. Intended semantics demonstrated elsewhere: django/utils/cache.py treats empty/falsy If-Modified-Since headers as missing using the idiom if_modified_since = if_modified_since and parse_http_date_safe(if_modified_since). This shows the expected relationship: falsy/whitespace-only header -> treat as missing/ignore. The static view does not follow this established pattern. (Location: django/utils/cache.py:get_conditional_response)

5. Test coverage gap: tests/view_tests/tests/test_static.py lacks an explicit test asserting that HTTP_IF_MODIFIED_SINCE == "" is ignored for django.views.static. The absence of this test allowed the regression to slip in. (Location: tests/view_tests/tests/test_static.py)
StepsToReproduce: Send an HTTP request for a static file to Django's static file view (serve) with the HTTP header If-Modified-Since set to an empty string (HTTP_IF_MODIFIED_SINCE: "").
Observe the response handling (server logs/traceback) when the view calls was_modified_since(request.META.get('HTTP_IF_MODIFIED_SINCE'), statobj.st_mtime, statobj.st_size).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: An empty or whitespace-only If-Modified-Since header is ignored (treated the same as a missing header) and the static file is served normally according to modification-time logic.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: An exception is raised when processing an empty If-Modified-Since header, causing the request to fail (traceback originates in was_modified_since handling of the header).

         else:
             from_model_state = from_state.models[app_label, self.model_name_lower]
             old_index = from_model_state.get_index_by_name(self.old_name)
+        # Don't alter when the index name is not changed.
+        if old_index.name == self.new_name:

         new_index = to_model_state.get_index_by_name(self.new_name)

problem_statement.Title: RenameIndex() crashes when re-applying after backward for auto-named (unnamed) index on PostgreSQL
problem_statement.Description: When RenameIndex() is used to rename an auto-generated (unnamed) index, running the migration backwards (which is implemented as a no-op for the unnamed-index case) and then re-applying the forward migration causes a PostgreSQL ProgrammingError: relation "new_pony_test_idx" already exists. The failure occurs during RenameIndex.database_forwards when the code attempts to perform a rename/create even though the target name is already present in the database.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption encoded in django/db/migrations/operations/models.py :: RenameIndex.database_backwards: the method treats reversing an unnamed (old_fields) RenameIndex as a no-op (it returns early), leaving the DB index with the explicit new_name. This behavior means the DB may already contain the target name after a backward migration.

2) Incorrect assumption encoded in django/db/migrations/operations/models.py :: RenameIndex.database_forwards: the method unconditionally calls schema_editor.rename_index(model, old_index, new_index) after resolving old_index by columns and new_index by name, assuming the index discovered by columns will have a different name than the desired new_name. There is no check that old_index.name != new_index.name; when the database already uses new_index.name this leads to a duplicate-creation/rename attempt and the PostgreSQL ProgrammingError.

3) Deterministic auto-name relationship present in django/db/backends/base/schema.py::_create_index_name and django/db/models/indexes.py: Index names are generated deterministically and reproducibly. Specifically, _create_index_name(..., length=8) uses names_digest(table, *columns, length=8); Index.suffix == "idx"; Index.max_name_length == 30. This means the original auto-generated name can be recomputed from table and column names and that the code relies on that deterministic naming elsewhere, but database_backwards chooses to be a no-op instead of restoring/computing the old name.

4) Unverified backend-specific assumption in django/db/backends/postgresql/schema.py::rename_index (gap): the investigation did not verify PostgreSQL-specific rename_index semantics or the DB introspection path used to discover index names. The code path in database_forwards assumes the schema editor will safely handle a rename when the DB already has the target name, but this was not confirmed for the PostgreSQL implementation; the observed crash shows that assumption is false in the tested case.
StepsToReproduce: Define a RenameIndex migration that targets an unnamed (auto-named) index via old_fields and specifies new_name (e.g. "new_pony_test_idx").
Apply the migration (database_forwards) so the DB contains the index named new_pony_test_idx.
Run the migration backwards (database_backwards) — for the old_fields/unnamed-index case database_backwards returns early (no-op), leaving the DB index named new_pony_test_idx.
Re-apply the migration forward (database_forwards). On PostgreSQL this raises: django.db.utils.ProgrammingError: relation "new_pony_test_idx" already exists.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Re-applying the forward RenameIndex after its backward run should not raise an error. If the database already has the target index name, re-applying the migration should be idempotent and complete without error (the index remains named new_pony_test_idx and no duplicate-creation or rename attempt is performed).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Re-applying the forward RenameIndex after a backward run raises a PostgreSQL ProgrammingError: relation "new_pony_test_idx" already exists; the migration crashes when database_forwards calls into the schema editor to rename/create the index.

hints_text:
I understand the issue that arises when one reverses a RenameIndex, but it was made "on purpose" somehow. In https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/27064, For backwards operations with unnamed old indexes, RenameIndex is a noop. From my understanding, when an unnamed index becomes "named", the idea was that it remained "named" even when reversing the operation. I guess the implementation is not entirely correct, since it doesn't allow idempotency of the operation when applying/un-applying it. I'll try to find a fix
Replying to David Wobrock: I understand the issue that arises when one reverses a RenameIndex, but it was made "on purpose" somehow. In https://code.djangoproject.com/ticket/27064, For backwards operations with unnamed old indexes, RenameIndex is a noop. From my understanding, when an unnamed index becomes "named", the idea was that it remained "named" even when reversing the operation. Yes, sorry, I should predict that this is going to cause naming issues. I guess the implementation is not entirely correct, since it doesn't allow idempotency of the operation when applying/un-applying it. I'll try to find a fix We should be able to find the old name with SchemaEditor._create_index_name().

problem_statement.Title: Migration fails when removing unique_together and converting ForeignKey to ManyToMany in one migration — ValueError: Found wrong number (0) of constraints
problem_statement.Description: When a single migration both removes a composed unique_together and changes a ForeignKey field to a ManyToManyField, applying that migration (migrate) raises a ValueError: "Found wrong number (0) of constraints for app_authors(project_data_set, state, start_date)". The error occurs during schema operations while deleting the composed unique constraint.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption in django/db/backends/base/schema.py:BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor._delete_composed_index — the code enforces the numeric check `len(constraint_names) != 1` and treats `len == 0` as an error. This encodes the assumption that exactly one DB constraint must exist to delete. The correct relationship (based on observed behavior) is: if `len(constraint_names) == 0` the deletion should be a no-op; only `len(constraint_names) > 1` should be treated as ambiguous and raise.

2) Incorrect assumption in django/db/migrations/operations/models.py:AlterUniqueTogether.database_forwards — the operation unconditionally asks the schema editor to remove the old composed unique constraint (based on old_model._meta.unique_together), encoding the expectation that the DB constraint still exists when this operation runs.

3) Contributing factor in django/db/migrations/operations/fields.py:RemoveField.database_forwards — RemoveField.database_forwards calls schema_editor.remove_field(...) which drops the column and any DB-level indexes/constraints attached to it. When RemoveField runs earlier in the same migration, the composed unique constraint may already have been removed, leaving zero matching constraints for the later AlterUniqueTogether removal.

4) Generation behavior in django/db/migrations/autodetector.py (generate_removed_constraints / _get_altered_foo_together) — the autodetector can emit both a field removal (RemoveField) and an AlterUniqueTogether in the same migration file, producing a sequence of operations that can attempt to delete the same constraint twice. This encodes the assumption that emitting both operations is safe in one migration.

5) Backend override relationship in django/db/backends/mysql/schema.py:DatabaseSchemaEditor._delete_composed_index — the MySQL override calls super(), so it inherits the base editor's strict numeric check and will also fail when the base method raises on zero matches. The underlying problem is in the base implementation, so backend overrides do not avoid the error.
StepsToReproduce: Define models as in the report: Authors with project_data_set as ForeignKey and unique_together = (('project_data_set', 'state', 'start_date'),), ProjectDataSet join model, etc.

Change Authors.project_data_set from ForeignKey(ProjectDataSet) to ManyToManyField(ProjectDataSet) and remove the unique_together constraint from Authors._meta in the same model change (so a single migration will both remove unique_together and alter the field).
Run: python3 manage.py makemigrations
Run: python3 manage.py migrate
Observe the migration failing with ValueError about 'Found wrong number (0) of constraints for app_authors(project_data_set, state, start_date)'.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The migration should complete successfully when a composed unique_together is removed and a ForeignKey is converted to a ManyToMany in the same migration. If the composed DB constraint has already been dropped by an earlier operation in the same migration, attempting to remove it again should be treated as a no-op (no user-visible error). The final schema should reflect the field change and the unique_together removal.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The migration aborts with an exception during schema alteration: ValueError: Found wrong number (0) of constraints for app_authors(project_data_set, state, start_date). The error originates from BaseDatabaseSchemaEditor._delete_composed_index, which raises when it finds zero matching constraints instead of treating this as a no-op.

Thanks for the report. Tentatively accepting, however I'm not sure if we can sort these operations properly, we should probably alter unique_together first migrations.AlterUniqueTogether( name='authors', unique_together=set(), ), migrations.RemoveField( model_name='authors', name='project_data_set', ), migrations.AddField( model_name='authors', name='project_data_set', field=models.ManyToManyField(to='dict.ProjectDataSet'), ), You should take into account that you'll lose all data because ForeignKey cannot be altered to ManyToManyField.
I agree that you'll loose data but Alter(Index|Unique)Together should always be sorted before RemoveField ​https://github.com/django/django/blob/b502061027b90499f2e20210f944292cecd74d24/django/db/migrations/autodetector.py#L910 ​https://github.com/django/django/blob/b502061027b90499f2e20210f944292cecd74d24/django/db/migrations/autodetector.py#L424-L430 So something's broken here in a few different ways and I suspect it's due to the fact the same field name project_data_set is reused for the many-to-many field. If you start from class Authors(models.Model): project_data_set = models.ForeignKey( ProjectDataSet, on_delete=models.PROTECT ) state = models.IntegerField() start_date = models.DateField() class Meta: unique_together = (('project_data_set', 'state', 'start_date'),) And generate makemigrations for class Authors(models.Model): project_data_set = models.ManyToManyField(ProjectDataSet) state = models.IntegerField() start_date = models.DateField() You'll get two migrations with the following operations # 0002 operations = [ migrations.AddField( model_name='authors', name='project_data_set', field=models.ManyToManyField(to='ticket_31788.ProjectDataSet'), ), migrations.AlterUniqueTogether( name='authors', unique_together=set(), ), migrations.RemoveField( model_name='authors', name='project_data_set', ), ] # 0003 operations = [ migrations.AddField( model_name='authors', name='project_data_set', field=models.ManyToManyField(to='ticket_31788.ProjectDataSet'), ), ] If you change the name of the field to something else like project_data_sets every work as expected operations = [ migrations.AddField( model_name='authors', name='project_data_sets', field=models.ManyToManyField(to='ticket_31788.ProjectDataSet'), ), migrations.AlterUniqueTogether( name='authors', unique_together=set(), ), migrations.RemoveField( model_name='authors', name='project_data_set', ), ] It seems like there's some bad interactions between generate_removed_fields and generate_added_fields when a field with the same name is added.

problem_statement.Title: Triple-quoted BaseCommand.help loses newlines/indentation in manage.py help output
problem_statement.Description: Multi-line (triple-quoted) BaseCommand.help text is shown with collapsed paragraphs and lost indentation when running manage.py help <command>; example blocks and intended newlines are flattened in the help output.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Hardcoded formatter assumption in BaseCommand.create_parser — File: django/core/management/base.py, Method: BaseCommand.create_parser. The parser is constructed with formatter_class=DjangoHelpFormatter which encodes the assumption "all management commands should use DjangoHelpFormatter". Because DjangoHelpFormatter (and argparse.HelpFormatter) collapse paragraph newlines/indentation, multi-line help strings are flattened.

2) DjangoHelpFormatter provides only option-reordering semantics, not raw-description semantics — File: django/core/management/base.py, Class: DjangoHelpFormatter (and its show_last). show_last is a hardcoded set ({"--version", "--verbosity", "--traceback", "--settings", "--pythonpath", "--no-color", "--force-color", "--skip-checks"}) and the class implements reordering behavior but otherwise inherits HelpFormatter paragraph handling that collapses blank lines and indentation. The incorrect assumption encoded here is that the default formatter should both reorder options and be acceptable for preserving raw description formatting.

3) No per-command formatter configuration exists — File: django/core/management/base.py, Class: BaseCommand. There is no formatter_class attribute on BaseCommand to allow commands to select an alternate formatter; the code encodes the assumption that formatter selection is not per-command. The correct relationship discovered is: the parser's formatter_class must be derived from the command (e.g., use self.formatter_class) rather than hardcoded, so commands can opt into formatters that preserve raw description formatting (such as argparse.RawDescriptionHelpFormatter).

4) Behavioral coupling between reordering and description formatting — Files: django/core/management/base.py (DjangoHelpFormatter) and BaseCommand.create_parser. Because reordering (show_last) is implemented in DjangoHelpFormatter, switching a command to a different formatter that preserves raw description (e.g., RawDescriptionHelpFormatter) will lose DjangoHelpFormatter's reordering behavior. The investigation identified this coupling as an implicit assumption encoded in DjangoHelpFormatter.show_last and create_parser's use of that class.
StepsToReproduce: Create a management command class inheriting BaseCommand with a multi-line/triple-quoted help string containing blank lines and indented example usage (as in the original report).
Run: ./manage.py help <your_command> (example: ./manage.py help tzkt_import).
Observe the formatting of the help text and example block in the output.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The help output preserves the multi-line formatting, blank lines, and indentation from BaseCommand.help. Example usage blocks and indented lines appear on their own lines with original indentation, matching the command's help string.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The help output collapses paragraphs and removes intended indentation from the multi-line help string: the example block is flattened into the preceding paragraph (or placed without original indentation), so example usage appears merged into the description instead of on separate indented lines.

hints_text:
This seems no fault of Django but is rather ​the default behavior of ArgumentParser ("By default, ArgumentParser objects line-wrap the description and epilog texts in command-line help messages"). This can be changed by using a custom ​formatter_class, though Django already specifies a custom one (​DjangoHelpFormatter).
It seems reasonable, to make it customizable by passing via kwargs to the ​BaseCommand.create_parser() (as documented): django/core/management/base.py diff --git a/django/core/management/base.py b/django/core/management/base.py index f0e711ac76..52407807d8 100644 a b class BaseCommand: 286286 Create and return the ``ArgumentParser`` which will be used to 287287 parse the arguments to this command. 288288 """ 289 kwargs.setdefault("formatter_class", DjangoHelpFormatter) 289290 parser = CommandParser( 290291 prog="%s %s" % (os.path.basename(prog_name), subcommand), 291292 description=self.help or None, 292 formatter_class=DjangoHelpFormatter, 293293 missing_args_message=getattr(self, "missing_args_message", None), 294294 called_from_command_line=getattr(self, "_called_from_command_line", None), 295295 **kwargs, What do you think?
Looks good but I don't see a reason for keeping a default that swallows newlines because PEP257 forbids having a multiline sentence on the first line anyway: Multi-line docstrings consist of a summary line just like a one-line docstring, followed by a blank line, followed by a more elaborate description. As such, the default formater which purpose is to unwrap the first sentence encourages breaking PEP 257. And users who are naturally complying with PEP257 will have to override the formatter, it should be the other way around.
Also, the not-unwraping formater will also look fine with existing docstrings, it will work for both use cases, while the current one only works for one use case and breaks the other. The default formater should work for both
Replying to James Pic: Also, the not-unwraping formater will also look fine with existing docstrings, it will work for both use cases, while the current one only works for one use case and breaks the other. The default formater should work for both It seems you think that Python's (not Django's) default behavior should be changed according to PEP 257. I'd recommend to start a discussion in Python's bugtracker. As far as I'm aware the proposed solution will allow users to freely change a formatter, which should be enough from the Django point of view.
No, I think that Django's default behavior should match Python's PEP 257, and at the same time, have a default that works in all use cases. I think my report and comments are pretty clear, I fail to understand how you could get my comment completely backward, so, unless you have any specific question about this statement, I'm going to give up on this.
So as part of this issue, do we make changes to allow a user to override the formatter through kwargs and also keep DjangoHelpFormatter as the default?
Replying to Subhankar Hotta: So as part of this issue, do we make changes to allow a user to override the formatter through kwargs and also keep DjangoHelpFormatter as the default? Yes, see comment.

         args = (element_id, mark_safe(json_str))

problem_statement.Title: django.utils.html.json_script hardcodes DjangoJSONEncoder and does not accept custom encoder
problem_statement.Description: The utility django.utils.html.json_script always uses DjangoJSONEncoder when producing JSON for embedding in HTML, and the template filter wrapper does not accept or forward a custom encoder parameter. Callers cannot supply a custom json.JSONEncoder subclass to control serialization.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Hardcoded encoder in util: django/utils/html.py::json_script assumes the only appropriate encoder is DjangoJSONEncoder; it calls json.dumps(value, cls=DjangoJSONEncoder).translate(_json_script_escapes), preventing callers from providing a different cls.

2) Template wrapper does not forward encoder: django/template/defaultfilters.py::json_script is defined as json_script(value, element_id=None) and returns _json_script(value, element_id), so it neither accepts nor forwards an encoder parameter from templates or template code.

3) Escape step requirement retained: django/utils/html.py::_json_script_escapes = { ord(">"): "\\u003E", ord("<"): "\\u003C", ord("&"): "\\u0026" } is applied via .translate(...) to the JSON string; any change must preserve that translate(...) call to keep HTML-embedding safe.

4) Encoder-specific logic context: django/core/serializers/json.py::DjangoJSONEncoder.default contains encoder-specific formatting (e.g., r = o.isoformat(); if o.microsecond: r = r[:23] + r[26:]; if r.endswith("+00:00"): r = r[:-6] + "Z"; time handling r[:12]) — json_script treats DjangoJSONEncoder as the sole encoder choice even though other encoders can be supplied conventionally via json.dumps(..., cls=CustomEncoder).

5) Missing test coverage for custom encoders: tests/utils_tests/test_html.py exercises json_script only with the default encoder and contains no test that passes a custom cls to json_script, so the absence of an encoder parameter is not covered by current tests.
StepsToReproduce: Open a Django project shell (python manage.py shell).
Define a custom encoder: from json import JSONEncoder; class CustomEncoder(JSONEncoder): ... (override default as needed).
Import the utility: from django.utils.html import json_script
Attempt to call json_script with a custom encoder: json_script({'key': value}, 'element-id', cls=CustomEncoder)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Callers can supply a custom json.JSONEncoder subclass via a cls parameter to json_script (and the template filter should accept/forward the same parameter). The returned HTML-embedded JSON should reflect the caller's encoder's serialization decisions while still applying the same HTML-safe escaping mapping (">", "<", "&" escaped to corresponding \uXXXX sequences).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: json_script always uses DjangoJSONEncoder for json.dumps, so callers cannot customize serialization. The template filter wrapper does not accept or forward an encoder parameter. The module still applies the HTML-escaping translate(...) step to the produced JSON.

problem_statement.Title: False-positive templates.E003 when same template tag module is present in TEMPLATES['OPTIONS']['libraries'] and autodiscovered
problem_statement.Description: The template system check check_for_template_tags_with_the_same_name reports templates.E003 when the same full module path for a template tag (e.g. 'someapp.templatetags.my_tags') is present both in TEMPLATES['OPTIONS']['libraries'] and produced by installed-app autodiscovery. The error message lists the identical full path twice, causing a false-positive conflict.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption encoded in django/core/checks/templates.py::check_for_template_tags_with_the_same_name: the code uses libraries = defaultdict(list) and unconditionally appends module_path from two sources (TEMPLATES['OPTIONS']['libraries'] and get_template_tag_modules()), assuming every appended entry is a distinct module. This assumption is wrong because identical full module_path strings from different sources can be the same module, and duplicate strings should not be treated as multiple different modules.

2) Detection logic that triggers E003 is based on list length: the check treats len(items) > 1 as evidence of multiple modules for the same module name, without deduplicating identical module_path strings. The relationship used is: if len(items) > 1 -> report templates.E003 with the items joined in the message; when items contains the same module_path twice, this produces a false-positive message listing identical paths twice.

3) Source of duplicate strings: django/template/backends/django.py (get_template_tag_modules and get_installed_libraries) yields (module_name, full_module_path) tuples via autodiscovery, while user TEMPLATES['OPTIONS']['libraries'] can contain the same full_module_path. Both sources are read independently by the check and appended into the same per-name list, allowing identical full paths to appear multiple times.

4) Ordering constraint from tests: tests/check_framework/test_templates.py requires deterministic ordering of reported module_paths; therefore deduplication must preserve insertion order (i.e. lists must not be replaced by unordered sets). The required relationship is that libraries[module_name] must contain unique module_path strings while preserving insertion order.

5) Locality of the encoding: the incorrect assumption and its effect are encoded in check_for_template_tags_with_the_same_name (django/core/checks/templates.py). The merging behavior in DjangoTemplates.get_templatetag_libraries and get_installed_libraries is relevant but not incorrect by itself; the false-positive arises because the check reads settings.TEMPLATES['OPTIONS']['libraries'] and also consumes autodiscovered module_paths, then unconditionally appends both into libraries[module_name].
StepsToReproduce: Create a template tag module, e.g. someapp/templatetags/my_tags.py, and ensure someapp is in INSTALLED_APPS.
In settings.TEMPLATES, add an OPTIONS entry 'libraries' mapping the short name to the same full module path, e.g. {'my_tags': 'someapp.templatetags.my_tags'}.
Run Django's system checks (e.g. python manage.py check) or run the checks that include check_for_template_tags_with_the_same_name.
Observe the reported error for 'my_tags'.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: No templates.E003 error should be reported when the same full module path for a template tag is present both in TEMPLATES['OPTIONS']['libraries'] and discovered via installed apps. The check should report a conflict only when multiple distinct full module paths exist for the same short module name.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: templates.E003 is reported and its message lists the identical full module path twice (e.g. "'my_tags' is used for multiple template tag modules: 'someapp.templatetags.my_tags', 'someapp.templatetags.my_tags'"), indicating a false-positive duplicate detection.

problem_statement.Title: QuerySet.only() after select_related() crashes on proxy models (ValueError: 'id' is not in list)
problem_statement.Description: When evaluating a QuerySet that uses select_related() to follow a ForeignKey to a proxy model and only() to restrict selected fields, queryset evaluation raises ValueError: 'id' is not in list. The error occurs while Django builds related-populator initialization data (RelatedPopulator) because the concrete model primary-key column is omitted from the SELECT column list.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect use of proxy model _meta during deferred-to-data traversal (django/db/models/sql/query.py: Query.deferred_to_data). The code assigns opts = cur_model._meta while cur_model can be a proxy class; this encodes the incorrect assumption that cur_model._meta always represents DB-mapped (concrete) options. The traversal must use the DB-owning options (concrete model _meta). Evidence/formula: current code uses opts = cur_model._meta; the correct relationship for DB-relevant options is opts = cur_model._meta.concrete_model._meta.

2) Mismatch between must_include initialization and traversal keys (django/db/models/sql/query.py: Query.deferred_to_data). must_include is initialized as must_include = {orig_opts.concrete_model: {orig_opts.pk}} (concrete_model used) but the traversal later uses proxy _meta keys (opts = cur_model._meta), producing inconsistent mapping keys and causing concrete-model columns (notably the PK) to be omitted from the final SELECT list.

3) RelatedPopulator assumes concrete-model attnames are present in the SELECT (django/db/models/query.py: RelatedPopulator.__init__). It builds init_list from klass_info["model"]._meta.concrete_fields and then finds the PK index with self.init_list.index(self.model_cls._meta.pk.attname). If the concrete-model PK attname is missing from init_list because deferred_to_data omitted that column, index() raises ValueError ('id' is not in list). This shows the pipeline expects concrete model attnames to be used when deciding which columns to include.

4) SQLCompiler stores klass_info with the Query model which may be a proxy (django/db/models/sql/compiler.py: SQLCompiler.get_select). klass_info = {"model": self.query.model, "select_fields": select_list} is compatible only if earlier deferred/field-selection code consistently used concrete_model metadata; the bug arises because deferred_to_data used proxy _meta while other code (RelatedPopulator, concrete_fields lookups) uses concrete model fields.
StepsToReproduce: Define models: CustomModel (normal model with name CharField), ProxyCustomModel (class CustomModel with Meta: proxy = True), and AnotherModel with custom = ForeignKey(ProxyCustomModel, on_delete=..., null=True, blank=True).
Start Django shell or run a management command in the same project.
Evaluate the queryset: list(AnotherModel.objects.select_related('custom').only('custom__name').all()).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The queryset evaluates without raising an exception. Returned AnotherModel instances include their related custom object (ProxyCustomModel) with the requested field(s) (custom__name) available; no ValueError is raised during queryset evaluation.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Query evaluation raises ValueError: 'id' is not in list during RelatedPopulator initialization (traceback originates from django/db/models/query.py when computing self.pk_idx), caused by the concrete primary-key column being omitted from the SELECT column list.

problem_statement.Title: inspectdb omits related_name causing reverse-accessor clashes (fields.E304)
problem_statement.Description: When inspectdb generates model code for tables that contain multiple foreign keys to the same target (including self-references), it does not emit explicit related_name arguments on the generated relation fields. This omission leads to duplicate default reverse-accessor names and triggers Django check fields.E304 at runtime.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) inspectdb omits related_name on emitted relation fields — incorrect assumption encoded in django/core/management/commands/inspectdb.py::Command.handle_inspection. Evidence: handle_inspection builds extra_params for each relation (using relations, att_name, extra_params) but never sets "related_name", relying on ORM defaults.

2) The ORM default accessor naming is assumed to be safe by inspectdb — incorrect assumption encoded in django/db/models/fields/reverse_related.py::ForeignObjectRel.get_accessor_name and enforced by django/db/models/fields/related.py::_check_clashes. Evidence: get_accessor_name() returns remote_field.related_name if present, otherwise opts.model_name + '_set'; _check_clashes compares these accessor names and raises fields.E304 when two accessors are equal, with a hint to "Add or change a related_name argument".

3) A related_name needs to be generated when a table references the same target multiple times (or self-references) rather than omitted — relationship/formula identified in django/core/management/commands/inspectdb.py (proposed insertion point). Exact mapping/formula: build
    target_refs = {}
    for col_name, (ref_db_col, ref_db_table) in relations.items():
        target_refs.setdefault(ref_db_table, []).append(col_name)
and when emitting a relational field, if ref_db_table == table_name or len(target_refs.get(ref_db_table, [])) > 1 then set
    extra_params["related_name"] = "%s_%s" % (table2model(table_name).lower(), att_name)
This shows the missing computation (related_name derived from source model and att_name) is required to prevent duplicate accessors.
StepsToReproduce: Create a database schema with a table that has two foreign-key columns to the same target table (or a self-referential table with multiple FK columns).
Run python manage.py inspectdb and capture the generated models (e.g., inspectdb > models.py).
Include the generated models in a Django project and run system checks or makemigrations (python manage.py check or python manage.py makemigrations).
Observe a fields.E304 error reporting that reverse accessors for the two generated relation fields clash and a hint to add or change related_name.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: inspectdb-generated model code should not produce reverse-accessor name collisions in a fresh project. For tables with multiple FKs to the same target (including self-references), the generated models should include distinct reverse-accessor names (so Django checks do not raise fields.E304) in the user-visible generated source.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: inspectdb-generated models omit related_name on relation fields. When those models are used, Django reports fields.E304: Reverse accessor for 'module.Model.field1' clashes with reverse accessor for 'module.Model.field2' and hints to add or change a related_name argument.
