problem_statement.Title: PostgreSQL dbshell: parameters passed after DBNAME are ignored/warned by psql
problem_statement.Description: When using manage.py dbshell with extra psql options (e.g. -c "SQL"), the PostgreSQL DatabaseClient constructs the psql argument list with the database name before the provided parameters; psql treats anything after the DBNAME as positional arguments and warns/ignores them.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/db/backends/postgresql/client.py::DatabaseClient.settings_to_cmd_args_env encodes an incorrect ordering assumption: it builds the final args as ['psql', <connection-options>, DBNAME, *parameters]. This assumes parameters may follow DBNAME, but psql requires options/parameters to precede the DBNAME. The correct relationship is args == ['psql', <connection-options>, *parameters, DBNAME] (i.e., parameters must be placed before the DBNAME).

2) tests/dbshell/test_postgresql.py::test_parameters encodes the same wrong assumption in its assertion (expects ['psql', 'dbname', '--help'] for parameters=['--help']), so the test mirrors the incorrect behavior and would prevent detecting regressions if unchanged.

3) django/core/management/commands/dbshell.py::Command.handle forwards CLI parameters unchanged to connection.client.runshell; therefore the DatabaseClient implementation is solely responsible for producing the psql-correct argument ordering (this forwarding behavior makes the client ordering error directly observable to users).
StepsToReproduce: From a Django project configured to use the PostgreSQL backend, run: ./manage.py dbshell -- -c "select * from some_table;"
Observe psql's output/warnings and whether the provided -c command is executed.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Options and flags provided after the -- in manage.py dbshell (for example -c "SQL") are passed to psql as options (before the database name) so psql executes them and returns their results (or exits) without emitting 'extra command-line argument' warnings.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior:
psql emits warnings that the extra command-line arguments (e.g. "-c" and the SQL string) are ignored, then opens an interactive prompt for the database. Example output observed:
psql: warning: extra command-line argument "-c" ignored
psql: warning: extra command-line argument "select * from some_table;" ignored
psql (10.21)
Type "help" for help.
some_database=>

problem_statement.Title: ManagementForm rendering in FormSet triggers RemovedInDjango50Warning for "django/forms/default.html"
problem_statement.Description: Including a FormSet (or its management_form) causes a RemovedInDjango50Warning about the default form templates even when the management form only produces hidden inputs. The warning is emitted during ManagementForm rendering and appears noisy because the management form's output is only hidden fields.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) RenderableFormMixin.render assumes form/formset rendering should hit the form-level templates and explicitly warns when the selected template equals "django/forms/default.html" or "django/forms/formsets/default.html"; location: django/forms/utils.py:RenderableFormMixin.render. 2) BaseRenderer defines the default template-name values as BaseRenderer.form_template_name = "django/forms/default.html" and BaseRenderer.formset_template_name = "django/forms/formsets/default.html", which are the exact strings that trigger the deprecation check; location: django/forms/renderers.py. 3) ManagementForm is a plain Form subclass with no render override, so it inherits Form rendering and therefore resolves to the renderer's default form_template_name (the deprecated "django/forms/default.html"); location: django/forms/formsets.py:ManagementForm. 4) Formset templates include the management form directly via "{{ formset.management_form }}", which causes the ManagementForm to be rendered (and thus exercise the RenderableFormMixin deprecation check) whenever a FormSet is rendered; location: django/forms/templates/django/forms/formsets/*.html. 5) The ManagementForm's fields (TOTAL_FORMS, INITIAL_FORMS, MIN_NUM_FORMS, MAX_NUM_FORMS) are IntegerField(widget=HiddenInput) so its intended visible output is only hidden inputs; routing it through the generic form-level template (and its deprecation check) is unnecessary and produces the noisy warning; location: django/forms/formsets.py:ManagementForm. 6) The test-suite contains expectations that certain uses of the default templates raise DEFAULT_TEMPLATE_DEPRECATION_MSG (e.g., str(form) and str(formset) in DeprecationTests), so behavior around default-template deprecation is relied upon by tests and must be considered when changing rendering behavior; location: tests/forms_tests/tests/test_forms.py::DeprecationTests and tests/forms_tests/tests/test_formsets.py::DeprecationTests.
StepsToReproduce: In a Django shell or test, define a simple form (e.g., class Choice(forms.Form): choice = forms.CharField()) and create a formset: ChoiceFormSet = formset_factory(Choice).
Instantiate the formset: formset = ChoiceFormSet().
Render the formset (for example, call str(formset) or render a template that uses {{ formset.management_form }} or {{ formset }}).
Observe that a RemovedInDjango50Warning is emitted mentioning "django/forms/default.html" (DEFAULT_TEMPLATE_DEPRECATION_MSG).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Rendering a FormSet that only emits hidden management inputs should not emit the RemovedInDjango50Warning about the default form templates; users should not see a deprecation warning when the visible output contains only hidden inputs produced by the management form.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Rendering a FormSet (or including {{ formset.management_form }} in a template) emits a RemovedInDjango50Warning complaining about "django/forms/default.html" or "django/forms/formsets/default.html", even though the management form's visible output is only hidden inputs.

problem_statement.Title: Combined Enum Flag values serialized as module.EnumClass[None] in migrations
problem_statement.Description: When a Flag/IntFlag value is a combination of members (for example re.UNICODE | re.IGNORECASE) the migrations serializer emits expressions like re.RegexFlag[None] instead of a valid expression that reconstructs the combined flags. This appears in generated migrations (field defaults and RegexValidator flags).
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) EnumSerializer assumes every enum value has a .name and unconditionally serializes using that name. Location: django/db/migrations/serializer.py: EnumSerializer.serialize. This produces strings of the form "%s.%s[%r]" % (module, enum_class.__qualname__, self.value.name) and yields module.EnumClass[None] when .name is None for combined Flag values.

2) EnumSerializer has no decomposition logic or appropriate fallback for Flag/IntFlag combined values. Location: django/db/migrations/serializer.py: EnumSerializer.serialize. The correct relationship when .name is absent for a Flag subclass is to decompose the value into named members and produce an OR expression of those members; if decomposition yields no named members, fall back to repr(self.value).

3) Exact decomposition rule (formula) that must hold to reconstruct the same Flag value (preserve): for each named member of enum_class include the member when either (member.value == 0 and self.value == member) OR (member.value != 0 and (self.value & member) == member). Each included part must be formatted as "%s.%s[%r]" % (module, enum_class.__qualname__, member.name) and the parts joined with " | " to produce an expression that evaluates back to the combined Flag value.

4) RegexSerializer (and other call sites) rely on serializer_factory(flags).serialize() producing a valid Python expression; because serializer.Registry maps enum.Enum -> EnumSerializer in django/db/migrations/serializer.py, the EnumSerializer behavior affects RegexSerializer.serialize and any other serializers that dispatch to it. Location: django/db/migrations/serializer.py: RegexSerializer.serialize and serializer registry.

5) Tests do not cover combined-flag serialization, so the regression was not detected. Location: tests/migrations/test_writer.py (missing test for combined flags such as re.UNICODE | re.IGNORECASE).
StepsToReproduce: Create a model field or validator that uses a combined RegexFlag value, for example: regex_flags = models.IntegerField(default=re.UNICODE | re.IGNORECASE)
Run python manage.py makemigrations
Open the generated migration file and inspect the default/validator flags expression (the migration contains default=re.RegexFlag[None] or flags=re.RegexFlag[None])

Alternative: Create a RegexValidator with flags=re.UNICODE | re.IGNORECASE, serialize it with MigrationWriter.serialize (or observe its serialized form in a migration) and inspect the flags part of the generated string
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Generated migration code should contain a valid Python expression that reconstructs the combined Flag value using named members joined by bitwise OR, for example: re.RegexFlag['UNICODE'] | re.RegexFlag['IGNORECASE']. For single named members, the serializer should keep the single-name form (module.Class['NAME']). If no named members decompose to the value, the serializer should emit a literal representation that round-trips (e.g., repr(value)).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The serializer emits module.EnumClass[None] (for example re.RegexFlag[None]) for combined Flag/IntFlag values. This produces an invalid or meaningless flags expression in the generated migration (e.g., re.compile(..., flags=re.RegexFlag[None])).

problem_statement.Title: Rendering formset.empty_form crashes when form_kwargs contains explicit form keywords (e.g. empty_permitted)
problem_statement.Description: When a formset is constructed with form_kwargs that include keys that BaseFormSet.empty_form also passes explicitly (notably 'empty_permitted'), rendering formset.empty_form in a template raises a duplicate-key keyword error and crashes template rendering.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) Incorrect assumption in django/forms/formsets.py::BaseFormSet.empty_form: the code assumes get_form_kwargs(None) will not contain any of the explicit keyword names it supplies. In practice empty_form calls self.form(auto_id=self.auto_id, prefix=self.add_prefix("__prefix__"), empty_permitted=True, use_required_attribute=False, **self.get_form_kwargs(None), renderer=self.renderer) so if get_form_kwargs(None) contains any of ("auto_id", "prefix", "empty_permitted", "use_required_attribute", "renderer") Python raises a duplicate-key error. 2) The form constructor accepts empty_permitted (django/forms/forms.py::BaseForm.__init__), so users reasonably pass {'empty_permitted': True/False} via form_kwargs; empty_form must not crash when users do this. 3) BaseFormSet._construct_form (django/forms/formsets.py) implements the safe pattern (defaults dict then defaults.update(kwargs) then self.form(**defaults)) which allows user kwargs to override defaults without duplicate-key errors; empty_form deviates from this safe merge pattern and encodes the incorrect assumption in its implementation. 4) The code path in empty_form also risks mutating user-supplied dicts returned by get_form_kwargs(None) unless a copy() is taken; the safe pattern used elsewhere copies/merges instead of expanding the user's dict in-place. 5) Tests (tests/forms_tests/tests/test_formsets.py:test_form_kwargs_empty_form) expect non-conflicting custom kwargs to appear on empty_form and expect empty_form.empty_permitted to be True, so the behavior must both preserve custom kwargs and enforce empty_permitted=True for the empty form.
StepsToReproduce: Define a ModelFormSet factory for a model (e.g. ff = modelformset_factory(MyModel, fields=['a_field'])).
Instantiate the formset with queryset=MyModel.objects.none() and form_kwargs={'empty_permitted': True} (or False): ff(queryset=MyModel.objects.none(), form_kwargs={'empty_permitted': True}).
Render a template that outputs the formset.empty_form (for example include {{ formset }} and {{ formset.empty_form }} in the template).
Load the view/URL that renders that template; template rendering will raise a duplicate-key keyword error and fail.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Rendering a template that includes {{ formset.empty_form }} should not crash. The empty_form should render successfully, should preserve user-supplied form_kwargs that do not conflict with required empty_form arguments, and empty_form.empty_permitted should be True.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Rendering formset.empty_form raises a duplicate-key keyword error (Python) when form_kwargs passed to the formset contain keys that empty_form also supplies (notably 'empty_permitted'), causing template rendering to fail.

problem_statement.Title: IndexError in django.utils.numberformat.format() when given empty string
problem_statement.Description: Calling django.utils.numberformat.format() with an empty-string value causes an IndexError: "string index out of range" because the function indexes the first character of str(number) without checking for emptiness. This surfaces when formatting fields (e.g. in admin list_display) that can pass an empty string to the formatter.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) django/utils/numberformat.py : format() — incorrect assumption that str(number) is non-empty. The code does str_number = str(number) and then unguardedly checks if str_number[0] == '-' to detect a sign; when str(number) == '' this indexing raises IndexError. 2) django/utils/numberformat.py : format() fast-path — incorrect assumption that mark_safe can be called with a non-string. The code returns mark_safe(number) for ints; mark_safe should receive a string (relationship: mark_safe(str(number))). 3) tests/utils_tests/test_numberformat.py : test coverage gap — the test suite contains no explicit case asserting behavior for empty-string input, allowing this regression; a target assertion is nformat('', '.') == ''. 4) django/contrib/admin/utils.py (display_for_field/display_for_value) — callers assume None is guarded and do not account for empty strings reaching formats.number_format; this caller assumption allows '' to propagate to numberformat.format and trigger the IndexError.
StepsToReproduce: Open a Django project shell with the project environment loaded.
Import the formatter: from django.utils import numberformat
Call the formatter with an empty string: numberformat.format(number='')
Observe the IndexError: "string index out of range".

Alternate reproduction: In the Django admin, have a model/display value that evaluates to an empty string for a numeric column and open the list_display page; the admin rendering path that calls formats.number_format may raise the same IndexError.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Formatting an empty-string value should not raise an exception; the formatter should return an empty string (and admin list_display should render the empty_value_display or empty string) instead of crashing.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling numberformat.format() with an empty string raises IndexError: "string index out of range" (trace originates from the unguarded access of str_number[0]).

problem_statement.Title: Password change link on UserChangeForm is broken (404) when UserAdmin is accessed via a related lookup using to_field
problem_statement.Description: When the User change form is reached via another model's admin linked with to_field (e.g. ?_to_field=uuid), the "Password" help-text link is constructed relative to the current URL ("../password/") and resolves to a URL that returns 404.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. django/contrib/auth/forms.py:UserChangeForm.__init__ contains a hardcoded relative path used to build the password help link: password.help_text = password.help_text.format("../password/"). This encodes the incorrect assumption that the current URL's <id> path-segment equals the instance PK.

2. django/contrib/auth/admin.py (UserAdmin.get_urls) registers the password-change endpoint as the URL pattern "<id>/password/", i.e. it expects the user's PK in the path. Linking to /.../<to_field_value>/password/ will fail when <to_field_value> is not the PK.

3. django/contrib/admin/options.py:get_object(request, object_id, from_field=None) implements the lookup selection as: field = (model._meta.pk if from_field is None else model._meta.get_field(from_field)). The admin related-link flow supplies TO_FIELD_VAR ("_to_field") so the change view can be reached via a non-PK identifier, but the password-change URL does not carry TO_FIELD_VAR and will therefore be resolved by PK.

4. Mismatch: the change form's "../password/" link is resolved against the current (possibly non-PK) path-segment, producing /.../<to_field_value>/password/ which does not match the PK-based password-change endpoint and thus returns 404.

5. Relationship that must hold (evidence/formula): when the instance PK is available the password-change path must include that PK (for example, using "../../{self.instance.pk}/password/" as the formatted value) because the admin endpoint is defined as "<id>/password/" and expects the PK. If no instance.pk is available, the current relative link behavior remains the only option.
StepsToReproduce: Have a second model registered in the admin with a relation to User that uses to_field (e.g. ForeignKey(User, to_field='uuid')).
From that model's admin, open the related User via the provided link (the URL will be like /admin/auth/user/22222222-3333-4444-5555-666677778888/change/?_to_field=uuid).
On the User change page, click the "this form" / password link in the Password field help text or inspect its href.
Observe the resulting URL and response (the link resolves to /.../<to_field_value>/password/ and returns 404).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: On the User change page (regardless of whether it was reached via PK or via a related lookup using _to_field), the "Password" help-text link should navigate to the admin password-change form for that specific user and return the password-change page (not a 404).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When the change page is reached via a to_field lookup (URL contains ?_to_field=...), the password help-text link is formatted as a relative "../password/" URL resolved against the non-PK path-segment, producing a path like /.../<to_field_value>/password/ that does not match the PK-based password-change endpoint and results in a 404.

problem_statement.Title: Hidden "initial-" inputs for fields with callable defaults are rendered with bound/submitted value, causing admin inline to be dismissed on second submit
problem_statement.Description: When a ModelForm field's model default is a callable (e.g. ArrayField(default=list)) and show_hidden_initial is enabled, the hidden "initial-..." input is re-rendered with the bound/submitted value on form re-display after a validation error. On the next submit that submitted value is treated as the original initial, so change-detection skips/dismisses the inline and the user's entered value is lost.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. django/db/models/fields/__init__.py::Field.formfield — When a model field has a callable default the field form creation sets defaults["initial"] = self.default and defaults["show_hidden_initial"] = True. This encodes the assumption that forms will persist a stable representation of the original initial value across re-renders.

2. django/forms/boundfield.py::BoundField.as_widget — as_widget unconditionally passes value=self.value() to widget.render even when only_initial=True (rendering the hidden "initial-..." input). For bound forms self.value() is the submitted/posted value, so the hidden initial is rendered using the submitted value rather than the original initial. (The incorrect relationship: widget.render is fed self.value() instead of a widget-formatted representation of self.initial when only_initial=True.)

3. Correct complementary read-side relationship expected by the forms machinery: django/forms/boundfield.py::BoundField._has_changed reads the posted hidden initial via form._widget_data_value(...) and then does initial_value = field.to_python(initial_value) before calling field.has_changed(initial_value, self.data). This encodes the requirement that the hidden input must contain a widget-formatted original initial that field.to_python can parse back into the original python value.

4. django/contrib/postgres/forms/array.py::SimpleArrayField.prepare_value and SimpleArrayField.to_python — prepare_value serializes list -> delimiter-joined string and to_python parses that string back to a list. This establishes that using field.prepare_value(self.initial) when rendering the hidden initial will produce a string that the field.to_python call in _has_changed can parse back. (Explicit relationship: prepare_value(self.initial) -> serialized string; later field.to_python(serialized string) -> original python value.)

5. Investigation omission: django/forms/forms.py::Form._widget_data_value and HiddenInput/MultipleHiddenInput.render implementations were not reviewed. The analysis did not confirm whether form._widget_data_value and the hidden widget render semantics (including empty-value semantics) are fully symmetric with prepare_value/to_python for all widgets; this is a gap that affects confidence that swapping the value passed to widget.render will always round-trip through _has_changed.
StepsToReproduce: Define models and admin as in the report: Thing model and RelatedModel with plop = ArrayField(models.CharField(max_length=42), default=list). Create RelatedModelForm.clean to raise ValidationError('whatever'), register RelatedModelInline (extra=1) on ThingAdmin.
Start Django dev server and open /admin/my_app/thing/add/.
In the inline row's plop field, type any value (e.g. 'test') and submit the form.
Observe the form re-displays with the inline showing the validation error from RelatedModelForm.clean.
Submit the form again without changing the inline.
Observe: no inline error is shown and the inline is dismissed/treated as unchanged; the plop value becomes empty/unfilled.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: After a validation error and form re-render, the hidden "initial-..." inputs should preserve a representation of the original model initial (callable default) so that change-detection correctly determines whether the inline was modified. Subsequent submits should not silently dismiss an inline that previously errored unless the user actually cleared or reverted the inline data.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: On re-render after validation error, the hidden "initial-..." input contains the submitted value (bound value). On the next submit that submitted value is treated as the original initial, change-detection considers the inline unchanged and dismisses it, causing the entered value to be lost.

problem_statement.Title: Sitemap.get_latest_lastmod raises ValueError when lastmod is callable and items() is empty
problem_statement.Description: When a Sitemap subclass exposes lastmod as a callable but its items() returns an empty sequence, Sitemap.get_latest_lastmod calls max(...) on an empty list and raises ValueError; this crashes sitemap index generation.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption: callable lastmod implies a non-empty iterable. Location: django/contrib/sitemaps/__init__.py:class Sitemap.method get_latest_lastmod. Evidence/formula: code calls max([self.lastmod(item) for item in self.items()]) and does not handle the empty-sequence case (ValueError).

2) Incorrect assumption encoded by base class: Sitemap.items() default may be empty. Location: django/contrib/sitemaps/__init__.py:Sitemap.items (default returns []). Evidence: base class allows empty sitemaps, so calling max(...) without handling empty lists is invalid.

3) Incomplete exception handling: get_latest_lastmod only catches TypeError, not ValueError. Location: django/contrib/sitemaps/__init__.py:Sitemap.get_latest_lastmod. Evidence: current code has except TypeError: but a max([]) raises ValueError which is not caught and propagates.

4) Misalignment with view expectations: views.index expects get_latest_lastmod to return a date/datetime or None and already handles None. Location: django/contrib/sitemaps/views.py:function index. Evidence: index checks site_lastmod is not None before using it, so get_latest_lastmod should return None rather than raising.

5) Existing correct behavior in a subclass demonstrates intended semantics: GenericSitemap.get_latest_lastmod returns None for empty querysets. Location: django/contrib/sitemaps/__init__.py:class GenericSitemap.method get_latest_lastmod. Evidence/formula: uses queryset.order_by(...).values_list(...).first() which yields None on empty sets; base-class behavior should match this semantics.

6) Required relationship/formula for safe behavior (from investigation): compute lastmod values, drop None, then take max and handle both TypeError and ValueError. Explicit relationship: values = [self.lastmod(item) for item in self.items()]; non_none_values = [v for v in values if v is not None]; try: return max(non_none_values) except (TypeError, ValueError): return None. This preserves semantics when items() is empty, when per-item lastmod are None, or when lastmod values are incomparable.
StepsToReproduce: Define a Sitemap subclass where lastmod is a callable (e.g. def lastmod(self, item): ...) and items(self) returns an empty list.
Register this sitemap in the project's sitemaps mapping and include the sitemap index URL (e.g. /sitemap.xml).
Send a GET request to the sitemap index (GET /sitemap.xml).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Requesting the sitemap index should not raise an exception. For a sitemap with no items (or with all per-item lastmod values None), get_latest_lastmod should return None and the sitemap index should be generated without error.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: A ValueError is raised: "max() arg is an empty sequence" (traceback shows django/contrib/sitemaps/__init__.py:Sitemap.get_latest_lastmod calling max([self.lastmod(item) for item in self.items()])), causing a 500 error when accessing /sitemap.xml.

problem_statement.Title: FileBasedCache.has_key race condition causes FileNotFoundError under concurrent access
problem_statement.Description: When multiple threads/processes concurrently access an expired file-based cache entry, FileBasedCache.has_key can raise FileNotFoundError. The method checks for file existence and then opens the file; if the file is removed between those operations (for example by _is_expired), open() raises and the exception propagates up through add() and get_or_set(), crashing callers.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Incorrect assumption in django/core/cache/backends/filebased.py:FileBasedCache.has_key: assumes os.path.exists(fname) guarantees the file can be opened immediately. The code does exists(fname) then open(fname, 'rb'), which is racy when another thread/process deletes the file.

2. Contributing side-effect in django/core/cache/backends/filebased.py:FileBasedCache._is_expired: _is_expired reads expiry via pickle.load(f) and, when expired, calls self._delete(f.name) (deleting the file). That delete can occur between has_key's exists() and open(), creating the observed window for FileNotFoundError.

3. Incorrect caller expectation in django/core/cache/backends/filebased.py:FileBasedCache.add and django/core/cache/backends/base.py:BaseCache.get_or_set: callers assume has_key is safe and will not raise FileNotFoundError. add() calls has_key and relies on it not propagating file-missing errors; when has_key raises, the normal add/get_or_set control flow is broken (stack trace shows FileNotFoundError propagating from has_key).

4. Inconsistent error handling compared to django/core/cache/backends/filebased.py:FileBasedCache.get: get already treats a missing cache file as benign by catching FileNotFoundError. has_key does not mirror this safe pattern, leading to inconsistent behavior across the backend.

5. Missing concurrency guard in django/core/cache/backends/filebased.py (vs. locmem): LocMemCache (django/core/cache/backends/locmem.py) uses a threading.Lock (with self._lock) around add/get to avoid check-then-act races. The file-based backend lacks an equivalent concurrency protection, making exists()+open() patterns vulnerable to races. The agent did not exhaustively scan all exists()->open patterns in filebased.py (e.g., touch(), _createdir), so other occurrences may exist.
StepsToReproduce: Configure Django to use the FileBasedCache backend and point its cache directory to a writable location.
Create a cache key whose on-disk cache file is expired (so that _is_expired will delete it when inspected).
From multiple threads or processes simultaneously call cache.get_or_set(key, default) or cache.add(key, value) targeting the same key.
Observe that one or more callers raise FileNotFoundError with a stack trace showing django/core/cache/backends/filebased.py:FileBasedCache.has_key -> open(...) raising.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Concurrent cache access should not cause unhandled FileNotFoundError. When multiple callers race to read an expired or concurrently-removed cache file, the cache API should treat a missing file as a benign cache miss (return default/False as appropriate) rather than propagating an exception to the caller.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Under concurrent access to an expired cache entry, a FileNotFoundError is raised from FileBasedCache.has_key (open on the cache file), propagating through FileBasedCache.add and BaseCache.get_or_set and causing callers to see an unhandled exception.

patch:
diff --git a/django/contrib/auth/management/__init__.py b/django/contrib/auth/management/__init__.py
--- a/django/contrib/auth/management/__init__.py
+++ b/django/contrib/auth/management/__init__.py
@@ -95,11 +95,16 @@ def create_permissions(
         .values_list("content_type", "codename")
     )
 
-    perms = [
-        Permission(codename=codename, name=name, content_type=ct)
-        for ct, (codename, name) in searched_perms
-        if (ct.pk, codename) not in all_perms
-    ]
+    perms = []
+    for ct, (codename, name) in searched_perms:
+        if (ct.pk, codename) not in all_perms:
+            permission = Permission()
+            permission._state.db = using
+            permission.codename = codename
+            permission.name = name
+            permission.content_type = ct
+            perms.append(permission)
+
     Permission.objects.using(using).bulk_create(perms)
     if verbosity >= 2:
         for perm in perms:
test_patch:
diff --git a/tests/auth_tests/test_management.py b/tests/auth_tests/test_management.py
--- a/tests/auth_tests/test_management.py
+++ b/tests/auth_tests/test_management.py
@@ -1485,3 +1485,22 @@ def test_permission_with_proxy_content_type_created(self):
                 codename=codename,
             ).exists()
         )
+
+
+class DefaultDBRouter:
+    """Route all writes to default."""
+
+    def db_for_write(self, model, **hints):
+        return "default"
+
+
+@override_settings(DATABASE_ROUTERS=[DefaultDBRouter()])
+class CreatePermissionsMultipleDatabasesTests(TestCase):
+    databases = {"default", "other"}
+
+    def test_set_permissions_fk_to_using_parameter(self):
+        Permission.objects.using("other").delete()
+        with self.assertNumQueries(6, using="other") as captured_queries:
+            create_permissions(apps.get_app_config("auth"), verbosity=0, using="other")
+        self.assertIn("INSERT INTO", captured_queries[-1]["sql"].upper())
+        self.assertGreater(Permission.objects.using("other").count(), 0)

problem_statement.Title: manage.py migrate --database=<alias> does not bind all post_migrate ORM reads to the specified database
problem_statement.Description: When running manage.py migrate --database=<alias>, post-migrate handlers and some ORM reads executed during migration still invoke the project's DB router and are not bound to the migrate --database alias. In tenant setups that derive the DB from thread-local request state, this causes router lookups to use missing thread-local data and raise errors (InvalidTenantException).
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Incorrect assumption: Passing using=db as a kwarg to emit_post_migrate_signal (django/core/management/sql.py::emit_post_migrate_signal) is sufficient to make all downstream ORM reads use that database alias. Location: django/core/management/sql.py::emit_post_migrate_signal.

2. Incorrect assumption: create_permissions and related post_migrate code paths that call database-bound managers (create_contenttypes(..., using=using), ContentType.objects.db_manager(using).get_for_model(...), Permission.objects.using(using).bulk_create(...)) guarantee no unconstrained ORM reads will occur during the same post_migrate call chain. Evidence shows an unconstrained read still occurs (manager/_db None) during post_migrate. Location: django/contrib/auth/management/__init__.py::create_permissions and related contenttypes/auth code paths.

3. Incorrect assumption and explicit routing behavior: ConnectionRouter._router_func (django/db/utils.py) treats the routing fallback as: if instance._state.db is set use it; otherwise, when all routers return None use DEFAULT_DB_ALIAS. Formula observed: chosen_db = instance._state.db if present else (router_return if router_return is not None else DEFAULT_DB_ALIAS). There is no migration-aware fallback. Location: django/db/utils.py::ConnectionRouter._router_func.

4. Consequence for tenant router: TenantDatabaseRouter assumes it will not be invoked during migrate when --database is specified, and that thread-local tenant state (local_state.subdomain) will be present; the router (utils.tenant_db_router.TenantDatabaseRouter.db_for_read and _default_db) reads local_state.subdomain and raises InvalidTenantException when it is None. Location: utils.tenant_db_router.TenantDatabaseRouter (db_for_read/_default_db) and TenantSubdomainMiddleware for how thread-local state is set.

5. Missing migration context fallback: There is no thread-local 'migration DB' consulted by ConnectionRouter._router_func as a fallback before DEFAULT_DB_ALIAS. The agent's findings propose a migration-context relationship: when routers return None and instance._state.db is not present, consult a temporary thread-local migration DB (e.g. _migration_state.db) before falling back to DEFAULT_DB_ALIAS. This relationship is: chosen_db = instance._state.db if present else (router_return if router_return is not None else (temporary_migration_db if set else DEFAULT_DB_ALIAS)). Location for proposed context: django/db/utils.py (thread-local holder _migration_state.db and helpers _set_temporary_migration_db/_clear_temporary_migration_db) and django/core/management/sql.py (set/clear around post_migrate emission).

6. Unvalidated assumption (investigation gap): The agent's proposed change focused on the read path but did not produce concrete traces or tests showing parity for db_for_write or other router call paths; treating the read-path fix as sufficient for write-paths or all router interactions remains unverified. Location to validate: django/db/utils.py (the shared _router_func used to generate db_for_read and db_for_write).
StepsToReproduce: Install/enable a middleware that sets a thread-local tenant identifier only for HTTP requests (example: TenantSubdomainMiddleware that sets local_state.subdomain).

Implement a DB router that chooses DB based on the thread-local tenant identifier and raises an exception when it's missing (example: utils.tenant_db_router.TenantDatabaseRouter calling _default_db which raises InvalidTenantException when local_state.subdomain is None).
Add the middleware to MIDDLEWARE and the router to DATABASE_ROUTERS. Set TENANT_MAP and relevant DATABASES in settings.
Run a migration with an explicit database alias: python manage.py migrate --database=<alias>.

Observe that during migration a log/print from the router (e.g., "read {}") appears and the router raises InvalidTenantException because thread-local tenant state is not set in the management command context.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When manage.py migrate is invoked with --database=<alias>, all database operations performed as part of that migration (including operations executed by post_migrate handlers and any ORM reads/writes they trigger) use the specified database alias. Handlers and ORM calls should not invoke routers that depend on request-only thread-local state for choosing a database when the migrate command explicitly set the target database.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Post-migrate processing triggers unconstrained ORM reads that call ConnectionRouter and the project's TenantDatabaseRouter (printing "read {}"), which reads missing thread-local request state and raises InvalidTenantException. The migrate --database alias is sent to post_migrate signal handlers but is not sufficient to prevent manager/queryset code with no explicit .using from invoking the DB router and falling back to DEFAULT_DB_ALIAS.

Thanks for this report, it's related with adding missing permissions. I was able to fix this by setting _state.db, however I'm not convinced that it's the best solution: django/contrib/auth/management/__init__.py diff --git a/django/contrib/auth/management/__init__.py b/django/contrib/auth/management/__init__.py index 0b5a982617..27fe0df1d7 100644 a b def create_permissions( 9494 ) 9595 .values_list("content_type", "codename") 9696 ) 97 98 perms = [ 99 Permission(codename=codename, name=name, content_type=ct) 100 for ct, (codename, name) in searched_perms 101 if (ct.pk, codename) not in all_perms 102 ] 97 perms = [] 98 for ct, (codename, name) in searched_perms: 99 if (ct.pk, codename) not in all_perms: 100 permission = Permission() 101 permission._state.db = using 102 permission.codename = codename 103 permission.name = name 104 permission.content_type = ct 105 perms.append(permission) 103106 Permission.objects.using(using).bulk_create(perms) 104107 if verbosity >= 2: 105108 for perm in perms: Partly related to #29843.
This patch resolves the problem at my end. I hope it can be added in the 4.1.4 since #29843 seems to be not actively worked on at the moment.
After diving a bit deeper it turned out that the issue was with one of the libraries in my project which was not adapted for multi-DB. I've made a PR with changes on the django-admin-interface which resolved my issue.
Aryan, this ticket doesn't have submitted PR.
Replying to Mariusz Felisiak: Thanks for this report, it's related with adding missing permissions. I was able to fix this by setting _state.db, however I'm not convinced that it's the best solution: django/contrib/auth/management/__init__.py diff --git a/django/contrib/auth/management/__init__.py b/django/contrib/auth/management/__init__.py index 0b5a982617..27fe0df1d7 100644 a b def create_permissions( 9494 ) 9595 .values_list("content_type", "codename") 9696 ) 97 98 perms = [ 99 Permission(codename=codename, name=name, content_type=ct) 100 for ct, (codename, name) in searched_perms 101 if (ct.pk, codename) not in all_perms 102 ] 97 perms = [] 98 for ct, (codename, name) in searched_perms: 99 if (ct.pk, codename) not in all_perms: 100 permission = Permission() 101 permission._state.db = using 102 permission.codename = codename 103 permission.name = name 104 permission.content_type = ct 105 perms.append(permission) 103106 Permission.objects.using(using).bulk_create(perms) 104107 if verbosity >= 2: 105108 for perm in perms: Partly related to #29843. I think bulk_create already sets the _state.db to the value passed in .using(), right? Or is it in bulk_create that we require _state.db to be set earlier? In which case, we could perhaps change something inside of this method. Replying to Vasanth: After diving a bit deeper it turned out that the issue was with one of the libraries in my project which was not adapted for multi-DB. I've made a PR with changes on the django-admin-interface which resolved my issue. So would it be relevant to close the issue or is the bug really related to Django itself?
Replying to David Wobrock: I think bulk_create already sets the _state.db to the value passed in .using(), right? Yes, but it's a different issue, strictly related with Permission and its content_type. get_content_type() is trying to find a content type using obj._state.db so when we create a Permission() without ._state.db it will first try to find a content type in the default db. So would it be relevant to close the issue or is the bug really related to Django itself? IMO we should fix this for permissions.
Replying to Mariusz Felisiak: Replying to David Wobrock: I think bulk_create already sets the _state.db to the value passed in .using(), right? Yes, but it's a different issue, strictly related with Permission and its content_type. get_content_type() is trying to find a content type using obj._state.db so when we create a Permission() without ._state.db it will first try to find a content type in the default db. Okay, I understand the issue now, thanks for the details!! First thing, it makes me wonder why we require to have a DB attribute set, at a moment where we are not (yet) interacting with the DB. So we are currently checking, when setting the content_type FK, that the router allows this relation. I guess one option is to not do that for not-saved model instances. Would it make sense to defer this to when we start interacting with the DB? But it brings a whole other lot of changes and challenges, like changing a deep behaviour of FKs and multi-tenancy :/ Apart from that, if we don't want to set directly the internal attribute _state.db, I guess we would need a proper way to pass the db/using to the model instantiation. What would be the most Django-y way? Passing it through the model constructor => this has quite a large impact, as a keyword argument would possibly shadow existing field names: Permission(..., db=using). Quite risky in terms of backward compatibility I guess. Adding a method to Model? Something like: Permission(...).using(db), which could perhaps then be re-used in other places also. (EDIT: which wouldn't work, as the setting the FK happens before setting the DB alias.) What do you think ? :) Or am I missing other solutions?
Apart from that, if we don't want to set directly the internal attribute _state.db, I guess we would need a proper way to pass the db/using to the model instantiation. _state is ​documented so using it is not so bad. What would be the most Django-y way? Passing it through the model constructor => this has quite a large impact, as a keyword argument would possibly shadow existing field names: Permission(..., db=using). Quite risky in terms of backward compatibility I guess. Adding a method to Model? Something like: Permission(...).using(db), which could perhaps then be re-used in other places also. What do you think ? :) Or am I missing other solutions? Django doesn't support cross-db relationships and users were always responsible for assigning related objects from the same db. I don't think that we should add more logic to do this. The Permission-content_type issue is really an edge case in managing relations, as for me we don't need a generic solution for it.

problem_statement.Title: Multi-level FilteredRelation with select_related() may cache wrong related object (argument-order mismatch)
problem_statement.Description: Using select_related() together with a multi-level FilteredRelation can cache the wrong related object on model instances; the wrong instance/value order is passed to the field cache setter when populating related objects.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/db/models/sql/compiler.py:SQLCompiler.get_related_selections — defines a helper local_setter with signature local_setter(final_field, obj, from_obj) and stores klass_info['local_setter'] = partial(local_setter, final_field). This encodes the incorrect assumption that the setter accepts (obj, from_obj).

2) django/db/models/query.py:RelatedPopulator.populate — the populate consumer calls klass_info['local_setter'](from_obj, obj). The consumer contract expects the setter to accept arguments in (from_obj, obj) order (i.e. (instance, value) when forwarded to Field.set_cached_value).

3) django/db/models/fields/mixins.py:FieldCacheMixin.set_cached_value — authoritative expected signature is set_cached_value(self, instance, value). Any setter supplied to RelatedPopulator.populate must ultimately call set_cached_value(instance, value).

4) Argument mapping / incorrect relationship observed (concrete): partial(local_setter, final_field) receives (from_obj, obj) and, with the buggy signature, maps to local_setter(final_field, obj=from_obj, from_obj=obj) → final_field.remote_field.set_cached_value(from_obj=obj, obj=from_obj). This swaps instance and value, causing the wrong object to be cached and returned by select_related().

5) Scope note (evidence gap): the investigation identifies this single helper as the producer of the incorrect contract; a full test run and a repo-wide search for other partial(local_setter, ...) usages were not executed and are outstanding checks.
StepsToReproduce: Add the following test to known_related_objects.tests.ExistingRelatedInstancesTests:
def test_wrong_select_related(self):
with self.assertNumQueries(3):
p = list(PoolStyle.objects.annotate(
tournament_pool=FilteredRelation('pool__tournament__pool'),
).select_related('tournament_pool'))
self.assertEqual(p[0].pool.tournament, p[0].tournament_pool.tournament)
Run the test suite (or this test) and observe the assertion failure.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When using select_related() with a multi-level FilteredRelation, the related objects cached on instances must be the correct related instances and types. For the test above, p[0].pool.tournament and p[0].tournament_pool.tournament should refer to the same Tournament instance (equal and of the Tournament type).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The related-object cache is populated with swapped values so that p[0].tournament_pool.tournament is not the Tournament instance expected; the test fails with a mismatch like '<Tournament: Tournament object (1)> != <PoolStyle: PoolStyle object (1)>', indicating the instance/value were swapped when setting the field cache.

problem_statement.Title: Admin 'Save as new' button shown to users without add permission
problem_statement.Description: The admin change-form submit-line shows the "Save as new" button to users who do not have add permission. The UI flag controlling this is computed in submit_row(context) and omits the add-permission check.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption in django/contrib/admin/templatetags/admin_modify.py :: submit_row(context): the code assumes has_change_permission (plus change and save_as and not is_popup) is sufficient to show the "Save as new" button. Current boolean expression: show_save_as_new = not is_popup AND has_change_permission AND change AND save_as. This omits has_add_permission. The correct relationship (the UI flag should reflect the permission required to perform the action) is: show_save_as_new = not is_popup AND has_add_permission AND has_change_permission AND change AND save_as.

2) Context supplier present: django/contrib/admin/options.py :: ModelAdmin.render_change_form provides has_add_permission and save_as in the template context, so submit_row has access to has_add_permission and no additional plumbing is required. The omission is therefore localized to submit_row.

3) Server-side enforcement exists: django/contrib/admin/options.py :: ModelAdmin._changeform_view treats a POST with "_saveasnew" as an add (object_id = None/add = True) and enforces has_add_permission(request), raising PermissionDenied if missing. This creates a mismatch between the UI and server-side enforcement when the UI omits the add-permission check.

4) Test coverage gap: existing admin tests typically use a superuser (has both add and change). There is no explicit test asserting that a user with change but not add does not receive show_save_as_new in the template context; this allows the UI inconsistency to go undetected by tests.
StepsToReproduce: Create or use a ModelAdmin with save_as enabled (ModelAdmin.save_as = True).
Create a staff user who has change permission on the model but does NOT have add permission.
Log in to the admin as that staff user.
Open the change form for an existing instance of the model (not a popup).
Observe whether the "Save as new" button/option is present in the submit-line.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Users who do not have add permission should not see a "Save as new" button or option in the admin change-form submit-line.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: A user who has change permission but lacks add permission sees the "Save as new" button in the submit-line when editing an object (when save_as is enabled and not in a popup).

I rewrite this for my project: "show_save_as_new": not is_popup and has_add_permission # This line that I put!!! and has_change_permission and change and save_as, Do we need to check both? Checking only has_add_permission should be enough.
Replying to Neesham: Yes, because "Save as New" is a save too (current object).
Oh, yes! Sorry and tanks ;-)

problem_statement.Title: Migration optimizer fails to collapse consecutive AlterField operations
problem_statement.Description: When multiple consecutive AlterField operations target the same model.field and there is no preceding AddField in the same optimization pass, the migration optimizer leaves all AlterField operations uncollapsed. The behavior is observed during MigrationOptimizer.optimize and manifests as multiple redundant AlterField operations in the optimized output.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Missing case in django/db/migrations/operations/fields.py:AlterField.reduce — AlterField.reduce does not handle the case where the incoming operation is another AlterField on the same model and field name. This encodes the incorrect assumption that a later AlterField is a boundary that should not replace an earlier AlterField.

2) Because AlterField.reduce lacks that branch, the reduce fallback (FieldOperation.reduce / Operation.reduce) yields no replacement for AlterField-vs-AlterField, leaving multiple AlterField operations intact. This is encoded by the absence of an `if isinstance(operation, AlterField) and self.is_same_field_operation(operation):` branch in AlterField.reduce.

3) The optimizer replacement contract: django/db/migrations/optimizer.py:MigrationOptimizer.optimize (optimize_inner) expects operation.reduce(other, app_label) to return a list to replace the earlier operation. Therefore the relationship needed to collapse earlier operations is: returning [operation] from AlterField.reduce (where `operation` is the later AlterField) will cause the optimizer to replace the earlier AlterField with the later one (i.e., collapse the sequence). This expected return-value relationship is relied on by the optimizer.

4) Symmetry gap relative to AddField.reduce: django/db/migrations/operations/fields.py:AddField.reduce implements the pattern of consuming a following AlterField (Add + Alter -> single Add with final field). The absence of symmetric behavior in AlterField.reduce encodes the incorrect assumption that only AddField needs to consume later AlterField operations.
StepsToReproduce:
Construct the following operations list in a Django migration context:
operations = [
    migrations.AddField(model_name='book', name='title', field=models.CharField(max_length=256, null=True)),
    migrations.AlterField(model_name='book', name='title', field=models.CharField(max_length=128, null=True)),
    migrations.AlterField(model_name='book', name='title', field=models.CharField(max_length=128, null=True, help_text='help')),
    migrations.AlterField(model_name='book', name='title', field=models.CharField(max_length=128, null=True, help_text='help', default=None)),
]

Run the migration optimizer across only the AlterField operations (i.e. without the AddField in the same pass). For example: call MigrationOptimizer().optimize(operations[1:], 'books') and inspect the returned list.
Observe the optimized result returned by the optimizer (the list of operations returned by optimize).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When optimizing a sequence of consecutive AlterField operations on the same model.field, the optimizer should collapse them so that only a single operation representing the final field state remains (i.e., the last AlterField in the sequence). If an AddField and then AlterField(s) occur in the same optimization pass, the optimizer should produce a single AddField reflecting the final field state.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When optimize is run on a sequence containing only the AlterField operations (no preceding AddField in the same pass), the optimizer returns all AlterField operations unchanged. Example: calling optimizer.optimize(operations[1:], 'books') returns a list containing three separate AlterField operations (one per input AlterField) instead of a single final AlterField.

Your analysis is correct Laurent, the reduction of multiple AlterField against the same model is simply not implemented today hence why you're running into this behaviour. Given you're already half way there ​I would encourage you to submit a PR that adds these changes and ​an optimizer regression test to cover them if you'd like to see this issue fixed in future versions of Django.
Thanks Simon, I submitted a PR.

problem_statement.Title: admin.E108 check misses list_display items that runtime rejects, causing 500 on admin list view
problem_statement.Description: A ModelAdmin list_display entry (for example, "choice") can pass the static system check (admin.E108) but cause a 500 error when rendering the admin changelist because runtime label resolution (label_for_field) rejects the same name. The failure is due to divergent field-resolution semantics used by the static check and by runtime code.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect assumption in django/contrib/admin/checks.py -> ModelAdminChecks._check_list_display_item: it treats obj.model._meta.get_field(item) as equivalent to the admin runtime notion of a valid non-GFK/non-reverse field. This is false; get_field(...) can return relation descriptors that runtime code excludes.

2) Divergent authoritative runtime semantics live in django/contrib/admin/utils.py:_get_non_gfk_field and label_for_field. _get_non_gfk_field(opts, name) is the canonical test used at render time: it calls opts.get_field(name) and then intentionally raises FieldDoesNotExist for reverse relations and GenericForeignKey cases, and raises FieldIsAForeignKeyColumnName when a field's attname equals name (FK attname "<fk>_id"). label_for_field depends on this behavior and falls back to attribute/callable checks, raising AttributeError("Unable to lookup '%s' on %s or %s") when _get_non_gfk_field excludes the name.

3) _check_list_display_item uses ad-hoc heuristics (e.g. isinstance(field, models.ManyToManyField), getattr(field, 'rel', None) and field.rel.field.many_to_one) to detect invalid relation types instead of reusing _get_non_gfk_field. These heuristics are fragile and do not match the exact conditions that cause runtime label resolution to reject a name, allowing invalid list_display entries to pass the check.

4) The checks module (django/contrib/admin/checks.py) does not import or call django.contrib.admin.utils._get_non_gfk_field or check for FieldIsAForeignKeyColumnName; the missing imports/usage cause the semantic mismatch between static checking and runtime.

5) The intended relationship that must be preserved by any check logic: obtain raw_field = model._meta.get_field(name) only to confirm existence, then call _get_non_gfk_field(opts, name) and handle its exceptions exactly as runtime does: FieldDoesNotExist => treat as attribute/callable or emit admin.E108; FieldIsAForeignKeyColumnName => treat as invalid FK column name; if raw_field indicates ManyToMany/reverse FK produce admin.E109. The current code does not implement this two-step relationship, causing incorrect diagnostics.

6) Test coverage gap: tests/modeladmin/test_checks.py lacks a regression test reproducing the exact Question/Choice list_display = ["choice"] scenario to assert the expected check (E108/E109) and prevent regressions. This gap allowed the mismatch to exist unnoticed.
StepsToReproduce: Define two models where Choice is related to Question (e.g. Choice has ForeignKey to Question or is otherwise related).
Register QuestionAdmin with list_display = ['choice'].
Start the Django development server and log into the admin.
Visit the admin changelist for Question, e.g. GET /admin/polls/question/.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The admin system check should flag the invalid list_display entry (E108 or E109 as appropriate) before runtime, and visiting the Question changelist should not raise an unhandled 500. The admin changelist should render normally or display a checked/ documented error instead of an internal server error.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Visiting /admin/polls/question/ raises a 500 Internal Server Error. The traceback shows django/contrib/admin/utils.py:label_for_field raising AttributeError: "Unable to lookup 'choice' on Question or QuestionAdmin". The static system check did not emit admin.E108 for this configuration.

diff --git a/django/db/migrations/operations/models.py b/django/db/migrations/operations/models.py
--- a/django/db/migrations/operations/models.py
+++ b/django/db/migrations/operations/models.py
@@ -303,6 +303,71 @@ def reduce(self, operation, app_label):
                         managers=self.managers,
                     ),
                 ]
+        elif (
+            isinstance(operation, IndexOperation)
+            and self.name_lower == operation.model_name_lower
+        ):
+            if isinstance(operation, AddIndex):
+                return [
+                    CreateModel(
+                        self.name,
+                        fields=self.fields,
+                        options={
+                            **self.options,
+                            "indexes": [
+                                *self.options.get("indexes", []),
+                                operation.index,
+                            ],
+                        },
+                        bases=self.bases,
+                        managers=self.managers,
+                    ),
+                ]
+            elif isinstance(operation, RemoveIndex):
+                options_indexes = [
+                    index
+                    for index in self.options.get("indexes", [])
+                    if index.name != operation.name
+                ]
+                return [
+                    CreateModel(
+                        self.name,
+                        fields=self.fields,
+                        options={
+                            **self.options,
+                            "indexes": options_indexes,
+                        },
+                        bases=self.bases,
+                        managers=self.managers,
+                    ),
+                ]
+            elif isinstance(operation, RenameIndex) and operation.old_fields:
+                options_index_together = {
+                    fields
+                    for fields in self.options.get("index_together", [])
+                    if fields != operation.old_fields
+                }
+                if options_index_together:
+                    self.options["index_together"] = options_index_together
+                else:
+                    self.options.pop("index_together", None)
+                return [
+                    CreateModel(
+                        self.name,
+                        fields=self.fields,
+                        options={
+                            **self.options,
+                            "indexes": [
+                                *self.options.get("indexes", []),
+                                models.Index(
+                                    fields=operation.old_fields, name=operation.new_name
+                                ),
+                            ],
+                        },
+                        bases=self.bases,
+                        managers=self.managers,
+                    ),
+                ]
         return super().reduce(operation, app_label)

problem_statement.Title: Squashed migrations retain Meta.index_together, causing RemovedInDjango51Warning
problem_statement.Description: When migrations are squashed, CreateModel.options can retain the deprecated Meta.index_together key (a set/tuple of field-tuples). That leftover key triggers RemovedInDjango51Warning when the squashed migration is imported or project state is built.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) CreateModel.reduce (django/db/migrations/operations/models.py) assumes it is acceptable to copy AlterTogetherOptionOperation.option_name/option_value verbatim into the CreateModel.options dict. This leaves the deprecated "index_together" key (a set/tuple of field tuples) in squashed CreateModel.options. 

2) ModelState.__init__ (django/db/migrations/state.py) encodes the requirement that every entry in options["indexes"] must be a models.Index with a name; it therefore invalidates any conversion that would create nameless Index objects. The codebase does not tolerate unnamed Index entries. 

3) Index naming is constrained (django/db/models/indexes.py): models.Index.suffix == "idx", models.Index.max_name_length == 30, and Index.set_name_with_model builds names using a digest (names_digest(..., length=6)) with the shape "<tablepart>_<colpart>_<hex>_idx". Any index-name generation used during squashing must follow this format and respect max_name_length. Formally: generated_name = "{tablepart}_{colpart}_{names_digest(..., length=6)}_{Index.suffix}" and len(generated_name) must be <= Index.max_name_length. 

4) RenameIndex.state_forwards (django/db/migrations/operations/models.py) demonstrates the canonical conversion pattern: add a models.Index(fields=..., name=...) and remove the index_together tuple via state.remove_model_options(..., "index_together", ...). CreateModel.reduce currently does not follow this established pattern when reducing AlterIndexTogether operations. 

5) Migration squashing pipeline assumption: MigrationOptimizer.optimize calls operation.reduce and therefore relies on CreateModel.reduce to produce migration-ready options. Because CreateModel.reduce currently propagates "index_together" verbatim, the optimizer emits squashed migrations that still contain deprecated options and cause warnings. 

6) Unvalidated helper assumption / gap: a proposed deterministic helper to generate Index.name for squashed migrations (to approximate Index.set_name_with_model without a real model) has not been validated with real model._meta.db_table and field.column values; it is unproven that names produced will always fit models.Index.max_name_length or avoid pathological collisions. This unvalidated assumption is recorded in the migration-reduction path (proposed helper location: django/db/migrations/operations/models.py).
StepsToReproduce: Create a Django model that uses Meta.index_together (e.g. index_together = {("field1", "field2")}).
Make and apply at least two migrations that introduce or alter index_together (so the project has multiple migrations to squash).
Run manage.py squashmigrations <app_label> <migration_name_or_number> to produce a squashed migration.
Inspect the generated squashed migration file: look for CreateModel(... options={...}) and check whether the options dict contains an "index_together" entry.
Import the squashed migration (or run manage.py check/migrate) and observe that a RemovedInDjango51Warning about index_together is emitted.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: After squashing, generated CreateModel.options should not contain the deprecated Meta.index_together key. Index definitions originally expressed via index_together should be represented as entries in options["indexes"] as named models.Index entries. Importing or checking the squashed migration should not emit RemovedInDjango51Warning related to index_together.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Squashing produces CreateModel.options that still include the deprecated "index_together" (a set/tuple of field tuples). Importing or checking the squashed migration emits RemovedInDjango51Warning about Meta.index_together; the squashed migration requires manual editing to remove the deprecation warnings.

--- a/django/template/defaultfilters.py
+++ b/django/template/defaultfilters.py
@@ -586,8 +586,9 @@ def join(value, arg, autoescape=True):
     """Join a list with a string, like Python's ``str.join(list)``."""
     try:
         if autoescape:
-            value = [conditional_escape(v) for v in value]

         return value
     return mark_safe(data)

problem_statement.Title: join filter escapes separator even when {% autoescape off %} is active
problem_statement.Description: The template filter `join` escapes the separator argument (the joining string) even when template autoescaping is disabled. This manifests when rendering a template with `{% autoescape off %}{{ some_list|join:some_var }}{% endautoescape %}`: list items remain unescaped but the separator is escaped.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect unconditional escaping of separator: django/template/defaultfilters.py::join(value, arg, autoescape=True) unconditionally calls conditional_escape(arg).join(value). Encoded assumption: the separator must always be passed through conditional_escape regardless of the autoescape flag. (Evidence: implementation uses data = conditional_escape(arg).join(value) and returns mark_safe(data)).

2) Ignoring provided autoescape flag for the separator: join receives autoescape (via needs_autoescape) and uses it only to escape list items (if autoescape: value = [conditional_escape(v) for v in value]) but not for arg. Encoded assumption: only list items are subject to the autoescape flag. (Location: django/template/defaultfilters.py::join)

3) conditional_escape behavior causes observed escaping: django/utils/html.py::conditional_escape(text) returns text.__html__() if present, otherwise escape(text). Therefore calling conditional_escape(arg) on a non-safe separator produces an escaped separator. (Location: django/utils/html.py::conditional_escape)

4) Established pattern shows intended relationship: other filters (e.g. django/template/defaultfilters.py::unordered_list) select an escaper based on autoescape and apply it uniformly. The relationship implied by that pattern is: escaper = conditional_escape if autoescape else identity; data = escaper(arg).join([escaper(v) for v in value]). The current code violates this pattern by applying the escaper to arg unconditionally.

5) Tests encode the old, inconsistent behavior: tests/template_tests/filter_tests/test_join.py::FunctionTests.test_autoescape_off expects the separator to be escaped even when autoescape=False, and the regression test in tests/template_tests/filter_tests/test_regression.py demonstrates the failing template case. These tests therefore assert the old incorrect semantics and must be updated to reflect consistent autoescape semantics for the separator. (Locations: tests/template_tests/filter_tests/test_join.py and tests/template_tests/filter_tests/test_regression.py)
StepsToReproduce:
Create template 'join01' with content: '{{ some_list|join:some_var }}' and render with context some_list = ['

Hello World!

', 'beta & me', ''] and some_var = '
' — observe escaped output when autoescape is on.

Create template 'join02' with content: '{% autoescape off %}{{ some_list|join:some_var }}{% endautoescape %}' and render with the same context — observe that the separator appears escaped (e.g. '<br/>') even though autoescape is off.

Run the provided unit test from the report (tests/template_tests/filter_tests/test_regression.py) that asserts output == some_var.join(some_list) for the autoescape-off template; it will fail with the current behavior.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When template autoescaping is disabled (e.g. inside `{% autoescape off %}`) the separator argument passed to the `join` filter should not be HTML-escaped. The rendered output should equal the raw join of the items with the unescaped separator (i.e. some_var.join(some_list)).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The `join` filter HTML-escapes the separator argument even when autoescape is off, producing separators like '<br/>' between items while list items themselves remain unescaped under autoescape off.

diff --git a/django/db/models/sql/query.py b/django/db/models/sql/query.py
--- a/django/db/models/sql/query.py
+++ b/django/db/models/sql/query.py
@@ -779,7 +779,13 @@ def _get_only_select_mask(self, opts, mask, select_mask=None):
         # Only include fields mentioned in the mask.
         for field_name, field_mask in mask.items():
             field = opts.get_field(field_name)
-            field_select_mask = select_mask.setdefault(field, {})
+            # Retrieve the actual field associated with reverse relationships
+            # as that's what is expected in the select mask.
+            if field in opts.related_objects:
+                field_key = field.field
+            else:
+                field_key = field
+            field_select_mask = select_mask.setdefault(field_key, {})
             if field_mask:
                 if not field.is_relation:
                     raise FieldError(next(iter(field_mask)))

problem_statement.Title: QuerySet.only() ignores select_related() column restrictions for reverse OneToOne relations
problem_statement.Description: On Django 4.2, calling QuerySet.only() together with select_related() using a reverse OneToOne relation (reverse lookup name, e.g. related_name='secondary') fails to restrict the selected columns of the related model; all related-model fields are still included in the generated SQL.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Incorrect key shape produced by Query._get_only_select_mask: _get_only_select_mask uses opts.get_field(field_name) and stores the returned value (a Reverse-object for reverse-relations) as the select_mask key instead of the forward Field object. Location: django/db/models/sql/query.py::Query._get_only_select_mask.  
2. Compiler lookup expects forward Field keys: SQLCompiler.get_related_selections looks up masks with the forward Field objects (uses select_mask.get(related_field)), so any mask keyed by a Reverse-object is ignored. Location: django/db/models/sql/compiler.py::SQLCompiler.get_related_selections.  
3. Defer-mask implementation shows the correct relationship: Query._get_defer_select_mask maps reverse relation names to reverse_rel.field (the forward Field) or to a (name, relation) tuple for filtered relations, producing select_mask keys that the compiler can match. Location: django/db/models/sql/query.py::Query._get_defer_select_mask. Relationship/formula to preserve: select_mask keys for reverse relations must be reverse_rel.field (forward Field object) or (name, relation) for filtered_relation entries.  
4. Net effect: because _get_only_select_mask stores reverse entries under a Reverse-object key while the compiler looks up using the forward Field object, the only() mask for reverse OneToOne relations is not applied and the compiler falls back to selecting all columns from the related model. Locations: django/db/models/sql/query.py::Query._get_only_select_mask and django/db/models/sql/compiler.py::SQLCompiler.get_related_selections.
StepsToReproduce: Define models: Main and Secondary where Secondary.main = OneToOneField(Main, primary_key=True, related_name='secondary', ...), and each model has several CharField columns.
Run the queryset: Main.objects.select_related('secondary').only('main_field_1', 'secondary__secondary_field_1')
Inspect the generated SQL (e.g. print(str(queryset.query)) or run the query and capture SQL).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The generated SQL should include only the Main fields specified plus only the named field(s) from the related Secondary model (e.g. secondary.secondary_field_1). Columns from Secondary not named in only() should be omitted from the SELECT.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The generated SQL includes all columns from the related Secondary model (secondary_field_1, secondary_field_2, secondary_field_3) despite only() specifying only secondary__secondary_field_1.

problem_statement.Title: bulk_create does not return primary keys when update_conflicts=True (PostgreSQL RETURNING suppressed)
problem_statement.Description: When calling QuerySet.bulk_create(..., update_conflicts=True, ... ) on backends that support RETURNING (PostgreSQL), primary keys are not populated on returned model instances because the QuerySet code suppresses RETURNING whenever any on_conflict handling is present.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) QuerySet._batched_insert encodes the incorrect assumption that any ON CONFLICT disables returning_fields: it uses the conditional "if bulk_return and on_conflict is None:" and therefore never calls _insert with both returning_fields and on_conflict. (File: django/db/models/query.py, class: QuerySet, method: _batched_insert).

2) QuerySet.bulk_create encodes the related incorrect assumption that only the no-conflict case can return rows: it guards the returned-rows count assertion with "if connection.features.can_return_rows_from_bulk_insert and on_conflict is None: assert len(returned_columns) == len(objs_without_pk)". This assumes UPDATE conflicts never return rows and excludes them from the 1:1 returned-rows check. (File: django/db/models/query.py, class: QuerySet, method: bulk_create).

Notes/evidence preserved from investigation: the SQL compiler and Postgres backend already support ON CONFLICT ... DO UPDATE ... RETURNING ... (SQLInsertCompiler and django/db/backends/postgresql/operations.py), and connection.features.can_return_rows_from_bulk_insert is True on Postgres, so the only blocking assumptions are the two conditionals above. The intended relationship for correctness (as found in the investigation) is: when connection.features.can_return_rows_from_bulk_insert is True and on_conflict != OnConflict.IGNORE, the code should accept/expect returned rows, i.e. assert len(returned_columns) == len(objs_without_pk).
StepsToReproduce: Use PostgreSQL backend and define a model with an auto PK.
Create objects: objs = [MyModel(...)] without PKs.
Call MyModel.objects.bulk_create(objs, update_conflicts=True, update_fields=[...], unique_fields=[...]).
Observe returned objects' pk attributes and/or log the generated SQL (verify absence of a RETURNING <id> clause).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When using bulk_create(..., update_conflicts=True, ...) on a backend that supports RETURNING (e.g. PostgreSQL), the operation should return primary keys for the upserted rows and populate the pk on the returned model instances (i.e. returned rows map 1:1 to input rows without PK).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: bulk_create(..., update_conflicts=True, ...) does not populate primary keys on returned model instances and the generated INSERT SQL lacks a RETURNING <id> clause (the code path suppresses returning_fields whenever any on_conflict is present).

problem_statement.Title: Nested-class classmethod serialized with wrong dotted path in migrations (outer class omitted)
problem_statement.Description: A classmethod defined on a nested (inner) class used as a Field.default is serialized into migration files with an incorrect dotted path that omits the outer class name, causing import/runtime errors when applying migrations.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) django/db/migrations/serializer.py::FunctionTypeSerializer.serialize assumes the class owning a bound method is top-level and uses klass.__name__ when building the dotted path. It returns "%s.%s.%s" % (module, klass.__name__, self.value.__name__), which drops outer-class qualification for nested classes.

2) The correct/expected serialization for types (shown by TypeSerializer.serialize) uses __qualname__; TypeSerializer returns "%s.%s" % (module, self.value.__qualname__). FunctionTypeSerializer is inconsistent with this established behavior and therefore produces incorrect module paths for bound methods of nested classes.

3) The practical effect of (1) and (2) is that the serializer produces "appname.models.Capability.default" instead of the fully qualified "appname.models.Profile.Capability.default" in generated migrations (i.e. the relationship should be module + klass.__qualname__ + method_name, not module + klass.__name__ + method_name).

4) Edge-case/gap: classes defined in local scopes have __qualname__ containing "<locals>"; FunctionTypeSerializer currently lacks the local-object handling/checks present in other serializer branches. This untested edge-case may require separate handling but is not addressed by the above findings.
StepsToReproduce: Run python manage.py makemigrations for the app containing the model.
Inspect the generated migration file and observe the default argument in migrations.AddField.
Optionally, run python manage.py migrate and observe the import/runtime error caused by the incorrect dotted path.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The generated migration should contain the fully qualified dotted path to the classmethod including the outer class, for example: default=appname.models.Profile.Capability.default, and migrations should apply without import/runtime errors related to that path.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The generated migration contains an incorrect dotted path that omits the outer class, for example: default=appname.models.Capability.default, which leads to import/runtime errors when applying the migration.
